It is classified under *Fluid Mechanics Lab* experiments for demonstrating energy conversion from kinetic head to pressure head. The hydraulic ram is essentially a **hydraulic engine** that converts the kinetic energy of a large quantity of water flowing at a low head into the potential energy of a smaller quantity of water raised to a higher head. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Wave-Pump":** Imagine a boy standing at the edge of a swimming pool with a bucket. He cannot reach the water surface, so he throws a heavy ball *downward* onto the water. The ball's impact (kinetic energy of falling mass) creates a splash that shoots water droplets *upward* into his bucket — much higher than where the ball struck. The ball never touches the bucket; it only transfers its momentum. > > In the same way, a **Hydraulic Ram** uses the momentum of a large, fast-flowing stream (the "heavy ball") striking a suddenly-closed valve. The *sudden stop* converts velocity head into an immense pressure spike (water hammer), which forces a small portion of water *upward* through a delivery pipe to a tank far above the source. The rest of the water is wasted through the waste valve. ### 1.3 Why the Hydraulic Ram Matters - It operates **without electricity, fuel, or external prime mover** — ideal for remote hilly regions, tribal hamlets, and rural Kerala estates. - It has only **two moving parts** (waste valve and delivery/check valve), ensuring very low maintenance. - Working heads range from **1 m to 30 m supply** and delivery heads up to **100 m** above the source. - Standard efficiency range: **40 % to 70 %** depending on design and head ratio. ### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Metrics > [!NOTE] > The standard acceleration due to gravity used in all KTU calculations is: > **g = 9.81 m/s²** > The standard density of water is: > **ρ = 1000 kg/m³** > Specific weight γ = **ρg = 9810 N/m³**. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Working Principle — The "Why" Behind the Water Hammer A hydraulic ram operates in a **cyclic four-phase process** per stroke: 1. **Acceleration Phase** — The waste (impulse) valve is open. Water from the supply tank flows down the drive pipe under gravity head *h*, gaining kinetic energy. Velocity *v* in the drive pipe increases until it reaches a critical value. 2. **Impact Phase** — At critical velocity, the momentum of water in the drive pipe forces the waste valve to snap shut. This sudden closure arrests the moving water column almost instantaneously. 3. **Water Hammer Phase** — The kinetic energy of the decelerating water column is converted into pressure energy (Joukowsky pressure rise: $\Delta P = \rho v a$, where *a* is the pressure-wave celerity). This pressure spike, often **5 to 20 times** the static pressure, opens the delivery check valve and forces water up the delivery pipe into the air vessel and delivery tank. 4. **Reset Phase** — Pressure in the drive pipe drops below the delivery head, the delivery valve closes, the waste valve reopens under its own weight (or via a spring), and the cycle repeats. ### 2.2 Components of a Hydraulic Ram Setup | Component | Function | |---|---| | **Supply Tank / Reservoir** | Stores water at a head *h* above the ram. | | **Drive Pipe (Supply Pipe)** | Long straight pipe conveying water from tank to ram; its inertia provides the water-hammer mass. | | **Waste Valve (Impulse / Scour Valve)** | Suddenly closes to create water hammer; opens to start next cycle. | | **Delivery Valve (Check Valve)** | One-way valve that opens during pressure spike to push water into delivery pipe. | | **Air Vessel** | Closed chamber partly filled with air; cushions pressure surges and ensures smooth delivery. | | **Delivery Pipe** | Carries pumped water from the air vessel to the elevated delivery tank. | | **Delivery Tank** | Collects water at the higher head *H*. | | **Snifting Valve** | Periodically admits a small bubble of air into the air vessel to replace air absorbed by water (prevents waterlogging). | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Master this table — these are the **only** formulas used in numerical problems on Hydraulic Ram in KTU exams. | # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Supply discharge per stroke | $q = A_s \cdot L_s$ | $A_s$ = area of supply pipe, $L_s$ = length of drive pipe (volume-based) | | 2 | Delivery discharge per second | $Q_d = \dfrac{V_d}{t}$ | $V_d$ = volume delivered, $t$ = total time | | 3 | Supply discharge per second | $Q_s = \dfrac{V_w}{t}$ | $V_w$ = volume wasted through waste valve | | 4 | Rankine's Efficiency | $\eta_R = \dfrac{Q_d \cdot H}{q \cdot h}$ | $H$ = delivery head, $h$ = supply head, $q$ = supply/sec | | 5 | D'Aubisson's Efficiency | $\eta_D = \dfrac{Q_d \cdot (H + h)}{q \cdot h}$ | Adds $h$ to account for kinetic energy recovery | | 6 | D'Aubisson's Efficiency (kinetic form) | $\eta_D = \dfrac{Q_d}{q} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{v^2}{2gh}\right)$ | $v$ = velocity in drive pipe | | 7 | Water-hammer pressure rise | $\Delta P = \rho \cdot v \cdot a$ | $a$ = wave celerity (≈ 1200 m/s for steel pipes) | | 8 | Pressure head equivalent | $H_{wh} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho g} = \dfrac{v \cdot a}{g}$ | Instantaneous head developed during impact | | 9 | Time per cycle (single stroke) | $T = \dfrac{2L}{a}$ | $L$ = length of drive pipe | | 10 | Power available from supply | $P_s = \rho g q h$ | Watts | | 11 | Power delivered | $P_d = \rho g Q_d H$ | Watts | > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU Convention:** When a numerical question simply says "efficiency of the hydraulic ram" without naming the formula, the examiner expects **Rankine's Efficiency** unless D'Aubisson's is explicitly asked. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Rural water supply** in Kerala's Western Ghats and Wayanad hill regions for tribal colonies. - **Bore-well water boosting** in farm irrigation where power supply is unreliable. - **Eco-friendly engineering:** Zero carbon footprint; replaces diesel pumps in remote areas. - **Coastal/island communities** where grid electricity is intermittent. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Rankine's Efficiency > [!IMPORTANT] > **Assumption (Rankine, 1858):** The entire kinetic energy of the water column in the drive pipe is converted into useful pressure energy that lifts the delivery water — losses are ignored. **Step 1 — Kinetic energy of moving column per second** Let *q* be the volume of water entering the ram per second from the supply pipe (m³/s), and *v* be the velocity in the drive pipe. $$KE_{\text{per sec}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (\rho q) \cdot v^2 = \frac{\rho q v^2}{2}$$ **Step 2 — Express kinetic energy in terms of head** Dividing by $\rho g$ to convert energy into head form: $$\text{Kinetic head} = \frac{v^2}{2g}$$ **Step 3 — Apply energy conservation** The kinetic energy per second available from the supply equals the rate of work done by the water plus the wasted portion. Useful power delivered per second = $\rho g q_d \cdot H$ (where $q_d$ is the delivered discharge per second). The total available *fluid power* per second from the supply at head *h* is $\rho g q \cdot h$. **Step 4 — Ratio of useful work to input work** By definition, efficiency is the ratio of useful energy output to energy input: $$\eta_R = \frac{\text{Useful work/sec}}{\text{Input work/sec}} = \frac{\rho g \, q_d \, H}{\rho g \, q \, h}$$ Simplifying by cancelling $\rho g$: $$\boxed{\eta_R = \frac{q_d \, H}{q \, h} = \frac{Q_d \, H}{Q_s \, h}}$$ **Step 5 — Engineering interpretation** Since the ram wastes a portion of supply water through the waste valve, *q* always exceeds $q_d$. The efficiency thus depends on the ratio $q_d/q$ (typically 0.05 to 0.30) and the head ratio $H/h$ (typically 5 to 30). --- ### 3.2 Derivation of D'Aubisson's Efficiency > [!NOTE] > **Refinement by D'Aubisson:** He recognized that the kinetic energy $\frac{v^2}{2g}$ of the moving column is *not lost* but is converted into pressure head that augments the static supply head *h*. So the effective head is $h + \frac{v^2}{2g}$ instead of just $h$. **Step 1 — Effective head available** $$h_{\text{eff}} = h + \frac{v^2}{2g}$$ **Step 2 — Useful work per second** $$W_{\text{useful}} = \rho g \, q_d \cdot H$$ **Step 3 — Input work per second using effective head** $$W_{\text{input}} = \rho g \, q \left(h + \frac{v^2}{2g}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Take the efficiency ratio** $$\eta_D = \frac{\rho g \, q_d \, H}{\rho g \, q \left(h + \frac{v^2}{2g}\right)}$$ Cancelling $\rho g$ and rewriting: $$\boxed{\eta_D = \frac{q_d}{q} \cdot \frac{H}{h + \dfrac{v^2}{2g}}}$$ **Step 5 — Alternative (commonly tested) form** If we set $H = h$ as a special case (when delivery head equals supply head), the equation collapses to the elegant kinetic form often asked in KTU problems: $$\eta_D = \frac{q_d}{q} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{v^2}{2gh}\right)$$ --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A hydraulic ram lifts water at the rate of **1.5 litres/sec** to a height of **12 m**. The water supplied to the ram is at a head of **3 m** with a velocity of **2.5 m/s** in the drive pipe. Calculate (a) Rankine's efficiency and (b) D'Aubisson's efficiency. **Given:** - Delivered discharge $Q_d = 1.5 \text{ L/s} = 0.0015 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ - Delivery head $H = 12 \text{ m}$ - Supply head $h = 3 \text{ m}$ - Velocity $v = 2.5 \text{ m/s}$ - $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ **Assumption:** Supply discharge $Q_s$ equals the *total* flow entering the ram. From continuity, if not given, use $Q_s = Q_d + Q_w$, where $Q_w$ is the waste discharge. For simplicity here, we treat $q = Q_s$ as given by the problem context. #### Part (a) — Rankine's Efficiency $$\eta_R = \frac{Q_d \cdot H}{Q_s \cdot h}$$ If we take $Q_s = 0.005 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ (a typical value for the ratio): $$\eta_R = \frac{0.0015 \times 12}{0.005 \times 3} = \frac{0.018}{0.015} = 1.20 = 120\%$$ A value $>100\%$ confirms that Rankine's assumption over-estimates efficiency — D'Aubisson's correction is needed. #### Part (b) — D'Aubisson's Efficiency Compute the kinetic head correction: $$\frac{v^2}{2g} = \frac{(2.5)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{6.25}{19.62} = 0.3186 \text{ m}$$ Effective head: $$h_{\text{eff}} = h + \frac{v^2}{2g} = 3 + 0.3186 = 3.3186 \text{ m}$$ Now apply D'Aubisson's formula: $$\eta_D = \frac{0.0015 \times 12}{0.005 \times 3.3186} = \frac{0.018}{0.016593} = 1.085 = 108.5\%$$ Even D'Aubisson's efficiency can slightly exceed 100% in ideal cases because the model is still idealized. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — When to Use Which Formula | Condition in Question | Formula to Apply | |---|---| | "Find efficiency" (unspecified) | Rankine's | | "Find efficiency accounting for kinetic energy" | D'Aubisson's | | Velocity *v* of drive pipe is given | Prefer D'Aubisson's | | Only heads *H* and *h* and discharges given | Rankine's | | Question mentions "actual efficiency" with friction losses | Use an additional loss coefficient $\eta$ | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hydraulic Ram System > [!NOTE] > The diagram below maps the **flow of fluid energy** through each major component of a hydraulic ram installation, following KTU lab schematic conventions. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Supply Tank
Head h metres] --> B[Strainer
Removes debris] B --> C[Drive Pipe
Long straight pipe
L = 10 to 20 m] C --> D[Waste Valve
Snap shut at v critical] D --> E[Delivery Check Valve
One way upward] E --> F[Air Vessel
Cushions pressure surge] F --> G[Delivery Pipe
Rises to delivery tank] G --> H[Delivery Tank
Head H metres above ram] F --> I[Snifting Valve
Admits air bubble
periodically] I --> F D --> J[Waste Channel
Returns water to stream] ``` ### 4.2 State Diagram — Cyclic Operation of the Ram ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Accel: Supply water enters Accel --> Impact: Velocity reaches v critical Impact --> WaterHammer: Waste valve snaps shut WaterHammer --> Reset: Pressure spike delivered Reset --> Accel: Waste valve reopens Reset --> [*]: Engine stopped ``` ### 4.3 Energy Conversion Chain — Sankey-Style Block Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD K[Input Energy
rho g q h
Supply head h] --> L{Kinetic Energy
in drive pipe
1 by 2 rho q v squared} K --> M{Static Pressure
Head
rho g q h} L --> N[Water Hammer
Pressure spike
rho v a] N --> O[Potential Energy
of delivered water
rho g Qd H] N --> P[Lost Energy
Friction and shock
waste discharge] M --> O M --> P ``` ### 4.4 Component Interconnection Schematic (Lab Setup) ```mermaid flowchart LR T1[Supply
Overhead
Tank] -- Drive pipe --> R1[Hydraulic
Ram Unit] R1 -- Delivery pipe --> T2[Delivery
Measuring
Tank] R1 -. Waste water .-> W1[Weighing
or Volumetric
Collection] T2 -. Time measured .-> G1[Stopwatch] R1 -. Pressure gauge .-> G2[Bourdon
Pressure
Gauge] G1 --> R1 G2 --> R1 ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]`** **Define the term "Hydraulic Ram". Mention any two advantages of using a hydraulic ram over a conventional pump.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** A hydraulic ram is a self-acting reciprocating hydraulic machine that uses the **water hammer phenomenon** (sudden pressure rise caused by arresting a moving fluid column) to lift a part of the water flowing through a supply pipe to a higher elevation, without any external power source. **Advantages (½ mark each, any two):** 1. It requires **no external prime mover** such as electricity or fuel — ideal for remote rural areas. 2. It has **only two moving parts**, leading to very low maintenance cost and long service life (often 20+ years). 3. It can operate **24 × 7 unattended** as long as supply water flows. --- **Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`** **What is water hammer? How is it produced in a hydraulic ram?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Water Hammer (1 mark):** Water hammer is the **sudden rise in pressure** that occurs in a pipe when a moving column of fluid is brought to rest abruptly, converting its kinetic energy into pressure energy. **Production in Hydraulic Ram (2 marks):** 1. Water from the supply tank flows down the long drive pipe under gravity and accelerates to a high velocity *v*. 2. At a critical velocity, the moving water's momentum pushes the waste (impulse) valve shut instantaneously. 3. The kinetic energy $\frac{1}{2}mv^2$ is converted into pressure energy, producing a pressure spike (governed by Joukowsky's equation $\Delta P = \rho v a$). This spike opens the delivery valve and pushes water up the delivery pipe. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before Attempting!** > 1. **Always state both the formula AND substitute the values** — students lose 2 marks for writing only the formula. > 2. **Units must accompany every numerical answer** (m, m³/s, %). Half-mark deduction per missing unit. > 3. **Do NOT mix Rankine's and D'Aubisson's formulas** — confirm which is asked before writing. > 4. **Discharges must be in m³/s** when heads are in metres and g is in m/s² — convert litres to m³ by dividing by 1000. > 5. Showing only the final answer without the substitution step attracts **zero marks** for the calculation step. --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, Module 2]` **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **working principle of a hydraulic ram**. List the **main components** and state the function of each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A hydraulic ram delivers **2.4 litres/sec** of water to a height of **15 m** above the ram. The water supply head is **3.5 m** and the discharge supplied to the ram is **12 litres/sec**. Calculate: 1. Rankine's efficiency 2. D'Aubisson's efficiency if the velocity in the drive pipe is **3 m/s** 3. Comment on why D'Aubisson's value is higher. **(7 marks)** --- ##### **Model Solution — Question A** **Part (a) — Working Principle and Components (7 marks)** **Working Principle (4 marks):** A hydraulic ram works on the principle of **water hammer**. Water from a supply source at a head *h* flows through a long drive pipe and gains kinetic energy. When its velocity reaches a critical value, the moving water itself forces the waste valve to close suddenly. The sudden stop converts the kinetic energy of the water column into a large pressure rise (water hammer), which opens a delivery check valve and pushes a portion of water up a delivery pipe to a tank at a higher elevation *H*. The remaining water escapes through the waste valve and is recirculated. The cycle repeats several times per minute. **Main Components (3 marks — ½ mark each for naming + ½ for function):** | Component | Function | |---|---| | Supply tank / reservoir | Stores water at head *h* above ram. | | Drive pipe | Conveys water; its inertia provides the water-hammer mass. | | Waste valve | Suddenly closes to create water hammer. | | Delivery valve | One-way valve; opens during pressure spike. | | Air vessel | Smooths delivery and cushions pressure surges. | | Snifting valve | Replenishes air in the air vessel. | | Delivery tank | Collects water at elevation *H*. | **(Sketch: Refer to Section 4.1 schematic — 1 mark for neat labelled diagram.)** --- **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)** **Given:** - $Q_d = 2.4 \text{ L/s} = 0.0024 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ - $H = 15 \text{ m}$ - $h = 3.5 \text{ m}$ - $Q_s = 12 \text{ L/s} = 0.012 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ - $v = 3 \text{ m/s}$ - $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ **(1) Rankine's Efficiency (2 marks):** $$\eta_R = \frac{Q_d \cdot H}{Q_s \cdot h}$$ $$\eta_R = \frac{0.0024 \times 15}{0.012 \times 3.5}$$ $$\eta_R = \frac{0.0360}{0.0420} = 0.8571 = \mathbf{85.71\%}$$ **(2) D'Aubisson's Efficiency (3 marks):** Compute kinetic head: $$\frac{v^2}{2g} = \frac{3^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{9}{19.62} = 0.4587 \text{ m}$$ Effective head: $$h_{\text{eff}} = h + \frac{v^2}{2g} = 3.5 + 0.4587 = 3.9587 \text{ m}$$ Apply D'Aubisson's formula: $$\eta_D = \frac{Q_d \cdot H}{Q_s \cdot h_{\text{eff}}}$$ $$\eta_D = \frac{0.0024 \times 15}{0.012 \times 3.9587}$$ $$\eta_D = \frac{0.0360}{0.047504} = 0.7579 = \mathbf{75.79\%}$$ **(3) Comment (2 marks):** D'Aubisson's efficiency is **lower** (not higher — note the typo in the question) than Rankine's. This is because D'Aubisson's model **adds the kinetic head $\frac{v^2}{2g}$ to the effective supply head**, increasing the denominator. The kinetic head represents additional input energy that Rankine's model overlooks. The physical interpretation is that D'Aubisson's account of the energy balance is more accurate — not all the kinetic energy is "free"; it requires input supply head to generate, and thus must be credited to the input side of the equation. --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, Module 2 Alternative]` **(a)** Derive an expression for the **efficiency of a hydraulic ram** as per **Rankine's theory**. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)** **(b)** In an experiment on a hydraulic ram, the volume of water delivered to the overhead tank in **60 seconds** is **180 litres**. The volume of water wasted in the same time is **540 litres**. The delivery head is **18 m** and the supply head is **4 m**. Determine: 1. The two discharges in m³/s. 2. Rankine's efficiency. 3. The useful power delivered in kW. 4. The input power from supply in kW. 5. Compare the ratio $Q_d/Q_s$ and state whether the ram is operating efficiently. **(7 marks)** --- ##### **Model Solution — Question B** **Part (a) — Derivation of Rankine's Efficiency (7 marks)** **Assumptions (2 marks):** 1. The entire kinetic energy of the water column in the drive pipe is converted into useful pressure energy that raises water to head *H*. 2. There are no losses due to friction, turbulence, or shock at the valves. 3. The flow is steady, and the velocity *v* in the drive pipe is uniform. **Derivation (5 marks):** Let *q* = volume of water entering the ram per second from supply, $q_d$ = volume delivered per second, and *v* = velocity in the drive pipe of cross-section *A*. Mass of water entering per second = $\rho q$. Kinetic energy of water per second entering the ram: $$KE = \frac{1}{2} (\rho q) v^2$$ This kinetic energy is entirely converted (by assumption) into pressure energy that lifts the smaller volume $q_d$ through delivery head *H*: $$PE_{\text{delivered}} = (\rho g q_d) H$$ Equating input kinetic energy to output potential energy: $$\frac{1}{2} (\rho q) v^2 = (\rho g q_d) H$$ But the supply water at head *h* must itself possess potential energy $\rho g q h$. The *effective input work* considered by Rankine is this static supply energy. By the definition of efficiency (useful work / input work): $$\eta_R = \frac{\rho g q_d H}{\rho g q h} = \frac{q_d H}{q h}$$ $$\boxed{\eta_R = \frac{q_d \cdot H}{q \cdot h}}$$ The ratio $q_d/q$ represents the fraction of water actually delivered, and $H/h$ is the head amplification ratio. --- **Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)** **Given:** - Time $t = 60 \text{ s}$ - Volume delivered $V_d = 180 \text{ L} = 0.180 \text{ m}^3$ - Volume wasted $V_w = 540 \text{ L} = 0.540 \text{ m}^3$ - $H = 18 \text{ m}$ - $h = 4 \text{ m}$ - $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ **(1) The two discharges (2 marks — 1 mark each):** $$Q_d = \frac{V_d}{t} = \frac{0.180}{60} = 0.00300 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = \mathbf{3.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}$$ $$Q_s = \frac{V_d + V_w}{t} = \frac{0.180 + 0.540}{60} = \frac{0.720}{60} = 0.01200 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = \mathbf{1.2 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}$$ **(2) Rankine's efficiency (2 marks):** $$\eta_R = \frac{Q_d \cdot H}{Q_s \cdot h}$$ $$\eta_R = \frac{0.00300 \times 18}{0.01200 \times 4}$$ $$\eta_R = \frac{0.0540}{0.0480} = 1.125 = \mathbf{112.5\%}$$ > [!WARNING] > A value exceeding 100% signals that **Rankine's assumption is over-simplified**. In practice, the *actual* efficiency is always less due to losses — D'Aubisson's correction gives a more realistic figure. **(3) Useful power delivered (1 mark):** $$P_d = \rho g Q_d H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.00300 \times 18$$ $$P_d = 529.74 \text{ W} = \mathbf{0.530 \text{ kW}}$$ **(4) Input power from supply (1 mark):** $$P_s = \rho g Q_s h = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.01200 \times 4$$ $$P_s = 470.88 \text{ W} = \mathbf{0.471 \text{ kW}}$$ **(5) Comment on $Q_d/Q_s$ ratio (1 mark):** $$\frac{Q_d}{Q_s} = \frac{0.00300}{0.01200} = 0.25 = 25\%$$ The delivery ratio is **25%**, which is within the acceptable design range (10–30%) for a well-designed hydraulic ram. The head amplification ratio is $H/h = 18/4 = 4.5$, which is conservative — typical rams operate at ratios of 5 to 20. Hence the ram is operating **moderately efficiently** for a low-head amplification application. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Hydraulic Ram** - [x] **Hydraulic Ram = water-hammer-driven pump with no external power.** - [x] **Principle:** sudden closure of waste valve converts kinetic energy of moving water into pressure energy. - [x] **Two key valves:** waste (impulse) valve and delivery check valve. - [x] **Air vessel + snifting valve** are essential to prevent waterlogging and to smooth delivery. - [x] **Rankine's Efficiency formula:** $\eta_R = \dfrac{Q_d H}{Q_s h}$. - [x] **D'Aubisson's Efficiency formula:** $\eta_D = \dfrac{Q_d H}{Q_s \left(h + \dfrac{v^2}{2g}\right)}$. - [x] Rankine's efficiency is **always greater than or equal to** D'Aubisson's efficiency for the same data (because it underestimates input energy). - [x] **Convert all discharges to m³/s** before substituting; **state units** in the final answer. - [x] Always draw a **labelled schematic** in 7-mark theory questions — it carries 1 to 2 marks. - [x] Standard gravity **g = 9.81 m/s²**; water density $\rho$ = 1000 kg/m³. - [x] Practical efficiency range: **40% to 70%** for well-designed rams; above 100% means the chosen formula is over-idealized. - [x] **Head amplification ratio** $H/h$ typically 4 to 30; **delivery ratio** $Q_d/Q_s$ typically 0.05 to 0.30. - [x] The drive pipe must be **straight and at least 10–20 m long** for sufficient water-hammer mass. ---

topics.title: Reynolds Experiment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL407 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Reynolds Experiment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Reynolds Experiment** is a classical laboratory investigation in fluid mechanics, originally conducted by **Osborne Reynolds in 1883**, designed to demonstrate the existence of the two principal regimes of viscous fluid flow — **Laminar Flow** and **Turbulent Flow** — and to experimentally determine the **Critical Reynolds Number ($Re_{cr}$)** at which the flow regime transitions from one to the other. The experiment uses a transparent test section through which water flows at controlled rates, while a coloured dye filament is injected at the inlet to visualize the streamline behaviour of the fluid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PCMEL407):** Students are expected to record observations for both regimes, calculate the critical Reynolds number, plot the corresponding graph, and verify the result against the universally accepted theoretical range of **$2000 < Re_{cr} < 4000$**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "What Reynolds Saw"

Imagine you are standing on a foot over-bridge and watching a river below:

- **Laminar Flow Analogy:** Think of pedestrians walking slowly through a narrow, single-file corridor in the same direction. They glide past each other in neat, parallel tracks with no mixing — exactly like the **dye filament travelling as a straight, undisturbed thread** through the water in the Reynolds experiment.
- **Turbulent Flow Analogy:** Now picture the same pedestrians rushing chaotically during an emergency evacuation, bumping, crossing paths, and forming eddies — this mirrors what happens to the dye filament at high flow rates: it **breaks up, diffuses, and mixes into the bulk fluid**.
- **Transitional Analogy:** This is the in-between "rush-hour" phase — the dye thread starts wobbling, breaking into wavy oscillations before completely disintegrating.

### 1.3 The Three Regimes of Flow

| Regime | Reynolds Number Range | Dye Behaviour | Physical Character |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Laminar (Streamline)** | $Re < 2000$ | Straight, parallel coloured thread | Fluid layers glide over each other without mixing; viscous forces dominate |
| **Transitional** | $2000 < Re < 4000$ | Wavy / oscillating filament, occasional breakup | Unstable; minor disturbances amplified intermittently |
| **Turbulent** | $Re > 4000$ | Filament disintegrates, dye diffuses randomly | Chaotic motion; inertia dominates over viscosity |

> [!NOTE]
> The numbers **2000** and **4000** are universal benchmarks for **flow in circular pipes** under steady conditions. They were first experimentally verified by Reynolds and have since been confirmed millions of times in research and industry.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Kinematic viscosity of water at $20^{\circ}C$ : $\nu = 1.003 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$**
- **Kinematic viscosity of water at $25^{\circ}C$ : $\nu = 0.887 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$**
- **Density of water at $20^{\circ}C$ : $\rho = 998\ \text{kg/m}^3$**
- **Dynamic viscosity of water at $20^{\circ}C$ : $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$**
- **Lower Critical Reynolds Number : $Re_{c,lower} \approx 2300$**
- **Upper Critical Reynolds Number : $Re_{c,upper} \approx 4000$**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Reynolds Number is Dimensionless:** It is a *pure ratio* of **inertial forces** to **viscous forces** within the fluid. Hence it has no units — it is a **similarity parameter** that allows engineers to scale laboratory results to full-scale prototypes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Physics Behind Reynolds' Insight

Reynolds hypothesized that the relative importance of **inertial forces** (the fluid's tendency to keep moving) versus **viscous forces** (the fluid's internal friction resisting motion) governs the nature of the flow. He combined these into a single dimensionless group now universally called the **Reynolds Number ($Re$)**.

$$\text{Inertial Force per unit volume} \propto \rho V^2$$
$$\text{Viscous Force per unit volume} \propto \mu \frac{V}{D^2}$$

Taking the ratio:

$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$$

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of the Experiment

1. **Setup:** A horizontal transparent glass tube of known internal diameter $D$ is connected between an overhead constant-head water tank and an overflow sump.
2. **Dye Injection:** A reservoir of potassium permanganate ($KMnO_4$) solution or fluorescent dye is connected to a fine hypodermic needle whose tip sits at the **centre of the tube inlet**.
3. **Initial Laminar Observation:** With the **outlet control valve almost closed**, the discharge $Q$ is very small. The dye travels as a **single straight thread** parallel to the axis of the tube — this is **streamline (laminar) flow**.
4. **Gradual Increase in Velocity:** The valve is opened progressively. The mean velocity $V$ in the test section increases in steps.
5. **Lower Critical Point ($V_{c,lower}$):** The dye filament begins to **oscillate sinusoidally** and eventually breaks up intermittently. This corresponds to the lower critical velocity.
6. **Upper Critical Point ($V_{c,upper}$):** As $V$ is increased further, the dye becomes **completely diffused**, mixing with the surrounding water. This corresponds to the upper critical velocity.
7. **Measurement of Discharge:** For each flow setting, the discharge $Q$ is measured by collecting a known volume $V_{col}$ (typically $10\ \text{mL}$) in a measuring cylinder and recording the time $t$.
8. **Computation:** $Re$ is computed for each velocity. The values at which the visual transition occurs are recorded as the **lower** and **upper** critical Reynolds numbers.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table** — Memorize every entry; these are the only formulas tested in KTU 2024 viva and written exams for this experiment.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Description | Units (SI) |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu}$ | $\rho$: density, $V$: velocity, $D$: diameter, $\mu$: dynamic viscosity | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Reynolds Number (alt.) | $Re = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$ | $\nu = \mu/\rho$: kinematic viscosity | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Cross-sectional Area | $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^{2}$ | $D$: internal diameter of test tube | $\text{m}^2$ |
| 4 | Mean Velocity | $V = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | $Q$: volumetric discharge | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 5 | Volumetric Discharge | $Q = \dfrac{V_{col}}{t}$ | $V_{col}$: collected volume, $t$: time | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 6 | Critical Velocity | $V_{c} = \dfrac{Re_{cr} \cdot \nu}{D}$ | $Re_{cr}$: critical Reynolds number | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 7 | Critical Discharge | $Q_{c} = A \cdot V_{c} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^{2} \cdot \dfrac{Re_{cr}\cdot\nu}{D}$ | Combined formula | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 8 | Lower Critical Re | $Re_{c,\text{lower}} \approx 2300$ | Threshold for transition onset | Dimensionless |
| 9 | Upper Critical Re | $Re_{c,\text{upper}} \approx 4000$ | Threshold for fully turbulent | Dimensionless |
| 10 | Friction Factor (laminar) | $f = \dfrac{64}{Re}$ | Darcy friction factor for laminar flow | Dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **Absolute Value Rule:** When writing formulas involving absolute value (e.g., magnitude of shear stress), use $\lvert\ \cdot\ \rvert$ in LaTeX, NOT the bare pipe character `|`, as it will break KTU answer-sheet markdown rendering.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Reynolds number is one of the most applied dimensionless numbers in engineering practice:

- **Chemical Engineering:** Reactor design, pipeline sizing, mixing equipment selection.
- **Aerospace:** Airfoil boundary-layer analysis (Re is critical for stall prediction).
- **Civil Engineering:** Water supply networks, sewer design (assesses self-cleansing velocity).
- **Biomedical:** Blood flow through arteries — pathological turbulence can indicate stenosis.
- **Mechanical:** Heat exchanger design — turbulent flow enhances heat transfer coefficient by an order of magnitude.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Full Derivation of Reynolds Number from First Principles

Consider a fluid element of characteristic length $L$ moving with velocity $V$ inside a pipe.

**Step 1 — Identify Inertial Force.**
From Newton's second law applied to a fluid mass $m$:

$$F_{\text{inertial}} = m \cdot a = \rho \cdot \text{Volume} \cdot \frac{dV}{dt} \propto \rho V^{2} L^{2}$$

Here the characteristic acceleration scales as $V/t \sim V^{2}/L$, and the volume scales as $L^{3}$, giving $\rho V^{2} L^{2}$.

**Step 2 — Identify Viscous Force.**
From Newton's law of viscosity, shear stress $\tau = \mu \dfrac{dV}{dy} \propto \mu \dfrac{V}{L}$.

Multiplying by the surface area over which it acts ($L^{2}$):

$$F_{\text{viscous}} = \tau \cdot A \propto \mu \frac{V}{L} \cdot L^{2} = \mu V L$$

**Step 3 — Form the Dimensionless Ratio.**

$$Re = \frac{F_{\text{inertial}}}{F_{\text{viscous}}} = \frac{\rho V^{2} L^{2}}{\mu V L} = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu}$$

**Step 4 — Apply to Pipe Flow.**
For flow inside a circular pipe, the characteristic length is taken as the **internal diameter $D$** (Reynolds' original choice):

$$\boxed{Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}}$$

where $\nu = \mu/\rho$ is the **kinematic viscosity**.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Standard KTU-Style Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** In a Reynolds Experiment, water at $25^{\circ}C$ flows through a glass tube of internal diameter $25\ \text{mm}$. Calculate (a) the lower and upper critical velocities, (b) the corresponding critical discharges, and (c) the Reynolds numbers for a measured discharge of $12.5\ \text{L/min}$.

**Given Data:**
- $D = 25\ \text{mm} = 0.025\ \text{m}$
- $\nu = 0.887 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$ (water at $25^{\circ}C$)
- $Re_{c,\text{lower}} = 2300$, $Re_{c,\text{upper}} = 4000$
- $Q_{\text{meas}} = 12.5\ \text{L/min} = \dfrac{12.5 \times 10^{-3}}{60}\ \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$

---

**Step 1 — Compute Cross-Sectional Area.**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^{2} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.025)^{2}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 0.7854 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 4.909 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^{2}
\end{aligned}$$

---

**Step 2 — Compute Lower Critical Velocity.**

$$V_{c,\text{lower}} = \frac{Re_{c,\text{lower}} \cdot \nu}{D} = \frac{2300 \times 0.887 \times 10^{-6}}{0.025}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{c,\text{lower}} &= \frac{2.040 \times 10^{-3}}{0.025} \\
&= 0.0816\ \text{m/s} \\
&\approx 81.6\ \text{mm/s}
\end{aligned}$$

---

**Step 3 — Compute Upper Critical Velocity.**

$$V_{c,\text{upper}} = \frac{Re_{c,\text{upper}} \cdot \nu}{D} = \frac{4000 \times 0.887 \times 10^{-6}}{0.025}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{c,\text{upper}} &= \frac{3.548 \times 10^{-3}}{0.025} \\
&= 0.1419\ \text{m/s} \\
&\approx 141.9\ \text{mm/s}
\end{aligned}$$

---

**Step 4 — Compute Corresponding Critical Discharges.**

$$Q_{c,\text{lower}} = A \cdot V_{c,\text{lower}} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.0816$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{c,\text{lower}} &= 4.006 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^{3}/\text{s} \\
&= 40.06\ \text{mL/s} \\
&= 2.404\ \text{L/min}
\end{aligned}$$

$$Q_{c,\text{upper}} = A \cdot V_{c,\text{upper}} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.1419$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{c,\text{upper}} &= 6.966 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^{3}/\text{s} \\
&= 69.66\ \text{mL/s} \\
&= 4.180\ \text{L/min}
\end{aligned}$$

---

**Step 5 — Compute Reynolds Number for the Measured Discharge.**

Convert discharge: $Q_{\text{meas}} = \dfrac{12.5 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 2.083 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$

Compute velocity:

$$V_{\text{meas}} = \frac{Q_{\text{meas}}}{A} = \frac{2.083 \times 10^{-4}}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.4244\ \text{m/s}$$

Compute Reynolds number:

$$Re_{\text{meas}} = \frac{V_{\text{meas}} \cdot D}{\nu} = \frac{0.4244 \times 0.025}{0.887 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Re_{\text{meas}} &= \frac{1.061 \times 10^{-2}}{0.887 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= 11\,962 \quad (\text{Dimensionless})
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Since $Re_{\text{meas}} = 11\,962 \gg 4000$, the flow at this discharge is **fully turbulent**. The dye filament would be completely diffused at this rate.

---

### 3.3 Standard Observation Table (Record Format for Lab Record)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **exact table format expected in your KTU lab record**. The viva examiner will grade based on column headings, units, and consistent decimal places.

| Sl. No. | Volume Collected $V_{col}$ (mL) | Time $t$ (s) | Discharge $Q = V_{col}/t$ (mL/s) | $Q$ in $\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$ | Velocity $V = Q/A$ (m/s) | Reynolds No. $Re = VD/\nu$ | Observed Flow Regime |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | 10 | 95.2 | 0.105 | $1.05\times 10^{-4}$ | 0.214 | 6,030 | **Laminar** |
| 2 | 10 | 42.6 | 0.235 | $2.35\times 10^{-4}$ | 0.479 | 13,490 | **Laminar** |
| 3 | 10 | 12.5 | 0.800 | $8.00\times 10^{-4}$ | 1.630 | 45,910 | **Transitional** |
| 4 | 10 | 6.8 | 1.471 | $1.47\times 10^{-3}$ | 2.998 | 84,440 | **Turbulent** |
| 5 | 10 | 4.1 | 2.439 | $2.44\times 10^{-3}$ | 4.971 | 1,40,030 | **Turbulent** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note: The values shown above are *illustrative only*. In your actual lab record, you will identify the precise velocity at which the dye filament begins to oscillate (lower critical) and at which it completely diffuses (upper critical).

### 3.4 Graphical Plot Required in Lab Record

A **graph of Reynolds Number ($Re$) versus Mean Velocity ($V$)** must be plotted on standard graph paper. The two critical points are marked on this curve to identify the transition zone.

- **X-axis:** Mean Velocity $V$ in m/s
- **Y-axis:** Reynolds Number $Re$ (dimensionless)
- **Lower Critical Point:** Marked at $V_{c,\text{lower}}$, $Re = 2300$
- **Upper Critical Point:** Marked at $V_{c,\text{upper}}$, $Re = 4000$
- **Result:** The plotted line should be **linear and pass through the origin**, since $Re \propto V$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Reynolds Apparatus

Since the Reynolds apparatus involves physical components that are difficult to render directly in Mermaid, the following **functional block diagram** maps the precise interaction between the major sub-systems, including control flow, fluid flow, and signal (visual) flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Overhead Constant Head Tank] --> A2[Stilling Chamber and Honeycomb Flow Straightener]
    A2 --> A3[Inlet Bell Mouth and Test Section Inlet Flange]
    A3 --> A4[Transparent Test Section - Borosilicate Glass Tube]
    A4 --> A5[Outlet Control Globe Valve - Variable Flow Resistance]
    A5 --> A6[Collecting Tank and Measuring Cylinder]

    B1[Dye Reservoir - KMnO4 Solution] --> B2[Dye Flow Control Needle Valve]
    B2 --> B3[Hypodermic Needle Injector Tip at Tube Centre]
    B3 --> A4

    A6 --> C1[Stopwatch for Time Measurement]
    C1 --> C2[Manual Observation Recording by Student]

    A4 --> D1[Visual Streamline Observation Window]
    D1 --> D2{Dye Filament Behaviour Classification}
    D2 --> D3[Straight Thread equals Laminar Flow]
    D2 --> D4[Wavy Oscillation equals Transitional Flow]
    D2 --> D5[Complete Diffusion equals Turbulent Flow]

    A1 --> E1[Overflow Pipe Returns Excess Water to Sump]
    A6 --> E1
    E1 --> A1
```

### 4.2 Functional Description of Each Block

| Block ID | Component | Engineering Function |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| A1 | Overhead Constant Head Tank | Maintains a steady hydraulic head to ensure constant driving pressure |
| A2 | Stilling Chamber with Honeycomb | Eliminates inlet turbulence and ensures uniform velocity profile |
| A3 | Bell Mouth Inlet | Reduces entry losses; provides smooth contraction into test section |
| A4 | Glass Test Tube | Transparent observation zone where flow regime is visualized |
| A5 | Globe Valve | Throttles discharge to vary the mean velocity $V$ |
| A6 | Measuring Cylinder | Collects timed volume for discharge calculation |
| B1–B3 | Dye Injection Subsystem | Provides the tracer for visual flow observation |
| C1–C2 | Data Acquisition (Manual) | Records time-volume data for $Q$ computation |
| D1–D5 | Visual Classification Logic | Categorizes flow into the three Reynolds regimes |
| E1 | Recirculation Loop | Returns water to overhead tank for sustainable experiments |

### 4.3 Schematic of Flow Physics Inside the Test Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Inlet - Uniform Velocity Profile] --> F2[Central Filament - Parabolic Velocity Profile Develops]
    F2 --> F3{Laminar}
    F2 --> F4{Transitional}
    F2 --> F5{Turbulent}

    F3 --> G1[Dye Thread - Straight and Unbroken]
    F4 --> G2[Dye Thread - Wavy and Oscillating]
    F5 --> G3[Dye Thread - Completely Diffused]
```

### 4.4 Safety & Procedural Precautions (Apparatus-Level)

> [!WARNING]
> **Mandatory lab safety and procedural precautions for the Reynolds Experiment:**
> - Always ensure the **glass test tube is firmly clamped** — bursting due to internal water hammer can cause serious injury.
> - **Open the outlet valve slowly** to prevent sudden surges that destroy the dye filament pattern.
> - Allow at least **60 seconds of stabilization time** after adjusting the valve before recording observations.
> - Keep the **dye reservoir sealed** to prevent contamination and oxidation of $KMnO_4$.
> - **Recalibrate stopwatch** at the start of each session to maintain timing accuracy.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]`
**Define Reynolds Number. What is its physical significance?**

**Model Answer:**
The Reynolds Number $Re$ is defined as the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces acting on a fluid element:

$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$$

It is a **dimensionless similarity parameter** that characterizes the flow regime. Its physical significance lies in predicting whether flow will be **laminar, transitional, or turbulent**, regardless of the fluid type or pipe dimensions, as long as the geometry and $Re$ value match. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Differentiate between laminar and turbulent flow.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Laminar Flow | Turbulent Flow |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Reynolds Number | $Re < 2000$ | $Re > 4000$ |
| Velocity Profile | Parabolic | Flat (logarithmic) |
| Fluid Layers | Distinct, non-mixing | Eddies and random mixing |
| Head Loss | Proportional to $V$ | Proportional to $V^{2}$ |
| Velocity | Low | High |

**(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the **Reynolds Experiment** with a neat sketch. Explain the procedure used to determine the critical Reynolds number. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Apparatus Required:**
- Overhead constant head tank
- Transparent glass test section of known diameter $D$
- Dye injection system (reservoir + needle valve + hypodermic needle)
- Outlet control valve
- Collecting tank and measuring cylinder
- Stopwatch

**Procedure:**
1. Fill the overhead tank with clean water and establish steady overflow so that head $H$ is constant.
2. Open the outlet valve slightly to allow slow flow through the test tube.
3. Allow the flow to stabilize for about a minute.
4. Open the dye valve so that a fine filament of potassium permanganate solution enters at the centre of the tube inlet.
5. Observe the dye through the test section. At very low flow, the dye travels as a **straight, undisturbed thread** → laminar flow.
6. Slowly open the outlet valve in small increments. At each step, allow stabilization, then note the time for a fixed volume (say 10 mL) to collect in the cylinder.
7. Record the velocity at which the dye thread **first begins to oscillate** → this gives $V_{c,\text{lower}}$ and $Re_{c,\text{lower}}$.
8. Continue increasing the flow until the dye **completely diffuses and disappears** into the bulk fluid → this gives $V_{c,\text{upper}}$ and $Re_{c,\text{upper}}$.
9. Compute $Re = VD/\nu$ for each velocity and plot the $Re$ vs $V$ graph.

**Result:** The critical Reynolds numbers obtained should lie between **2000 and 4000**, confirming Reynolds' classical finding.

**(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** In a Reynolds Experiment, water at $20^{\circ}C$ flows through a glass tube of internal diameter $20\ \text{mm}$. The critical velocity at which the dye filament begins to oscillate is found to be $0.115\ \text{m/s}$. Determine the **lower critical Reynolds number**. Take $\nu = 1.003 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $D = 20\ \text{mm} = 0.020\ \text{m}$
- $V_{c,\text{lower}} = 0.115\ \text{m/s}$
- $\nu = 1.003 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$

**Formula:**

$$Re_{c,\text{lower}} = \frac{V_{c,\text{lower}} \cdot D}{\nu}$$

**Substitution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Re_{c,\text{lower}} &= \frac{0.115 \times 0.020}{1.003 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= \frac{2.300 \times 10^{-3}}{1.003 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= 2293.3
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{Re_{c,\text{lower}} \approx 2293 \approx 2300}$$

**Verification:** This value lies within the universally accepted range of 2000–2300 for lower critical Reynolds number, validating Reynolds' classical result.

**(7 marks)**

---

#### QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary Exam 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a **labelled diagram**, explain the construction and working of the **Reynolds Apparatus**. List the observations and the inferences drawn from the experiment. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (Refer to SECTION_4 diagram):**
The apparatus consists of:
1. **Overhead Tank:** Maintains a constant hydraulic head $H$.
2. **Inlet Section:** Includes a stilling chamber and honeycomb flow straightener to ensure uniform, turbulence-free inflow.
3. **Test Section:** A long borosilicate glass tube of precisely known internal diameter $D$ (typically 15–25 mm).
4. **Dye Injection System:** A small reservoir of $KMnO_4$ solution connected via a needle valve to a fine hypodermic needle positioned at the centre of the tube inlet.
5. **Outlet Control Valve:** A globe valve to throttle flow.
6. **Measuring System:** A measuring cylinder and stopwatch.

**Working:**
Water flows under gravity from the overhead tank, through the test tube, and discharges into the collecting tank. The dye filament introduced at the inlet traces the centre streamline. By varying the outlet valve, the flow velocity is changed, and the dye behaviour reveals the flow regime.

**Observations:**
- At very low velocity, dye travels as a **straight line** (laminar).
- As velocity increases, dye thread becomes **wavy**.
- At high velocity, dye **mixes completely** with water (turbulent).

**Inferences:**
- Flow can be **laminar, transitional, or turbulent**.
- Transition occurs at $Re \approx 2000$–$4000$.
- **Lower critical $Re$** corresponds to onset of instability.
- **Upper critical $Re$** corresponds to fully developed turbulent flow.

**(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** A $30\ \text{mm}$ diameter pipe carries water at a mean velocity of $2.0\ \text{m/s}$. Determine:
**(i)** Whether the flow is laminar or turbulent.
**(ii)** The Darcy friction factor $f$.
Take $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $D = 30\ \text{mm} = 0.030\ \text{m}$, $V = 2.0\ \text{m/s}$, $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$

**(i) Compute Reynolds Number:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Re &= \frac{V D}{\nu} = \frac{2.0 \times 0.030}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= 6.0 \times 10^{4} = 60{,}000
\end{aligned}$$

Since $Re = 60{,}000 \gg 4000$, the flow is **fully turbulent**.

**(ii) Compute Friction Factor (using Blasius equation for smooth turbulent pipes):**

$$f = \frac{0.316}{Re^{0.25}} = \frac{0.316}{(60{,}000)^{0.25}}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Re^{0.25} &= (6.0 \times 10^{4})^{0.25} \\
&= (6.0)^{0.25} \times (10^{4})^{0.25} \\
&= 1.565 \times 10 \\
&= 15.65
\end{aligned}$$

$$f = \frac{0.316}{15.65} = 0.0202$$

$$\boxed{f \approx 0.0202 \text{ (Dimensionless)}}$$

> **Note:** If the question demands the laminar formula $f = 64/Re$, that applies *only* when $Re < 2000$, which is not the case here.

**(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes — Avoid these at all costs:**
> 1. **Forgetting units conversion:** Writing $D = 25\ \text{mm}$ directly into $Re = VD/\nu$ will give a $Re$ off by a factor of 1000. **Always convert mm → m first.**
> 2. **Using $\mu$ instead of $\nu$:** Since $Re = VD/\nu$ uses *kinematic* viscosity, plugging in $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ will yield wrong results. $\nu = \mu/\rho \approx 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$ for water.
> 3. **Confusing lower and upper critical Re:** $Re_{c,\text{lower}} \approx 2300$ (onset of instability) is *not* equal to $Re_{c,\text{upper}} \approx 4000$ (fully turbulent). The examiner will deduct **2 marks** for this swap.
> 4. **Not sketching the apparatus:** Even a freehand diagram in the descriptive question earns 1–2 marks. **Always draw the apparatus schematic** even if the question doesn't explicitly demand it.
> 5. **Mixing up $A = \pi D^2 /4$ with $\pi D$:** Cross-sectional area uses $\dfrac{\pi}{4}D^{2}$, not $\pi D$. A slip here leads to a $4/\pi$ error.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Final High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **Aim of Experiment:** To demonstrate laminar, transitional, and turbulent flow and to determine the **critical Reynolds number**.
- **Core Formula:** $\boxed{Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}}$ — dimensionless.
- **Critical Values to Memorize:** Lower $Re \approx 2300$, Upper $Re \approx 4000$.
- **Pipe Flow Regime Rules:**
  - $Re < 2000$ → **Laminar**
  - $2000 < Re < 4000$ → **Transitional**
  - $Re > 4000$ → **Turbulent**
- **Mean Velocity:** $V = Q/A = \dfrac{Q}{\frac{\pi}{4}D^{2}}$
- **Critical Velocity Formula:** $V_{c} = Re_{cr} \cdot \nu / D$
- **Dye Behaviour is the Visual Key:** Straight = laminar; wavy = transitional; diffused = turbulent.
- **Apparatus Components:** Overhead tank → stilling chamber → glass test tube (with dye injector) → outlet valve → measuring cylinder.
- **Viscosity of Water at Lab Temperature (assume $20^{\circ}\text{C}$):** $\nu = 1.003 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$.
- **Friction Factor (laminar only):** $f = 64 / Re$.
- **Graph Required:** $Re$ vs $V$ (linear, passing through origin).
- **Practical Skill Tested in Viva:** Identifying flow regime from a given $Re$ value, calculating critical velocity, explaining why Reynolds number is dimensionless.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bernoulli's Experiment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL407 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Bernoulli's Experiment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Bernoulli's Experiment** is a standard hydraulic laboratory investigation conducted on a transparent **Bernoulli's Apparatus** to *experimentally verify Bernoulli's Theorem* for a steady, incompressible, inviscid (ideal) fluid flow along a streamline. The apparatus consists of a horizontal converging-diverging (or venturi-shaped) test section fitted with multiple **piezometric tapping points** connected to **vertical manometer tubes**. The rise of water in these piezometric tubes directly indicates the **pressure head (p/ρg)** at corresponding sections. By simultaneously measuring the piezometric head, velocity of flow (via continuity), and datum head, the total energy at each section is computed and compared to verify the constancy predicted by Bernoulli's equation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition to Memorize:**
> "An experiment conducted on a test section of varying cross-section to verify that the sum of pressure head, velocity head, and elevation head remains constant at every section of the flow for an ideal fluid, i.e., $\frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{v^2}{2g} + z = \text{Constant}$."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are pushing water through a horizontal pipe that suddenly becomes **narrower** (like a garden hose being squeezed). What happens?

- The water **speeds up** in the narrow section — this is the *velocity head* increasing.
- The water **pushes less hard** against the walls of the narrow section — this is the *pressure head* decreasing.
- The two effects **trade off** — faster flow means lower pressure, and slower flow means higher pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Crowded Subway Gate:**
> Think of people walking through a wide corridor that narrows to a single gate. As people bunch up at the narrow gate, they *speed up* to get through, but the *crowd pressure* on the walls drops because everyone is focused on moving forward, not pressing sideways. This energy *trade-off* between pressure and velocity is exactly what Bernoulli's experiment visually demonstrates through the **varying water heights in the piezometer tubes**.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Density of water (at 25°C):** $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Specific weight of water:** $\gamma = \rho g = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$
- **Standard test fluid:** Clean tap water at room temperature (**~20–25°C**)
- **Standard tapping point count:** 6 to 8 pressure tappings along the test section
- **Typical test section length:** ~**0.5 m to 1.0 m** for KTU lab apparatus
- **Discharge measurement tolerance:** ±**2%** is acceptable per KTU lab manual standards

> [!TIP]
> The experiment is *visual* and *quantitative* — students must record piezometric heights, calculate the theoretical total head at each tapping, and prove (within experimental error) that this total head is constant.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Structured Logic Steps

The experiment is built on **two foundational equations** that the student must master:

### Step 1: Bernoulli's Equation (Energy Conservation)

For a steady, incompressible, frictionless flow along a streamline, the total mechanical energy per unit weight (called the **total head**, $H$) is conserved:

$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 = H$$

### Step 2: Continuity Equation (Mass Conservation)

For an incompressible fluid, the volumetric discharge $Q$ is the same at every cross-section:

$$Q = A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2 = \text{Constant}$$

### Step 3: Expressing Velocity from Continuity

$$v_i = \frac{Q}{A_i} \quad \text{(where } i = 1, 2, 3, \dots, n \text{ sections)}$$

This is the *experimental velocity* used in the Bernoulli verification.

### Step 4: Pressure Head from Piezometric Readings

The piezometric tube height above the centerline of the test section gives the **piezometric head**:

$$\frac{p_i}{\rho g} + z_i = h_i \quad \text{(piezometric height measured from a common datum)}$$

So Bernoulli's equation simplifies to:

$$h_i + \frac{v_i^2}{2g} = H \quad \text{(constant for all sections)}$$

### Step 5: Why This Matters

- In the **converging section**: area decreases → velocity increases → pressure head (piezometric height) *decreases* (water level in piezometer falls).
- In the **diverging section**: area increases → velocity decreases → pressure head *recovers* (water level in piezometer rises).
- The **sum** of the two remains **constant** — this is what the experiment visually proves.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind the Apparatus Design:**
> A varying cross-section is essential. If the pipe were uniform, both pressure head and velocity head would be constant, and the experiment would be trivial. The convergent-divergent shape forces a measurable energy trade-off.

## 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application |
|-------------------|-------------|
| **Aerospace** | Airfoil lift generation (low pressure on top wing) |
| **Civil — Pipe Networks** | Venturi meters for flow measurement |
| **Chemical** | Flow nozzles, orifice meters |
| **Mechanical** | Carburetor, atomizer, paint sprayer design |
| **Hydraulic Machines** | Draft tubes in turbines, pump suction analysis |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Formulas for the 3-Hour Lab Exam & Viva**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $H_i = \frac{p_i}{\rho g} + \frac{v_i^2}{2g} + z_i$ | Total head at section $i$ | m |
| 2 | $H_i = h_i + \frac{v_i^2}{2g}$ | Total head using piezometric height $h_i$ | m |
| 3 | $Q = A_i \cdot v_i$ | Continuity equation at any section | m³/s |
| 4 | $v_i = \frac{Q}{A_i}$ | Velocity at section $i$ from continuity | m/s |
| 5 | $v_i = \frac{\sqrt{2g \cdot (H - h_i)}}{1}$ | Velocity from head difference (alternative) | m/s |
| 6 | $Q = \frac{V_{\text{meas}}}{t}$ | Discharge from collecting tank: volume/time | m³/s |
| 7 | $\% \text{ Error} = \frac{\vert H_{\text{max}} - H_{\text{min}} \vert}{H_{\text{mean}}} \times 100$ | Verification error of Bernoulli's theorem | % |
| 8 | $A_i = \frac{\pi}{4} d_i^2$ | Cross-sectional area for circular sections | m² |
| 9 | $A_i = b_i \times d_i$ | Cross-sectional area for rectangular sections | m² |
| 10 | $Re = \frac{\rho v d}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (for flow regime confirmation) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $h_f = H_1 - H_2$ | Head loss between two sections (if any, due to viscous effects) | m |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **piezometric head** $h_i$ with the **total head** $H$. Remember: $h_i$ is what you *read off the piezometer tube*; $H$ is $h_i$ *plus* the velocity head, and $H$ should be the *constant* across all sections.

## 2.4 Boundary Conditions & Assumptions

- Steady flow (time-invariant at any point)
- Incompressible fluid (water at room temperature)
- Inviscid (frictionless) — *idealized; in practice, small losses exist*
- Flow along a single streamline (1-D approximation)
- No energy addition (no pump) or extraction (no turbine) between sections
- Atmospheric pressure acts uniformly on all piezometer tube tops
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Bernoulli's Equation (From First Principles — Euler's Equation)

This is the derivation students must understand for the **viva voce** and theory part of the lab exam.

### Step A: Start with Euler's Equation of Motion

For an inviscid, steady, incompressible flow, the Navier-Stokes equations reduce to Euler's equation along a streamline:

$$-\frac{\partial p}{\partial s} = \rho \frac{dv}{dt} = \rho v \frac{dv}{ds}$$

Where $s$ is the distance along the streamline.

### Step B: Express Along a Streamline

For flow with elevation component, we resolve gravity into its component along the streamline:

$$-\frac{\partial p}{\partial s} - \rho g \frac{dz}{ds} = \rho v \frac{dv}{ds}$$

### Step C: Rearrange and Multiply by $ds$

Rearranging the terms:

$$-\frac{\partial p}{\partial s} ds - \rho g \, dz = \rho v \, dv$$

### Step D: Identify Acceleration Identity

The left side involves two *exact differentials* and the right side is also an exact differential. Rewriting:

$$-dp - \rho g \, dz = \rho v \, dv$$

### Step E: Integrate Between Two Points (1) and (2) on the Streamline

$$\int_{p_1}^{p_2} (-dp) + \int_{z_1}^{z_2} (-\rho g \, dz) = \int_{v_1}^{v_2} \rho v \, dv$$

Evaluating each integral:

$$-(p_2 - p_1) - \rho g (z_2 - z_1) = \frac{\rho}{2} (v_2^2 - v_1^2)$$

### Step F: Rearrange the Energy Equation

Bring all terms to one side:

$$p_1 - p_2 + \rho g (z_1 - z_2) = \frac{\rho}{2}(v_2^2 - v_1^2)$$

Rearranging to a positive energy balance:

$$p_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 + \rho g z_1 = p_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2 + \rho g z_2$$

### Step G: Divide by Specific Weight $\rho g$ to Get the "Head" Form

$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 = H$$

This is **Bernoulli's Equation** in its classical head form. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of Each Term (Per Unit Weight of Fluid):**
> - $\frac{p}{\rho g}$ → **Pressure head** — energy due to fluid pressure.
> - $\frac{v^2}{2g}$ → **Velocity head (kinetic head)** — energy due to motion.
> - $z$ → **Elevation head (datum head)** — energy due to position.

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation of Velocity from Piezometric Head

Given that the discharge $Q$ is measured independently, the velocity at any section $i$ is:

$$v_i = \frac{Q}{A_i}$$

The **theoretical total head** at section $i$ is:

$$H_i^{\text{theo}} = h_i + \frac{v_i^2}{2g}$$

Substituting $v_i$:

$$H_i^{\text{theo}} = h_i + \frac{1}{2g}\left(\frac{Q}{A_i}\right)^2$$

The **percentage error** for verification:

$$\%E = \frac{\vert H_{\max} - H_{\min} \vert}{H_{\text{mean}}} \times 100$$

Where:

$$H_{\text{mean}} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} H_i$$

If $\%E < 5\%$, the experimental verification of Bernoulli's theorem is **satisfactory** for KTU lab standards.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Typical Calculation Workflow

| Step | Input Data | Computed Quantity | Formula |
|------|------------|-------------------|---------|
| 1 | Time $t$ for known volume $V$ | $Q$ | $Q = V/t$ |
| 2 | Piezometric readings $h_1, h_2, \dots, h_n$ | Pressure head at each section | $h_i$ (direct reading) |
| 3 | Diameters $d_1, d_2, \dots, d_n$ | Cross-sectional area $A_i$ | $A_i = \frac{\pi}{4}d_i^2$ |
| 4 | $Q$ and $A_i$ | Velocity $v_i$ at each section | $v_i = Q/A_i$ |
| 5 | $v_i$ | Velocity head at each section | $v_i^2 / 2g$ |
| 6 | $h_i$ and $v_i^2/2g$ | Total head $H_i$ at each section | $H_i = h_i + v_i^2/2g$ |
| 7 | All $H_i$ values | $H_{\text{mean}}$ | $H_{\text{mean}} = \sum H_i / n$ |
| 8 | $H_{\text{max}}, H_{\text{min}}$ | Percentage error | $\%E$ formula above |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Bernoulli's Apparatus

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow** of the experimental setup (water path, measurement points, and instrument interactions). All node IDs use alphanumeric prefixing per the compilation safeguard.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Supply Tank] --> B[Sump with Pump]
    B --> C[Control Valve]
    C --> D[Inline Flow Control]
    D --> E[Bernoulli Test Section]
    E --> F[Discharge Collection Tank]
    F --> G[Stopwatch and Measuring Cylinder]
    E --> P1[Piezometric Tapping 1 - Inlet]
    E --> P2[Piezometric Tapping 2 - Throat Inlet]
    E --> P3[Piezometric Tapping 3 - Throat]
    E --> P4[Piezometric Tapping 4 - Throat Outlet]
    E --> P5[Piezometric Tapping 5 - Diffuser Outlet]
    P1 --> M1[Manometer Tube 1]
    P2 --> M2[Manometer Tube 2]
    P3 --> M3[Manometer Tube 3]
    P4 --> M4[Manometer Tube 4]
    P5 --> M5[Manometer Tube 5]
    M1 --> R[Scale Reference Datum]
    M2 --> R
    M3 --> R
    M4 --> R
    M5 --> R
    R --> S[Observation Table Recording]
    G --> S
    S --> X[Calculate Q, v_i, H_i]
    X --> Y[Verify Constant Total Head]
    Y --> Z[Compute Percentage Error]
```

## 4.2 Piezometric Head Variation Along the Test Section — Conceptual Flowchart

This block diagram shows how the piezometric head behaves in different zones of the test section, which is the *key visual observation* students must record.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Inlet Section - Large Area] --> B[Converging Zone - Area Decreases]
    B --> C[Throat - Minimum Area]
    C --> D[Diverging Zone - Area Increases]
    D --> E[Outlet Section - Larger Area]
    A --> A1[High Piezometric Head]
    B --> B1[Decreasing Piezometric Head]
    C --> C1[Minimum Piezometric Head]
    D --> D1[Recovering Piezometric Head]
    E --> E1[Partially Recovered Piezometric Head]
    A1 --> F[Constant Total Head Line]
    B1 --> F
    C1 --> F
    D1 --> F
    E1 --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Lab Examiner's Insight:** The "constant total head line" is a horizontal imaginary reference that connects the *tops* of all the velocity-head-corrected points. Visually, it is the line on which $H_i$ would plot constant if drawn on a graph of $h_i$ versus section number. The slight downward drift from inlet to outlet indicates real viscous losses.

## 4.3 Apparatus Components Summary Table

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **Sump tank** | Reservoir for water; houses the centrifugal pump |
| **Centrifugal pump** | Supplies pressurized water to the test section |
| **Control valve (ball valve)** | Regulates discharge through the test section |
| **Stilling chamber / honeycomb** | Straightens flow to remove swirl and turbulence |
| **Test section (convergent-divergent)** | Variable area section with pressure tappings |
| **Piezometer tubes** | Vertical transparent tubes to read pressure head |
| **Collecting tank** | Receives discharged water for volumetric measurement |
| **Stopwatch** | Measures time for known volume collection |
| **Pointer gauge / scale** | Reference datum for measuring piezometric heights |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: State and explain Bernoulli's theorem as applied to fluid flow. Mention the assumptions involved.**

**Model Answer:**

Bernoulli's theorem states that *for a steady, incompressible, inviscid (frictionless) fluid flow along a streamline, the sum of pressure head, velocity head, and elevation (datum) head remains constant between any two points*.

**Mathematical Statement:**

$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 = \text{Constant}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady flow (flow parameters do not change with time).
2. Incompressible fluid (constant density).
3. Inviscid (no viscous/frictional losses).
4. Flow along a streamline.
5. No external energy addition or extraction between the two points.
6. No heat transfer.

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: How is the velocity of flow determined experimentally in a Bernoulli's apparatus? What is the role of the piezometric tube?**

**Model Answer:**

The velocity at any section $i$ of the test section is determined **indirectly** using the **continuity equation**:

$$v_i = \frac{Q}{A_i}$$

Where $Q$ is the volumetric discharge measured by collecting water in a tank for a known time interval, and $A_i$ is the cross-sectional area at section $i$ (calculated from the measured diameter).

**Role of the Piezometric Tube:**

A piezometric tube is a transparent vertical tube open to atmosphere at the top and connected to a pressure tapping at the section of interest. The water rises in the tube to a height equal to the **piezometric head** $h_i = \frac{p_i}{\rho g} + z_i$. By reading these heights, the pressure head at each section can be directly obtained without using a manometer.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q (a) [7 Marks]: Describe with a neat sketch the experimental setup used to verify Bernoulli's theorem. List the observations and calculations required.**

**Model Answer:**

**Apparatus Description:**

The Bernoulli's apparatus consists of:

1. **A sump tank** at the bottom housing a centrifugal pump.
2. **A control valve** to regulate the discharge.
3. **A transparent convergent-divergent test section** mounted horizontally with a series of pressure tappings (typically 5 to 8) along its length.
4. **Vertical piezometer tubes** connected to each tapping via flexible tubing.
5. **A collecting tank** at the downstream end with a stopwatch for discharge measurement.
6. **A pointer gauge** for reading piezometric heights from a common datum.

**Working Principle:**

Water is pumped from the sump, passes through the control valve, enters the test section where its area varies (convergent → throat → divergent), and discharges into the collecting tank. The water levels in the piezometer tubes directly indicate the piezometric head at each section.

**Observations Required:**
- Diameter at each tapping section ($d_1, d_2, \dots, d_n$).
- Piezometric heights ($h_1, h_2, \dots, h_n$) from a common datum.
- Rise of water in collecting tank ($V$) and corresponding time ($t$).
- Room temperature (for water density correction if needed).

**Calculations Required:**
1. Cross-sectional area at each section: $A_i = \frac{\pi}{4}d_i^2$
2. Discharge: $Q = V / t$
3. Velocity at each section: $v_i = Q / A_i$
4. Velocity head: $v_i^2 / 2g$
5. Total head: $H_i = h_i + v_i^2/2g$
6. Mean total head: $H_{\text{mean}} = \sum H_i / n$
7. Percentage error: $\%E = \frac{\vert H_{\max} - H_{\min} \vert}{H_{\text{mean}}} \times 100$

**Verification:** If the variation in $H_i$ is within ~5%, Bernoulli's theorem is experimentally verified.

---

**Q (b) [7 Marks]: In a Bernoulli's experiment, the following data were obtained. Verify Bernoulli's theorem and compute the percentage error.**

| Section | Diameter (mm) | Piezometric Head (cm) |
|---------|---------------|------------------------|
| 1 | 25 | 28.0 |
| 2 | 22 | 27.2 |
| 3 | 18 | 25.5 |
| 4 | 15 | 22.0 |
| 5 | 20 | 25.0 |
| 6 | 25 | 27.0 |

Discharge collected = 6.0 litres in 30 seconds.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate Discharge**

$$Q = \frac{V}{t} = \frac{6.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3}{30 \text{ s}} = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Areas**

$$A_i = \frac{\pi}{4} d_i^2$$

| Section | $d_i$ (m) | $A_i$ (m²) |
|---------|-----------|------------|
| 1 | 0.025 | $4.909 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| 2 | 0.022 | $3.801 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| 3 | 0.018 | $2.545 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| 4 | 0.015 | $1.767 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| 5 | 0.020 | $3.142 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| 6 | 0.025 | $4.909 \times 10^{-4}$ |

**Step 3: Calculate Velocities and Velocity Heads**

For section 1:

$$v_1 = \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{-4}}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.4074 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\frac{v_1^2}{2g} = \frac{(0.4074)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{0.1660}{19.62} = 0.00846 \text{ m} = 0.846 \text{ cm}$$

Performing similar calculations for all sections:

| Section | $v_i$ (m/s) | $v_i^2/2g$ (cm) | $h_i$ (cm) | $H_i$ (cm) |
|---------|-------------|-----------------|------------|------------|
| 1 | 0.4074 | 0.846 | 28.0 | 28.846 |
| 2 | 0.5262 | 1.412 | 27.2 | 28.612 |
| 3 | 0.7859 | 3.149 | 25.5 | 28.649 |
| 4 | 1.1320 | 6.532 | 22.0 | 28.532 |
| 5 | 0.6366 | 2.066 | 25.0 | 27.066 |
| 6 | 0.4074 | 0.846 | 27.0 | 27.846 |

**Step 4: Mean Total Head**

$$H_{\text{mean}} = \frac{28.846 + 28.612 + 28.649 + 28.532 + 27.066 + 27.846}{6}$$

$$H_{\text{mean}} = \frac{169.551}{6} = 28.259 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 5: Percentage Error**

$$H_{\text{max}} = 28.846 \text{ cm}, \quad H_{\text{min}} = 27.066 \text{ cm}$$

$$\%E = \frac{\vert 28.846 - 27.066 \vert}{28.259} \times 100 = \frac{1.780}{28.259} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\%E = 6.30\%}$$

**Step 6: Verification**

Since the total head values are approximately constant (varying within ~6%), Bernoulli's theorem is **experimentally verified** (acceptable range in KTU labs is up to 8-10%).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes Students Make:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** (mm to m, cm to m) — leads to a factor of 100 or 1000 error.
> 2. **Using the wrong area formula** — circular section uses $\pi d^2/4$, not $d^2$.
> 3. **Confusing piezometric head with total head** — $H$ must include $v^2/2g$, not just $h$.
> 4. **Rounding off too early** — keep 4-5 significant figures in intermediate steps.
> 5. **Not showing the unit conversions explicitly** in the answer — examiners deduct marks for this.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q (a) [7 Marks]: Derive Bernoulli's equation from Euler's equation of motion. State clearly the assumptions used in the derivation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Starting Point — Euler's Equation:**

For an inviscid, steady flow of an incompressible fluid, the equation of motion along a streamline is:

$$\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial s} + g \frac{dz}{ds} + v \frac{dv}{ds} = 0$$

**Step 1: Multiply through by $ds$:**

$$dp + \rho g \, dz + \rho v \, dv = 0$$

**Step 2: Integrate between two points (1) and (2) on the streamline:**

$$\int_{1}^{2} dp + \rho g \int_{1}^{2} dz + \rho \int_{1}^{2} v \, dv = 0$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the integrals:**

$$(p_2 - p_1) + \rho g (z_2 - z_1) + \frac{\rho}{2}(v_2^2 - v_1^2) = 0$$

**Step 4: Rearrange:**

$$p_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 + \rho g z_1 = p_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2 + \rho g z_2$$

**Step 5: Divide by $\rho g$ to obtain head form:**

$$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 = H \quad \blacksquare$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady flow.
2. Incompressible fluid ($\rho$ = constant).
3. Inviscid fluid (no viscosity, hence no shear stress).
4. Flow along a streamline.
5. Body force is conservative (gravity only).

---

**Q (b) [7 Marks]: A horizontal Bernoulli's apparatus has a test section with three tapping points. The diameters and piezometric heads are given below. The discharge measured is 1.5 × 10⁻⁴ m³/s. Verify Bernoulli's theorem and find the theoretical velocity at each section.**

| Section | Diameter (mm) | Piezometric Head (mm) |
|---------|---------------|------------------------|
| 1 | 30 | 200 |
| 2 | 20 | 165 |
| 3 | 30 | 195 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate Areas**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.030)^2 = 7.069 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.020)^2 = 3.142 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_3 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.030)^2 = 7.069 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Calculate Velocities (using $v = Q/A$)**

$$v_1 = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-4}}{7.069 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.2122 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_2 = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-4}}{3.142 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.4775 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_3 = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-4}}{7.069 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.2122 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Velocity Heads**

$$\frac{v_1^2}{2g} = \frac{(0.2122)^2}{19.62} = 0.00230 \text{ m} = 2.30 \text{ mm}$$

$$\frac{v_2^2}{2g} = \frac{(0.4775)^2}{19.62} = 0.01162 \text{ m} = 11.62 \text{ mm}$$

$$\frac{v_3^2}{2g} = \frac{(0.2122)^2}{19.62} = 0.00230 \text{ m} = 2.30 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Calculate Total Head at Each Section**

$$H_1 = h_1 + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} = 200 + 2.30 = 202.30 \text{ mm}$$

$$H_2 = h_2 + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} = 165 + 11.62 = 176.62 \text{ mm}$$

$$H_3 = h_3 + \frac{v_3^2}{2g} = 195 + 2.30 = 197.30 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: Verification**

The total head values are **not** constant (varying from 176.62 to 202.30 mm). This suggests either:
- Experimental error in piezometric readings.
- The datum was not taken correctly.
- Frictional losses between sections 1 and 2.

$$H_{\text{mean}} = \frac{202.30 + 176.62 + 197.30}{3} = 192.07 \text{ mm}$$

$$\%E = \frac{\vert 202.30 - 176.62 \vert}{192.07} \times 100 = 13.37\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> A 13% error is **higher** than the typical 5% acceptance threshold. The student would be required to identify the cause (likely inconsistent datum, air bubbles in piezometer tubes, or frictional losses in the converging section) and suggest improvements.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 TOP 5 REASONS STUDENTS LOSE MARKS IN THE LAB EXAM:**
>
> 1. **No unit conversions shown:** A typical 7-mark calculation problem loses 1-2 marks if you don't show $d$ in metres and $h$ in metres. Always write: "$d = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}$".
>
> 2. **Skipping intermediate steps:** Examiners in KTU award *step marks*. A direct jump from the given data to the final answer (no work shown) gets zero marks even if the answer is correct.
>
> 3. **Confusing $\pi d^2/4$ with $\pi d^2$:** This is the single most common formula error — a factor of 4 error propagates everywhere.
>
> 4. **Not labelling the table columns with units:** KTU lab answer books require column headers like "Diameter, $d$ (m)" — not just "Diameter".
>
> 5. **No conclusion/error statement:** The final 1-2 marks are for stating the percentage error and whether Bernoulli's theorem is "verified" or "not verified" within experimental limits. Many students forget this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 HIGH-YIELD REVISION BULLETS — MEMORIZE BEFORE EXAM:**

- ✅ **Bernoulli's theorem** is a statement of **conservation of mechanical energy** in an ideal fluid.
- ✅ The **three heads** are: **pressure head** ($p/\rho g$), **velocity head** ($v^2/2g$), and **datum head** ($z$).
- ✅ Total head $H = \frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{v^2}{2g} + z$ = **constant** (along a streamline, for inviscid, steady, incompressible flow).
- ✅ **Piezometric head** $h = \frac{p}{\rho g} + z$ is what the piezometer tube shows; the **total head** $H = h + \frac{v^2}{2g}$.
- ✅ **Velocity is NOT measured directly** — it is calculated from the continuity equation $v = Q/A$.
- ✅ **Discharge $Q$** is measured by **collecting a known volume** in a tank and **timing** it.
- ✅ **Area** for circular sections: $A = \frac{\pi}{4}d^2$; for rectangular sections: $A = b \times d$.
- ✅ In the **converging section**: velocity ↑, pressure head ↓, total head = constant.
- ✅ In the **diverging section**: velocity ↓, pressure head ↑, total head = constant.
- ✅ **Acceptance criterion for KTU lab**: percentage error in $H$ should be less than **~5% to 10%**.
- ✅ **Five key assumptions**: steady, incompressible, inviscid, along a streamline, no external energy input/output.
- ✅ **Derivation origin**: from **Euler's equation of motion** along a streamline.
- ✅ **Practical applications**: venturi meter, airfoil lift, carburetor, draft tube of a turbine.
- ✅ **Units consistency**: convert mm → m and cm → m **before** substituting into formulas to avoid catastrophic errors.
- ✅ **Sign convention for gravity**: elevation $z$ is measured **upwards** from a chosen datum.
- ✅ The experiment **does not verify** that $h$ is constant (it varies); it verifies that $H = h + v^2/2g$ is constant.
- ✅ **Real-world losses**: in practice, $H$ decreases slightly from inlet to outlet due to viscous friction (head loss $h_f$).
- ✅ **Continuity equation** $Q = A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ is the *second* pillar of this experiment (Bernoulli is the first).
