- ✅ **Graph plotting** (optional but recommended): Plot $h$ vs section number and $v^2/2g$ vs section number on the same axis to visually show the trade-off.
- ✅ **Dimensional check**: every term in Bernoulli's equation has units of **metres** (length), since all are energy per unit weight.
- ✅ **Common error in viva**: students claim Bernoulli applies to *all fluids*; the correct statement is "applies to **inviscid, incompressible, steady** flow."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Metacentric Height and Radius of Gyration of Floating Bodies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL407 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Metacentric Height and Radius of Gyration of Floating Bodies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Metacentric Height (GM):** The distance between the **Center of Gravity (G)** of a floating body and the **Metacenter (M)** of the body, measured along the vertical line passing through G and B (Center of Buoyancy). It is the primary geometric index used to quantify the **static stability** of a floating or submerged body in a fluid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Metacenter (M):** The point at which the line of action of the new buoyant force (after a small angular tilt) intersects the original vertical line passing through the center of gravity. The metacenter exists only for infinitesimal tilts (typically $\theta \le 15°$) in the linear stability regime.

**Radius of Gyration (k):** The radial distance from the axis of rotation (waterline centroidal axis) at which the entire waterline area ($A$) may be assumed to be concentrated as a point mass, so that its moment of inertia equals the actual moment of inertia of the area about that axis.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine pushing a **rectangular wooden block** floating in a bathtub gently at one corner. The block tilts slightly and then rights itself. Why? Because the **Center of Buoyancy (B)** shifts to the new submerged geometry, creating a restoring couple with the **Center of Gravity (G)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Think of a **tightrope walker** holding a long pole. By holding the pole low, the walker's center of gravity is low, and they are very stable. By raising the pole, the center of gravity rises, and balance becomes harder. A floating ship behaves the same way:
> - **Low GM (G below M):** Ship is "stiff" — quick, snappy righting motion (like a cork).
> - **High GM:** Ship is "tender" — slow, lazy righting motion.
> - **Negative GM (G above M):** Ship **capsizes** — unstable (G is above M, so the body overturns).

> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics used in this experiment:**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
> - Standard tilt angle limit (linear stability): $\theta \le 4°$ to $15°$
> - Water density at standard conditions: $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Stability Theory — Step-by-Step Logic

When a floating body is tilted through a small angle $\theta$:

1. **Body is at equilibrium in the upright position:** G and B lie on the same vertical axis.
2. **Body is rotated through a small angle $\theta$:** The buoyancy force shifts to a new point $B'$. The new buoyancy vector is vertical and acts through $B'$.
3. **Restoring Couple is generated:** The line $B'G$ (or its extension) intersects the original vertical centerline at point **M** (Metacenter).
4. **Metacentric Radius (BM):** Distance between the new center of buoyancy ($B'$) and the metacenter (M). It depends purely on geometry.
5. **Metacentric Height (GM):** Distance between G and M. It is the **net stability indicator**.

### 2.1.1 Conditions of Equilibrium of Floating Bodies

| Condition | Mathematical Statement | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Stable Equilibrium | $M$ is **above** $G$ (i.e., $GM > 0$) | Body returns to upright position after tilt |
| Neutral Equilibrium | $M$ coincides with $G$ (i.e., $GM = 0$) | Body remains in tilted position |
| Unstable Equilibrium | $M$ is **below** $G$ (i.e., $GM < 0$) | Body capsizes |

## 2.2 Derivation of the Core Metacentric Formula

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **metacentric radius** $BM$ is a purely geometric property of the waterline area:
>
> $$BM = \frac{I}{V}$$
>
> where $I$ is the **second moment of area** of the waterplane (plan) about its longitudinal centroidal axis, and $V$ is the **submerged volume** of the floating body.

**Engineering Utility:** This formula is used in **ship design, pontoon bridges, floating docks, submarines, FPSO vessels, buoys**, and **offshore platforms** to ensure the structure does not capsize under loading and wave-action conditions.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | SI Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $BM = \dfrac{I}{V}$ | Metacentric radius (geometric property) | m |
| 2 | $I = A \cdot k^2$ | Moment of inertia via radius of gyration | m⁴ |
| 3 | $I = \dfrac{L \cdot B^3}{12}$ | Rectangular waterline area (about longitudinal axis) | m⁴ |
| 4 | $V = A_{cs} \cdot L_{sub}$ | Submerged volume of a rectangular body | m³ |
| 5 | $W = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot V$ | Total weight of floating body (Archimedes) | N |
| 6 | $GM = BM - BG$ | Metacentric height (when $G$ is above $B$) | m |
| 7 | $GM = \dfrac{w \cdot x}{W \cdot \tan\theta}$ | GM from shift-weight experiment | m |
| 8 | $GM = \dfrac{x}{\tan\theta}$ (when $W = w$) | Simplified form for equal shift weight and body weight | m |
| 9 | $k = \sqrt{\dfrac{I}{A}}$ | Radius of gyration of waterplane | m |
| 10 | $BG = \dfrac{V \cdot \rho_{body}}{2 \cdot A_{sub}} - \text{stuff}$ | Centroidal distance (measured from buoyancy center) | m |

> **Notation Key:** $I$ = second moment of area about longitudinal axis (m⁴), $V$ = displaced volume (m³), $A$ = waterplane area (m²), $k$ = radius of gyration (m), $L,B$ = length & breadth of waterplane (m), $W$ = weight of floating body (N), $w$ = shift weight (N), $x$ = horizontal shift of weight (m), $\theta$ = angle of tilt (radians or degrees), $g$ = 9.81 m/s², $\rho_w$ = 1000 kg/m³.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> - **Naval Architecture:** Used to design the hull profile of ships so that GM falls within an "asymptotic stability range" of 0.05 m to 0.20 m for ocean-going vessels.
> - **Offshore Engineering:** Determines roll period of semi-submersible platforms ($T \approx 2\pi \sqrt{k^2 / (g \cdot GM)}$).
> - **Civil Engineering:** Essential for designing **pontoon bridges**, **floating jetties**, and **caissons**.
> - **Hydraulic Engineering:** Floating weirs and gate buoys must have positive GM to avoid overturning.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Metacentric Radius Formula $BM = I/V$

### Setup

Consider a rectangular floating body of length $L$ and breadth $B$ (where $B$ is perpendicular to the tilt axis), floating in water. The body is tilted through a small angle $\theta$ about its longitudinal centroidal axis.

### Step 1 — Identify the Geometry

When the body tilts by angle $\theta$, the waterline cuts the body at an angle. On one side, an additional wedge of volume is submerged; on the opposite side, a wedge emerges. Let the original waterline plane be $W_1W_2$ and the tilted waterline be $W_1'W_2'$.

### Step 2 — Volume of the Wedge Immersed

Let $\delta x$ be the small horizontal displacement of a strip of width $\delta b$ on the waterplane. The depth of immersion of this strip on the heavier side is $\delta x \cdot \tan\theta$. Thus the **additional immersed volume** of an elemental strip is:

$$dV_{imm} = (\delta b) \cdot (\delta x \cdot \tan\theta)$$

### Step 3 — Horizontal Shift of the Center of Buoyancy

The center of buoyancy shifts horizontally from $B$ to $B'$. The new buoyant force still acts vertically upward, but its line of action is now eccentric to the original $B$. The horizontal distance between $B$ and $B'$ is denoted $BB'$.

By the **principle of moments** about the original $B$:

$$V \cdot BB' = \int (\delta x \cdot \tan\theta) \cdot \delta b \cdot \delta x = \tan\theta \int (\delta x)^2 \cdot \delta b$$

### Step 4 — Recognize the Integral as the Second Moment of Area

The integral $\int (\delta x)^2 \cdot \delta b$ over the entire waterplane is by definition the **second moment of area** $I$ of the waterplane about its longitudinal centroidal axis.

Therefore:

$$V \cdot BB' = \tan\theta \cdot I$$

### Step 5 — Geometric Relation between $BB'$ and $BM$

From the geometry of the inclined body, the angle subtended at the metacenter $M$ by the segment $BB'$ equals the tilt angle $\theta$. For small angles:

$$\tan\theta \approx \theta = \frac{BB'}{BM}$$

Substituting:

$$V \cdot BB' = \frac{BB'}{BM} \cdot I$$

### Step 6 — Final Simplification

Canceling $BB'$ from both sides:

$$\boxed{\,BM = \frac{I}{V}\,}$$

**Physical interpretation:** $BM$ depends *only* on the geometry of the waterplane and the submerged volume — it is a **constant** for a given loading condition.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the Experimental Metacentric Height Formula

### Setup

A floating body of total weight $W$ carries a movable weight $w$ (the "shift weight" or "poise"). Initially, $w$ is placed at the geometric center of the body. It is then shifted horizontally by a distance $x$ along the length, causing the body to tilt by an angle $\theta$.

### Step 1 — Initial Equilibrium

With $w$ at the center, the body floats upright. The weight $W$ acts through $G$ (downward), and the buoyant force $\rho_w g V$ acts upward through $B$.

### Step 2 — Apply Shift and Establish Moment Balance

When $w$ is shifted by $x$, it creates a **destabilizing couple** of magnitude $w \cdot x$ (with moment arm = $x$).

The body tilts by angle $\theta$, generating a **restoring couple** of magnitude $W \cdot GM \cdot \tan\theta$ (since the horizontal moment arm from $G$ to the new buoyant force line is $GM \cdot \tan\theta$).

### Step 3 — Equilibrium of Couples

For equilibrium, the destabilizing couple must equal the restoring couple:

$$w \cdot x = W \cdot GM \cdot \tan\theta$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $GM$

$$\boxed{\,GM = \frac{w \cdot x}{W \cdot \tan\theta}\,}$$

### Step 5 — Special Simplified Form

In most laboratory setups, the floating body is *just* the model (no extra constant load), so the total weight $W$ is measured, and $w$ is the explicit shift weight. However, in the standard KTU experiment where the **shift weight $w$ equals the body weight** (i.e., $W = w$ — this is a simplification used in the standard pontoon apparatus), the formula reduces to:

$$\boxed{\,GM = \frac{x}{\tan\theta}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the actual PCMEL407 apparatus, a **spring balance** is typically used to measure the weight, and the formula used is the general one: $GM = w \cdot x / (W \cdot \tan\theta)$.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Radius of Gyration

The moment of inertia $I$ of the waterplane is also expressed in terms of the radius of gyration $k$:

$$I = A \cdot k^2$$

For a **rectangular waterplane** of length $L$ and breadth $B$ (tilt axis is parallel to length $L$):

$$I = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12}$$

Therefore:

$$A \cdot k^2 = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12}$$

$$k^2 = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12 \cdot A} = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12 \cdot (L \cdot B)} = \frac{B^2}{12}$$

$$\boxed{\,k = \frac{B}{\sqrt{12}} = \frac{B}{2\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.2887 \cdot B\,}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Check:** For a square waterplane of side $1 \text{ m}$, $k \approx 0.2887 \text{ m}$. This means the radius of gyration is approximately **28.87%** of the breadth — a useful benchmark for sanity checks during exams.

## 3.4 Summary of the Experimental Calculation Procedure

The standard KTU PCMEL407 experimental procedure is:

1. Measure the dimensions of the floating body (typically a pontoon): $L, B, H$ (height/depth).
2. Float the body in water and add a shift weight $w$ at the center. Record total weight $W$ (using a spring balance).
3. Measure the depth of immersion $h$ (or submerged volume $V = L \cdot B \cdot h$).
4. Shift the weight $w$ by a known distance $x$ (e.g., 50 mm, 100 mm, 150 mm).
5. Measure the resulting tilt angle $\theta$ (using a spirit level or graduated scale).
6. Compute $GM$ from: $GM = w \cdot x / (W \cdot \tan\theta)$.
7. Compute $BM = I / V$, where $I = L \cdot B^3 / 12$.
8. Compute $BG = BM - GM$.
9. Compute radius of gyration: $k = \sqrt{I / A} = B / (2\sqrt{3})$ for a rectangular pontoon.
10. Repeat for multiple values of $x$ and tabulate.

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (Model Solution Style)

**Given:** A rectangular pontoon of length $L = 0.6$ m, breadth $B = 0.4$ m, depth $H = 0.15$ m floats in water. The total weight $W = 80$ N. A shift weight $w = 20$ N is placed on the pontoon and moved by $x = 0.05$ m. The tilt angle measured is $\theta = 5.5°$.

**Step 1:** Compute the metacentric height (experimental):

$$\tan(5.5°) = 0.09629$$

$$GM = \frac{w \cdot x}{W \cdot \tan\theta} = \frac{20 \times 0.05}{80 \times 0.09629} = \frac{1.0}{7.703} \approx 0.1298 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the moment of inertia of waterplane:

$$I = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \frac{0.6 \times (0.4)^3}{12} = \frac{0.6 \times 0.064}{12} = \frac{0.0384}{12} = 0.0032 \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 3:** Compute the submerged volume (assuming pontoon is fully immersed, $V = L \cdot B \cdot H$):

$$V = 0.6 \times 0.4 \times 0.15 = 0.036 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4:** Compute the metacentric radius:

$$BM = \frac{I}{V} = \frac{0.0032}{0.036} \approx 0.0889 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5:** Compute $BG$:

$$BG = BM - GM = 0.0889 - 0.1298 = -0.0409 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6:** Interpret the result:

Since $GM > 0$, the body is **stable** in equilibrium. However, $BM < GM$ indicates that $G$ lies **below** $B$ (i.e., the body is bottom-heavy, which is the typical case for a pontoon made of dense material). The radius of gyration is:

$$k = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.0032}{0.24}} = \sqrt{0.01333} \approx 0.1155 \text{ m}$$

Or directly: $k = B / (2\sqrt{3}) = 0.4 / 3.464 = 0.1155$ m ✓ (matches).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — Stability Geometry of a Floating Body

This block-level functional architecture depicts the geometric relations between the key points (G, B, M) involved in the metacentric theory, including the role of the shift weight in the experiment.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Floating Body in Upright Position] --> B[Weight W acts at Center of Gravity G downward]
    A --> C[Buoyant Force FB acts at Center of Buoyancy B upward]
    B --> D[Equilibrium Condition: G and B on same vertical line]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Apply Shift Weight w at distance x from center]
    E --> F[Body tilts by small angle theta]
    F --> G[New Buoyancy acts at point B-prime]
    G --> H[Line B-prime G extended intersects original vertical at Metacenter M]
    H --> I[Metacentric Radius BM equals I over V]
    H --> J[Metacentric Height GM equals BM minus BG]
    I --> K[If GM greater than zero: STABLE]
    I --> L[If GM equals zero: NEUTRAL]
    I --> M[If GM less than zero: UNSTABLE CAPSIZE]
    J --> N[Radius of Gyration k equals sqrt of I over A]
    N --> O[k equals B over 2 sqrt 3 for rectangle]
    K --> P[Restoring Couple W times GM times tan theta]
    L --> P
    M --> P
    P --> Q[Equilibrium: w times x equals W times GM times tan theta]
```

## 4.2 Apparatus-Level Block Diagram — KTU PCMEL407 Experimental Setup

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Pontoon / Floating Body] --> P2[Water Tank]
    P1 --> P3[Shift Weight poise]
    P1 --> P4[Spirit Level / Tilt Indicator]
    P3 --> P5[Ruler for Shift Distance x]
    P1 --> P6[Spring Balance for Weight W]
    P1 --> P7[Measuring Tape for Dimensions L B H]
    P2 --> P8[Water with Density rho w]
    P8 --> P9[Submerged Volume V Displacement]
    P4 --> P10[Tilt Angle theta Reading]
    P5 --> P11[Shift Distance x Reading]
    P6 --> P12[Weight W Reading]
    P7 --> P13[Geometric Dimensions]
    P10 --> P14[Compute GM from formula]
    P11 --> P14
    P12 --> P14
    P9 --> P14
    P13 --> P15[Compute BM and k]
    P14 --> P16[Tabulate and Average]
    P15 --> P16
    P16 --> P17[Report Metacentric Height and Radius of Gyration]
```

## 4.3 Geometric Schematic of the Tilt Configuration (ASCII Representation)

> [!NOTE]
> *Note:* A precise engineering schematic of the tilted pontoon. The labels indicate the locations of G, B, B', and M, and the tilt angle $\theta$.

```
        Original Vertical (centerline)
                |
                |     M  <----- METACENTER (intersection)
                |    /|
                |   / |
                |  /  |
                | /   |
                G     |  <----- shift weight 'w' moved right
               /|     |
              / |     |
             /  |     |
            /   |     |  <----- theta (tilt angle)
           /    B'    |
          /     |     |
         /______|_____|________
        |               |     |
        |  Buoyant       |     |
        |  Force (up)    |     |
        |  at B'         |     |
        |________________|_____|
              Waterline
```

**Reading the schematic:**
- The original vertical line passes through $G$ (and initially through $B$).
- After tilt, the new buoyancy point $B'$ is shifted horizontally to the right.
- Extending the line $GB'$ upward until it crosses the original vertical gives the metacenter $M$.
- $GM$ is measured along the original vertical from $G$ to $M$.
- $\theta$ is the angle between the original vertical and the new $GB'$ line.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper PCMEL407, 2024 Scheme]**

> **Q:** Define the terms **(a) Metacentric Height (GM)** and **(b) Radius of Gyration (k)** with respect to a floating body.

**Model Answer (3 Marks Distribution):**

- **(a) Metacentric Height (GM):** (1.5 Marks) The distance between the **Center of Gravity (G)** of a floating body and its **Metacenter (M)**, measured along the vertical line passing through G. It is the governing parameter that determines the **static stability** of a floating body. If $GM > 0$, the body is stable; if $GM = 0$, neutral; if $GM < 0$, unstable (capsizes).

- **(b) Radius of Gyration (k):** (1.5 Marks) It is the radial distance from the waterline centroidal axis (the axis about which tilting occurs) at which the **entire waterplane area may be assumed concentrated as a point mass**, producing the same second moment of area as the actual distributed area. Mathematically, $k = \sqrt{I / A}$.

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — PCMEL407, 2024 Scheme]**

> **Q:** State and explain the **three conditions of equilibrium** of a floating body. How are they related to the metacentric height?

**Model Answer (3 Marks Distribution):**

A floating body can be in any of three equilibrium states depending on the relative position of $G$ and $M$:

| Condition | Relation | Physical Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Stable** | $M$ above $G$ (i.e., $GM > 0$) | Restoring couple develops, body returns to upright |
| **Neutral** | $M$ coincides with $G$ (i.e., $GM = 0$) | No restoring couple, body remains in tilted position |
| **Unstable** | $M$ below $G$ (i.e., $GM < 0$) | Overturning couple, body capsizes |

Therefore, the **sign of $GM$** is the unique indicator of the type of equilibrium.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

> **Q (A):** (a) Derive the relation $BM = \dfrac{I}{V}$ for a floating body. Explain the physical significance of each term. **(7 Marks)**
>
> (b) A rectangular pontoon of length 0.5 m and breadth 0.3 m floats in water with a draft of 0.1 m. A weight of 15 N is shifted across the deck by 0.06 m, causing a tilt of 4°. The total weight of the pontoon plus load is 90 N. Calculate the metacentric height and the radius of gyration. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (A):**

**(a) Derivation of $BM = I/V$ — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** Consider a floating body tilted through a small angle $\theta$ about a longitudinal axis through the center of buoyancy $B$. On the heavier (sinking) side, an additional wedge of volume $dV_1$ is submerged; on the lighter (emerging) side, a wedge $dV_2$ emerges. The two wedges are equal in volume by conservation of the floating condition:

$$dV_1 = dV_2 = dV$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** The center of buoyancy shifts from $B$ to $B'$. The horizontal distance $BB'$ is determined by taking moments of the wedge volumes about the original $B$:

$$V \cdot BB' = \int x \cdot dV = \int x \cdot (x \cdot \tan\theta) \cdot dA = \tan\theta \int x^2 \, dA$$

The integral $\int x^2 \, dA$ is the **second moment of area $I$** of the waterplane about the longitudinal axis.

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** From the geometry of the tilt, $\tan\theta = BB' / BM$. Substituting:

$$V \cdot BB' = \frac{BB'}{BM} \cdot I \quad \Rightarrow \quad V = \frac{I}{BM} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{BM = \frac{I}{V}}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** **Physical significance:** $I$ is the second moment of area of the waterplane (m⁴), and $V$ is the submerged volume (m³). $BM$ (in m) is a **purely geometric property** — it depends only on the shape of the waterplane and the depth of immersion, not on the weight of the body.

**(b) Numerical Solution — 7 Marks**

**Given:** $L = 0.5$ m, $B = 0.3$ m, $h = 0.1$ m, $w = 15$ N, $x = 0.06$ m, $\theta = 4°$, $W = 90$ N.

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** Compute the metacentric height (experimental):

$$\tan(4°) = 0.06993$$

$$GM = \frac{w \cdot x}{W \cdot \tan\theta} = \frac{15 \times 0.06}{90 \times 0.06993} = \frac{0.9}{6.2937} \approx 0.1430 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Compute the moment of inertia of the waterplane:

$$I = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12} = \frac{0.5 \times (0.3)^3}{12} = \frac{0.5 \times 0.027}{12} = \frac{0.0135}{12} = 1.125 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Compute the submerged volume:

$$V = L \cdot B \cdot h = 0.5 \times 0.3 \times 0.1 = 0.015 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Compute the metacentric radius:

$$BM = \frac{I}{V} = \frac{1.125 \times 10^{-3}}{0.015} = 0.075 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Compute the waterplane area and radius of gyration:

$$A = L \cdot B = 0.5 \times 0.3 = 0.15 \text{ m}^2$$

$$k = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.125 \times 10^{-3}}{0.15}} = \sqrt{7.5 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 0.0866 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Verify with the rectangular formula:

$$k = \frac{B}{2\sqrt{3}} = \frac{0.3}{3.464} \approx 0.0866 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Answer:** $GM \approx 0.143$ m, $k \approx 0.0866$ m. Since $GM > 0$, the pontoon is **stable**.

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme — Internal Choice Alternative]**

> **Q (B):** (a) Describe, with a neat sketch, the **experimental procedure** to determine the metacentric height of a floating body in the laboratory. List the apparatus required. **(7 Marks)**
>
> (b) A ship model of weight 250 N floats in a test tank. The waterplane area is 0.4 m². The center of gravity is located 0.05 m above the center of buoyancy. If the moment of inertia of the waterplane is $0.012 \text{ m}^4$, determine: (i) the metacentric radius, (ii) the metacentric height, and (iii) state whether the model is stable. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer (B):**

**(a) Experimental Procedure — 7 Marks**

**Apparatus Required (2 Marks):**
1. Rectangular floating pontoon (model ship) of known dimensions ($L, B, H$).
2. Water tank (sufficient depth, transparent side wall preferred).
3. Movable shift weight (poise) of known mass.
4. Spirit level or graduated tilt indicator.
5. Ruler / measuring tape (mm precision).
6. Spring balance or weighing scale.
7. Pointer / needle for reading immersion depth.

**Procedure (5 Marks):**

1. **Step 1 (1 Mark):** Measure the dimensions $L, B, H$ of the pontoon accurately. Record the values.
2. **Step 2 (1 Mark):** Float the pontoon in the water tank. Place the shift weight $w$ at the **geometric center** of the deck. Use a spirit level to confirm the pontoon is horizontal.
3. **Step 3 (1 Mark):** Using the spring balance, record the total weight $W$ of the pontoon + shift weight.
4. **Step 4 (1 Mark):** Shift the weight $w$ along the length of the pontoon by a known distance $x$ (e.g., 25, 50, 75, 100 mm). Record the tilt angle $\theta$ using the spirit level / tilt indicator for each $x$.
5. **Step 5 (1 Mark):** Calculate $GM = w \cdot x / (W \cdot \tan\theta)$ for each reading, then average the values. Compute $BM = I / V$ and radius of gyration $k = \sqrt{I/A}$. Tabulate the results and report.

**Precautions:**
- Tilt angle should remain **small** ($\le 15°$) for linear stability theory to apply.
- The tank should be wide enough that the pontoon's tilt is not affected by the tank walls.
- Repeat readings and take the mean to minimize random error.

**(b) Numerical Solution — 7 Marks**

**Given:** $W = 250$ N, $A = 0.4 \text{ m}^2$, $BG = 0.05$ m (G is above B), $I = 0.012 \text{ m}^4$.

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** Compute the metacentric radius $BM$:

$$BM = \frac{I}{V}$$

We need the submerged volume $V$. From Archimedes' principle, $W = \rho_w g V$:

$$V = \frac{W}{\rho_w g} = \frac{250}{1000 \times 9.81} = \frac{250}{9810} \approx 0.02548 \text{ m}^3$$

$$BM = \frac{0.012}{0.02548} \approx 0.471 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Compute the metacentric height $GM$:

$$GM = BM - BG = 0.471 - 0.05 = 0.421 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Check stability:

Since $GM = 0.421 \text{ m} > 0$, the model is **stable** in equilibrium. The metacenter $M$ lies **0.421 m above G**.

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Compute the radius of gyration:

$$k = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.012}{0.4}} = \sqrt{0.03} \approx 0.1732 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer:** $BM \approx 0.471$ m, $GM \approx 0.421$ m, $k \approx 0.1732$ m, and the model is **stable**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing $BM$ and $GM$:** $BM$ is the distance from the Center of Buoyancy $B$ to the Metacenter $M$ (a geometric property). $GM$ is the distance from the Center of Gravity $G$ to $M$ (the stability criterion). Mixing them up is the **#1 reason for losing 2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert $W$ units:** If the weight is given in kgf or grams-force, students often use it directly in the $GM$ formula expecting Newtons. Always convert: $W (\text{N}) = m (\text{kg}) \times 9.81$.
> 3. **Using $V$ as the total body volume instead of the submerged volume:** Only the **submerged** volume enters the $BM = I/V$ formula. This is a frequent conceptual error.
> 4. **Mixing up tilt angle in degrees vs. radians:** The $\tan\theta$ function in calculators may default to degrees; always confirm the calculator mode. For small angles, $\tan\theta \approx \theta$ (radians), but this is only an approximation.
> 5. **Sign convention for $BG$:** $BG$ is positive when $G$ is **above** $B$ (most common case), and the formula is $GM = BM - BG$. If $G$ is **below** $B$ (a hollow body with ballast), then $GM = BM + BG$ — the **plus** sign applies.
> 6. **Missing the second moment of area for tilted orientation:** When the tilt axis is along the *breadth*, the relevant dimension is $L$ (length), and $I = B \cdot L^3 / 12$ — students often incorrectly use $I = L \cdot B^3 / 12$ for both orientations.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save This for the Night Before the Exam):**

- [ ] **Metacentric Height (GM):** Distance from $G$ to $M$ along the vertical — **the stability criterion**.
- [ ] **Metacentric Radius (BM):** Distance from $B$ to $M$ — **a geometric property** given by $BM = I/V$.
- [ ] **Experimental Formula:** $GM = w \cdot x / (W \cdot \tan\theta)$.
- [ ] **Stability Rule:** $GM > 0$ (stable), $GM = 0$ (neutral), $GM < 0$ (unstable).
- [ ] **Sign of $BG$:** $BG = BM - GM$ when $G$ is above $B$ (typical pontoon).
- [ ] **Radius of Gyration:** $k = \sqrt{I/A}$; for a rectangle, $k = B / (2\sqrt{3}) \approx 0.2887 \cdot B$.
- [ ] **Moment of Inertia (rectangle):** $I = L \cdot B^3 / 12$ (about axis parallel to length $L$).
- [ ] **Submerged Volume:** $V = W / (\rho_w g)$ from Archimedes' principle.
- [ ] **Small-angle assumption:** Tilt angle $\theta \le 15°$ is the linear-stability range.
- [ ] **Stiff vs Tender:** Stiff ship has small GM (snappy return); tender ship has large GM (slow return).
- [ ] **Procedure in 5 steps:** Measure → Place weight at center → Shift weight by $x$ → Measure $\theta$ → Compute $GM$.
- [ ] **Repeat & average** multiple readings of $x$ and $\theta$ to reduce experimental error.
- [ ] **Apparatus to remember:** Pontoon, water tank, shift weight, spirit level, spring balance, measuring tape.
- [ ] **Engineering applications:** Ship design, pontoon bridges, offshore platforms, floating jetties, buoys.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Test on Positive Displacement Pumps
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL407 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance Test on Positive Displacement Pumps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Positive Displacement Pump (PDP)** is a hydraulic machine that delivers a fixed volume of fluid per cycle regardless of the discharge pressure developed, provided the fluid does not leak out of the pump. The fluid is physically displaced (trapped, moved, and expelled) by the periodic movement of mechanical members such as pistons, plungers, gears, vanes, or screws.

In the KTU 2024 scheme lab syllabus (PCMEL407, Module Experiment 2), the **Performance Test on a Reciprocating Pump** is the canonical PDP experiment. Here, a single-cylinder, single-acting reciprocating pump is driven through a crank–slider mechanism. The performance is characterized by measuring:

- **Theoretical Discharge ($Q_{th}$)**
- **Actual Discharge ($Q_{act}$)**
- **Slip of the pump (in volume and as a percentage)**
- **Coefficient of Discharge ($C_d$)**
- **Mechanical Efficiency ($\eta_m$)**
- **Overall Efficiency ($\eta_o$)** and **Volumetric Efficiency ($\eta_v$)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Reciprocating pumps are the only true positive displacement pumps directly covered in the KTU B.Tech Fluid Mechanics Lab syllabus. The test procedure, observation table format, and formula derivations are standard questions in the University End Semester Evaluation (ESE).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Strawberry Filler" 🍓

Imagine you are filling tiny strawberry jam jars using a hand-operated piston syringe:

- **Forward Stroke:** The piston pulls back, creating a vacuum. The jar is sealed except for one inlet — jam rushes in to fill the chamber. **This is suction.**
- **Return Stroke:** The piston pushes forward, sealing the inlet. The jam is forced out of the only available outlet — into the delivery pipe. **This is delivery.**

The syringe delivers the **same volume every cycle**, regardless of how heavy or thick the jam is, or how high the outlet pipe rises. This is the *positive displacement* principle — the pump's geometry, not its speed, dictates the discharge.

A centrifugal pump would behave like pouring from a tilted bucket — the higher you tilt it (more pressure), the less comes out. A PDP behaves like the syringe — it **forces** out a fixed quantity.

## 1.3 Why Test a Reciprocating Pump?

A freshly manufactured pump **never** delivers its geometric theoretical volume. Several losses degrade performance:

- **Leakage past piston rings** → Fluid slips back to suction side
- **Late opening and early closing of valves** → Incomplete filling/emptying
- **Air/vapor entrainment** → Volume not fully occupied by liquid

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Lab Metric:** The difference between theoretical and actual discharge is called **"Slip"** — one of the most important KTU exam terms for this experiment. Most lab viva questions begin here.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value (in KTU Lab Manual) |
|---|---|
| Density of water at 25 °C | $\rho = 996.16 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ (often approximated as **$1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$**) |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$ |
| Standard motor speed (4-pole) | $N = 1440 \; \text{rpm}$ |
| Standard test duration | $t = 60 \; \text{seconds}$ (typically 1 minute) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Single-Acting Reciprocating Pump

A single-acting pump performs **only one delivery stroke per complete crank revolution**. The suction stroke is passive (driven by vacuum).

### Step-by-Step Operating Logic:

1. **Suction Stroke:** As the crank rotates 0° → 180°, the piston retracts. Cylinder pressure drops below atmospheric; the suction valve opens, and water fills the cylinder.
2. **Delivery Stroke:** From 180° → 360°, the piston advances. Pressure rises above delivery head; the suction valve slams shut and the delivery valve opens, pushing water into the delivery pipe.

> The volume swept per cycle equals the swept cylinder volume:
>
> $$V_{swept} = A \cdot L$$

where:
- $A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$ — Cylinder cross-sectional area
- $L$ — Stroke length (m)
- $D$ — Cylinder bore diameter (m)

## 2.2 Slip — The Critical Performance Index

Slip arises because the water cannot instantly follow the piston. The **theoretical discharge assumes instantaneous filling**; the **actual discharge is always less**.

### Step-wise Derivation of Slip:

1. Assume ideal filling: water enters the cylinder at the same rate as piston retraction. **Theoretical discharge** per second:
   
   $$Q_{th} = \frac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$$

2. In reality, due to leakage past valves and piston packing, the water delivered to the tank per second is $Q_{act}$.

3. The volume lost per second is **Slip**:
   
   $$S = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$$

4. Percentage Slip is more useful for comparison:
   
   $$\% \text{ Slip} = \frac{Q_{th} - Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \times 100$$

> [!NOTE]
> Slip can be **negative** if the pump runs at low speed and high head — air bubbles expand inside the cylinder, delivering *more* water than theoretically pumped. KTU viva: "Can slip ever be negative?" — **YES**, with explanation.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> Use `\vert` for absolute value symbols in formulas to keep markdown tables intact.

| Sl. No. | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theoretical Discharge (Single-Acting) | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | One delivery per revolution |
| 2 | Theoretical Discharge (Double-Acting) | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{2 A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | Two deliveries per revolution |
| 3 | Actual Discharge | $Q_{act} = \dfrac{R}{t}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | $R$ = rise in measuring tank; $t$ = time |
| 4 | Slip Volume | $S = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | Always positive for normal operation |
| 5 | Percentage Slip | $\%S = \dfrac{Q_{th} - Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \times 100$ | dimensionless | Typical range: 2% – 8% |
| 6 | Coefficient of Discharge | $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | dimensionless | Equivalent to volumetric efficiency |
| 7 | Theoretical Power | $P_{th} = \rho g Q_{th} H$ | $\text{Watts (W)}$ | $H$ = total head in m |
| 8 | Actual (Useful) Power | $P_{act} = \rho g Q_{act} H$ | $\text{W}$ | Power transferred to water |
| 9 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_o = \dfrac{\rho g Q_{act} H}{\text{Shaft Input Power}} \times 100$ | % | $g = 9.81$ |
| 10 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_m = \dfrac{\rho g Q_{th} H}{\text{Shaft Input Power}} \times 100$ | % | Theoretical water power ÷ shaft input |
| 11 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_v = C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | % | Same as coefficient of discharge |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Municipal Waterworks:** Reciprocating pumps drive village-level drinking water schemes where head is high and flow is low.
- **Oil & Gas:** Mud pumps on drilling rigs are triplex reciprocating pumps — exact PDP principle.
- **Hydraulic Presses & Cranes:** High-pressure, low-flow applications.
- **Concrete Pumping:** Slurry transport in construction.

In all cases, the **% Slip test** quantifies wear and tear on piston rings and valves — a critical preventive maintenance diagnostic.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Discharge of a Single-Acting Reciprocating Pump

We start from the geometry of one crank revolution.

**Step 1 — Swept Volume per Stroke:**

The piston displaces a cylindrical volume equal to the cylinder area times the stroke length.

$$V_{swept} = A \cdot L$$

**Step 2 — Number of Delivery Strokes per Second:**

For a single-acting pump, one revolution produces exactly one delivery stroke. If the crank rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute, then in one second it rotates $\dfrac{N}{60}$ revolutions.

$$\text{Strokes per second} = \frac{N}{60}$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical Volume Delivered per Second:**

The theoretical discharge is the swept volume times the number of delivery strokes per second.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{th} &= V_{swept} \times \text{Strokes per second} \\
Q_{th} &= (A \cdot L) \times \frac{N}{60} \\
Q_{th} &= \frac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Area is in $\text{m}^2$, stroke in m, N in rpm. Multiplying by $\frac{1}{60}$ converts minutes to seconds. This is why the formula has $\frac{N}{60}$ and not $N$ alone.

For a **double-acting** pump, two deliveries occur per revolution, doubling the discharge:

$$Q_{th}^{DA} = \frac{2 A L N}{60}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Coefficient of Discharge from First Principles

**Step 1 — Define Coefficient of Discharge:**

The ratio of actual to theoretical discharge is termed the coefficient of discharge. This is a measure of how effectively the pump fills and empties its cylinder.

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$$

**Step 2 — Express in Terms of Measured Quantities:**

Substitute $Q_{act} = \dfrac{R}{t}$ (rise of water in tank divided by time) and $Q_{th} = \dfrac{ALN}{60}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_d &= \frac{R/t}{ALN/60} \\
C_d &= \frac{60 \, R}{A \cdot L \cdot N \cdot t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Substitution (Example):**

Let $A = 0.01 \; \text{m}^2$, $L = 0.15 \; \text{m}$, $N = 1440 \; \text{rpm}$, $R = 0.06 \; \text{m}$ in a measuring tank of $0.5 \; \text{m}^2$ area, $t = 60 \; \text{s}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{act} &= \frac{0.5 \times 0.06}{60} = 0.0005 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s} \\
Q_{th} &= \frac{0.01 \times 0.15 \times 1440}{60} = 0.036 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s} \\
C_d &= \frac{0.0005}{0.036} = 0.0139 \; (\text{or } 1.39\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** A $C_d$ below 5% indicates heavy slip — likely due to worn piston rings or stuck valves. A well-maintained pump typically shows $C_d > 0.92$ (i.e., slip < 8%).

## 3.3 Derivation: Mechanical & Overall Efficiency

**Step 1 — Theoretical Work Done per Second:**

The pump must lift $Q_{th}$ to a height $H$ against gravity. The ideal mechanical work input needed per second is:

$$P_{th} = \rho \, g \, Q_{th} \, H$$

**Step 2 — Actual Useful Power Delivered to Water:**

Only $Q_{act}$ reaches the overhead tank effectively:

$$P_{act} = \rho \, g \, Q_{act} \, H$$

**Step 3 — Mechanical Efficiency:**

It is the ratio of theoretical water power to the actual shaft power supplied by the motor (measured using a dynamometer or wattmeter):

$$\eta_m = \frac{\rho \, g \, Q_{th} \, H}{P_{shaft}} \times 100$$

**Step 4 — Overall Efficiency:**

The overall efficiency compares useful water power to actual shaft input:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_o &= \frac{\rho \, g \, Q_{act} \, H}{P_{shaft}} \times 100 \\
\eta_o &= \eta_m \times \eta_v \\
\eta_o &= \eta_m \times C_d
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the **master relationship** for the KTU viva: Overall efficiency = Mechanical efficiency × Volumetric efficiency. Always confirm this identity holds during your lab calculations.

## 3.4 Derivation: Percentage Slip Computation

Given theoretical and actual discharges, percentage slip is defined as the ratio of slip volume to theoretical volume, expressed as a percentage:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% S &= \frac{Q_{th} - Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \times 100 \\
\% S &= \left( 1 - \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \right) \times 100 \\
\% S &= (1 - C_d) \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This identity directly links slip to coefficient of discharge — a high $C_d$ means low slip, and vice versa.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Single-Acting Reciprocating Pump Test Rig

The test rig consists of interconnected functional blocks. Each block performs a specific role, and the interactions between them produce measurable data for performance evaluation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Motor Drive Unit] -->|Mechanical Power| A2[Crank Slider Mechanism]
    A2 -->|Reciprocating Motion| A3[Cylinder and Piston Assembly]
    A3 -->|Vacuum Suction| A4[Suction Valve Non Return]
    A4 -->|Water Draw| A5[Foot Valve and Strainer]
    A5 -->|Intake| A6[Sump or Reservoir]
    A3 -->|Pressurized Delivery| A7[Delivery Valve Non Return]
    A7 -->|Flow Upward| A8[Delivery Pipe with Pressure Gauge]
    A8 -->|Discharge to| A9[Overhead Measuring Tank]
    A9 -->|Rise R in Time t| A10[Stopwatch and Scale]
    A1 -.->|Shaft Power Input| A11[Dynamometer or Wattmeter]
    A11 -->|Power Data| A12[Calculation and Tabulation Block]

    style A1 fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style A2 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1f6f3a,color:#000
    style A3 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    style A4 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    style A5 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    style A6 fill:#e2d4f0,stroke:#3d1a5e,color:#000
    style A7 fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#833c0c,color:#000
    style A8 fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style A9 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1f6f3a,color:#000
    style A10 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    style A11 fill:#e2d4f0,stroke:#3d1a5a,color:#000
    style A12 fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
```

**Diagram Explanation (for KTU record):**

- **A1:** Motor drive unit (typically 2 HP, 1440 rpm). Provides rotational mechanical energy.
- **A2:** Crank-slider mechanism converts rotary motion into linear reciprocating motion of the piston.
- **A3:** Cylinder and piston — the heart of the pump. Sweeps a volume $A \cdot L$ per stroke.
- **A4:** Suction valve is a one-way (non-return) valve opening only during the suction stroke.
- **A5:** Foot valve prevents the suction line from emptying when the pump stops (priming retention).
- **A6:** Water source — sump or reservoir.
- **A7:** Delivery valve opens only when delivery pressure exceeds static head.
- **A8:** Pressure gauge indicates total delivery head $H$ (static + friction).
- **A9:** Overhead measuring tank — volume rise is observed to compute $Q_{act}$.
- **A10:** Stopwatch and scale — primary measurement instruments.
- **A11:** Dynamometer or wattmeter — measures shaft input power for efficiency calculations.
- **A12:** Calculation block — all the formulas from SECTION 3 are applied here.

## 4.2 Operational Cycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SuctionStroke
    SuctionStroke: Suction Stroke - Piston Retracts
    SuctionStroke --> DeliveryStroke: Crank at 180 degrees
    DeliveryStroke: Delivery Stroke - Piston Advances
    DeliveryStroke --> SuctionStroke: Crank at 360 degrees - One Cycle Complete
    DeliveryStroke --> [*]: Pump Stopped
    SuctionStroke --> [*]: Pump Stopped
```

**State Description:**

- **Suction Stroke:** Crank position 0° → 180°. Piston moves backward, suction valve opens, delivery valve closes, water fills cylinder.
- **Delivery Stroke:** Crank position 180° → 360°. Piston moves forward, delivery valve opens, suction valve closes, water is forced upward.
- **Cycle Complete:** After 360° rotation, the crank returns to the initial position and the cycle repeats.

## 4.3 Energy Flow Through the Pump

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1[Shaft Power Input from Motor] -->|P shaft| E2[Mechanical Losses Bearings Friction]
    E2 -->|P ideal| E3[Theoretical Water Power]
    E3 -->|P water to flow| E4[Actual Water Power Delivered]
    E1 --> E3
    E3 -->|P slip loss| E5[Slip Losses Leakage Backflow]
    E5 --> E4

    style E1 fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style E2 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    style E3 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    style E4 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1f6f3a,color:#000
    style E5 fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#833c0c,color:#000
```

**Energy Interpretation:**

- $E_1 \to E_3$: Mechanical losses reduce shaft input to the theoretical water power level.
- $E_3 \to E_4$: Slip losses further reduce the actual water power delivered.
- **Overall Efficiency** = $E_4 / E_1$ = (Mechanical Efficiency) × (Volumetric Efficiency).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Define slip of a reciprocating pump and explain why it occurs.**

**Model Answer:**

Slip of a reciprocating pump is defined as the difference between the theoretical discharge and the actual discharge of the pump, expressed either as a volume per second or as a percentage of the theoretical discharge.

**Mathematically:**

$$S = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$$

Slip occurs due to several factors:

1. **Leakage of water past the piston packing and piston rings** — water slips back from the delivery side to the suction side.
2. **Delayed opening and early closing of suction and delivery valves** — the valves do not open instantly, so the cylinder is not filled completely.
3. **Entrapped air or vapor bubbles** in the cylinder reduce the effective volume available for water.
4. **Inertia of water** in the long suction pipe prevents instantaneous movement, delaying the filling process.

> [!NOTE]
> Slip is typically 2%–8% for a well-maintained pump. A higher slip indicates worn piston rings or faulty valves that require maintenance.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between a positive displacement pump and a centrifugal pump.**

**Model Answer:**

| Sl. No. | Feature | Positive Displacement Pump | Centrifugal Pump |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Working principle | Fluid is physically displaced by moving parts | Fluid is accelerated by centrifugal force of an impeller |
| 2 | Discharge vs Head | Discharge is **independent** of head | Discharge **decreases** as head increases |
| 3 | Pressure developed | **High pressure** possible at low flow | **Lower pressure** at high flow |
| 4 | Priming | Not required (self-priming in some cases) | **Mandatory** priming required |
| 5 | Suitability | Viscous fluids, high head, low flow | Large volumes, low to medium head |
| 6 | Examples | Reciprocating pump, gear pump | Radial flow pump, axial flow pump |
| 7 | Efficiency curve | Almost constant across head range | Has a peak (Best Efficiency Point) |

---

## PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a single-acting reciprocating pump. State the formula for theoretical discharge. (7 marks)

**b)** In a performance test on a single-acting reciprocating pump, the following data were observed: Cylinder diameter $D = 100 \; \text{mm}$, stroke length $L = 150 \; \text{mm}$, crank speed $N = 1440 \; \text{rpm}$, total head $H = 20 \; \text{m}$, rise of water in measuring tank $R = 0.4 \; \text{m}$ in $t = 60 \; \text{s}$. Measuring tank cross-sectional area $= 0.5 \; \text{m}^2$. Shaft input power $= 1.5 \; \text{kW}$. Calculate the theoretical discharge, actual discharge, slip, percentage slip, coefficient of discharge, mechanical efficiency, and overall efficiency. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A single-acting reciprocating pump consists of a piston moving inside a cylinder, with two non-return valves — one on the suction side and one on the delivery side. A crank-slider mechanism converts the rotary motion of the motor into reciprocating motion of the piston.

**Working:**

- **Suction Stroke:** The crank rotates from 0° to 180°. The piston moves backward, creating a partial vacuum inside the cylinder. Atmospheric pressure on the sump surface forces water through the foot valve and the suction valve into the cylinder. The delivery valve remains closed.
- **Delivery Stroke:** The crank rotates from 180° to 360°. The piston moves forward, increasing pressure in the cylinder. The suction valve closes and the delivery valve opens, pushing water up the delivery pipe to the overhead tank.

**Theoretical Discharge Formula:**

$$Q_{th} = \frac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$$

where $A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$, $L$ is the stroke length, and $N$ is the speed in rpm.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Cylinder Cross-Sectional Area:**

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.100)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical Discharge:**

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60} = \frac{7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.150 \times 1440}{60} = 0.02827 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Actual Discharge:**

The volume of water collected in $60 \; \text{s}$ is the tank area times the rise:

$$
V = 0.5 \times 0.4 = 0.2 \; \text{m}^3
$$

$$
Q_{act} = \frac{V}{t} = \frac{0.2}{60} = 3.333 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Slip and Percentage Slip:**

$$
S = Q_{th} - Q_{act} = 0.02827 - 0.00333 = 0.02494 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
\% S = \frac{0.02494}{0.02827} \times 100 = 88.21\%
$$

**Step 5 — Coefficient of Discharge:**

$$
C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{0.00333}{0.02827} = 0.1178
$$

**Step 6 — Mechanical Efficiency:**

Theoretical water power:

$$
P_{th} = \rho \, g \, Q_{th} \, H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.02827 \times 20 = 5546.6 \; \text{W}
$$

$$
\eta_m = \frac{P_{th}}{P_{shaft}} \times 100 = \frac{5546.6}{1500} \times 100 = 369.77\%
$$

**Step 7 — Overall Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_o = \frac{\rho \, g \, Q_{act} \, H}{P_{shaft}} \times 100 = \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.00333 \times 20}{1500} \times 100 = 43.55\%
$$

> **Interpretation:** A percentage slip of 88% is unreasonably high — this indicates severe pump damage (worn rings or stuck valves). In a healthy lab pump, expect $\%S < 10\%$.

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**a)** Explain the term "Coefficient of Discharge" of a reciprocating pump. Describe a laboratory test procedure to determine the slip and coefficient of discharge of a single-acting reciprocating pump. (7 marks)

**b)** A double-acting reciprocating pump has cylinder diameter $D = 150 \; \text{mm}$, stroke length $L = 200 \; \text{mm}$, speed $N = 90 \; \text{rpm}$, and total head $H = 30 \; \text{m}$. During a test, the actual discharge was found to be $Q_{act} = 0.005 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$. Calculate the slip, percentage slip, coefficient of discharge, theoretical power, and overall efficiency if the shaft power input is $4 \; \text{kW}$. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Coefficient of Discharge:** It is the ratio of the actual discharge of the pump to its theoretical discharge.

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$$

A higher $C_d$ indicates better volumetric efficiency and less slippage. For a well-maintained pump, $C_d$ is typically greater than 0.92.

**Laboratory Test Procedure:**

1. **Setup:** Couple the reciprocating pump to an electric motor via a V-belt and measure the bore diameter $D$ and stroke length $L$ using vernier calipers. Note the speed $N$ from the motor nameplate (or measure with a tachometer).
2. **Priming:** Fill the suction pipe and pump casing with water to remove air. Open the delivery valve.
3. **Run the Pump:** Start the motor and let the pump run steadily for a few minutes until the discharge stabilizes.
4. **Measure Actual Discharge:** Direct the delivery pipe into a measuring tank of known cross-sectional area. Note the rise of water $R$ over a measured time interval $t$ (typically 60 seconds) using a stopwatch.
5. **Measure Shaft Power:** Use a rope-brake dynamometer or wattmeter to record the shaft input power.
6. **Record Head:** Note the reading of the pressure gauge on the delivery side to compute the total head $H$.
7. **Compute:** Calculate $Q_{th}$, $Q_{act}$, slip, percentage slip, $C_d$, $\eta_m$, and $\eta_o$ using the standard formulas.
8. **Repeat:** Take at least three readings at different delivery pressures (by throttling the delivery valve) for a complete characteristic plot.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Cylinder Area:**

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.150)^2 = 1.767 \times 10^{-2} \; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical Discharge (Double-Acting Formula):**

For a double-acting pump, two deliveries occur per revolution:

$$
Q_{th} = \frac{2 \cdot A \cdot L \cdot N}{60} = \frac{2 \times 1.767 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.200 \times 90}{60} = 1.061 \times 10^{-2} \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Slip and Percentage Slip:**

$$
S = Q_{th} - Q_{act} = 0.01061 - 0.005 = 5.61 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
\% S = \frac{0.00561}{0.01061} \times 100 = 52.87\%
$$

**Step 4 — Coefficient of Discharge:**

$$
C_d = \frac{0.005}{0.01061} = 0.4712
$$

**Step 5 — Theoretical Power:**

$$
P_{th} = \rho \, g \, Q_{th} \, H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.01061 \times 30 = 3122.4 \; \text{W} = 3.122 \; \text{kW}
$$

**Step 6 — Overall Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_o = \frac{\rho \, g \, Q_{act} \, H}{P_{shaft}} \times 100 = \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.005 \times 30}{4000} \times 100 = 36.79\%
$$

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Using $N$ instead of $N/60$:** Theoretical discharge formula must have $N$ divided by 60 to convert rpm to revolutions per second. Forgetting this gives an answer 60× too large.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 for double-acting pumps:** Using $A \cdot L \cdot N / 60$ for a double-acting pump costs a full 2–3 marks. Always check the question.
> 3. **Inconsistent units:** If $D$ is in mm, divide by 1000 to get meters. Forgetting this gives area in $\text{mm}^2$ instead of $\text{m}^2$, and discharge in wrong units.
> 4. **Confusing $C_d$ with $\eta_v$:** They are mathematically equal for a reciprocating pump, but you must mention both names to get full marks.
> 5. **Reporting overall efficiency greater than 100%:** This is physically impossible and indicates a calculation error or wrong units.
> 6. **Not mentioning priming in the procedure:** Priming the pump before starting is a **mandatory step** in any KTU procedure question.
> 7. **Forgetting $g = 9.81$:** Always include the gravitational constant with units when calculating power.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **Positive Displacement Pump** delivers a **fixed volume per cycle**, independent of pressure.
- ✅ A **single-acting pump** delivers once per revolution; **double-acting** delivers twice.
- ✅ **Theoretical Discharge:** $Q_{th} = \dfrac{ALN}{60}$ (single-acting) or $\dfrac{2ALN}{60}$ (double-acting).
- ✅ **Actual Discharge:** $Q_{act} = \dfrac{V_{collected}}{t} = \dfrac{R \cdot A_{tank}}{t}$.
- ✅ **Slip** is always $Q_{th} - Q_{act}$, and is caused by leakage, valve lag, and entrapped air.
- ✅ **% Slip** = $(1 - C_d) \times 100$. Typical values: 2%–8%.
- ✅ **Coefficient of Discharge** $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ = **Volumetric Efficiency**.
- ✅ **Mechanical Efficiency** $\eta_m = \dfrac{\rho g Q_{th} H}{P_{shaft}} \times 100$.
- ✅ **Overall Efficiency** $\eta_o = \eta_m \times \eta_v = \eta_m \times C_d$.
- ✅ **Theoretical Power** = $\rho \, g \, Q_{th} \, H$ — the energy ideal pump would impart to the fluid.
- ✅ Always **prime the pump** before starting to expel air from the suction line.
- ✅ Slip can be **negative** if air expands inside the cylinder (cavitation-like effect at low speed).
- ✅ **Standard test:** Use a measuring tank of known area, record rise $R$ in time $t$, and compute $Q_{act}$.
- ✅ **Physical Constants:** $\rho_{water} = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$, $N_{motor} = 1440 \; \text{rpm}$.
- ✅ **Real-world use:** High head, low flow applications — water supply schemes, hydraulic presses, oil well drilling.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Test on Centrifugal Pumps and Determination of Operating Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL407 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance Test on Centrifugal Pumps and Determination of Operating Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCMEL407 — Module 2):**
> A *performance test on a centrifugal pump* is a controlled laboratory experiment conducted to evaluate the actual operating behaviour of a centrifugal pump under varying discharge (flow) conditions, by simultaneously measuring the **manometric head (H)**, the **discharge (Q)**, the **shaft/brake power (P)**, and the **overall efficiency (η)**. The results are plotted as characteristic curves that uniquely identify the pump's operating point in any given pipeline system.

### 1.1 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture)

Imagine a **ceiling fan that pushes water instead of air**, connected to a hose. When you partially block the hose opening with your thumb, the water squirts higher and harder (head rises), but less water comes out (discharge drops). When you remove your thumb completely, lots of water flows out but the pressure drops. Meanwhile, the motor (input energy) works harder the more water you push.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A centrifugal pump converts the **kinetic energy** of a rotating *impeller* into **pressure energy** of the fluid. The performance test simply maps the trade-off between *flow rate*, *pressure head*, and *energy consumed*, just as the fan-hose analogy above does.

### 1.2 Standard Performance Metrics (with SI Units)

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Discharge | Q | m³/s | Volume of water delivered per second |
| Manometric Head | H | m | Pressure head developed by pump |
| Input Shaft Power | SP | W or kW | Power supplied to the pump shaft |
| Brake Power (Output) | BP | W or kW | Power transmitted to the water |
| Overall Efficiency | η₀ | % (dimensionless) | Ratio of water power to shaft power |
| Speed | N | rpm | Rotational speed of impeller |
| Specific Gravity of Mercury | S_Hg | **13.6** | Used in manometer calculations |

> [!TIP]
> In all KTU 2024 evaluations, the **standard density of water ρ = 1000 kg/m³** and **g = 9.81 m/s²** must be used unless stated otherwise.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Stepwise Logic

1. **Suction Phase:** The rotating impeller creates a low-pressure region at the eye (centre). Atmospheric pressure on the open suction tank forces water into the pump casing.
2. **Acceleration Phase:** The impeller vanes fling the water outward by centrifugal action, increasing its kinetic energy.
3. **Conversion Phase:** The volute casing decelerates the water, converting **kinetic energy → pressure energy** (Bernoulli's principle).
4. **Delivery Phase:** Pressurised water is discharged through the delivery pipe to the measuring tank.

### 2.2 Why a Differential Mercury Manometer is Used

A single pressure gauge cannot capture the **net pressure rise** across the pump. A **U-tube differential manometer** with mercury (S_Hg = **13.6**) directly measures the *manometric head* by balancing the pressure difference between the suction and delivery sides.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bernoulli Application Between Suction (1) and Delivery (2):**
> Applying Bernoulli's equation across the pump and simplifying gives the **manometric head** Hₘₐₙ (also called the *pump head* or *developed head*).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

| # | Quantity | Formula | SI Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Manometric Head | Hₘₐₙ = h + 13.6 \cdot x | m of water | h = vertical distance between pressure tap centres; x = mercury deflection |
| 2 | Discharge (Tank Method) | Q = (A \cdot R) / t | m³/s | A = tank area; R = rise in water level; t = time |
| 3 | Discharge (Volumetric) | Q = Volume / t | m³/s | For small flows |
| 4 | Velocity in Pipe | v = Q / A = Q / (\pi d²/4) | m/s | d = pipe inner diameter |
| 5 | Velocity Head | v² / (2g) | m | Added to pressure head in total head |
| 6 | Brake Power (Rope-Brake) | BP = 2\pi N (W₁ - W₂) R / 60 | W | W₁, W₂ = spring-balance loads; R = brake-drum radius |
| 7 | Shaft Power (Electrical) | SP = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi | W | For motor-coupled pumps |
| 8 | Water Power (Output) | WP = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H | W | Useful power transferred to water |
| 9 | Manometric Efficiency | \etaₘₐₙ = (Hₘₐₙ / H) \cdot 100 | % | Ratio of manometric head to total head |
| 10 | Mechanical Efficiency | \etaₘₑcₕ = (BP / SP) \cdot 100 | % | Frictional losses in bearings, etc. |
| 11 | Overall Efficiency | \eta₀ = (WP / SP) \cdot 100 | % | Master efficiency of the pump |
| 12 | Overall (Combined) | \eta₀ = \etaₘₐₙ \cdot \etaₘₑcₕ \cdot \etaᵥ | % | \etaᵥ (volumetric) \approx 1 for centrifugal pumps |
| 13 | Specific Speed | Nₛ = N \sqrt{Q} / H^{3/4} | rpm | Used to classify pump type |
| 14 | NPSH (Suction Limit) | NPSH = (pₐₜₘ / \rho g) - (pᵥ / \rho g) - Hₛ | m | Avoids cavitation |

> [!WARNING]
> In the KTU board exam, **units of BP and WP must be in the same system (either W or kW)** before computing efficiency. Mixing W and kW is a common mistake that costs full marks.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

Centrifugal pump performance tests are critical for:
- **Selection of pumps** for municipal water supply, irrigation, and HVAC systems.
- **Verification** of manufacturer curves against field data.
- **Diagnosis** of wear, blockage, or cavitation in operating pumps.
- **Determination of the Best Efficiency Point (BEP)** for stable system operation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Manometric Head (Hₘₐₙ)

**Setup:** Let the suction pressure tap be at section **1** (centre elevation z₁) and the delivery tap at section **2** (centre elevation z₂). A U-tube mercury manometer is connected between the two taps. Let **x** be the difference in mercury levels and **h = z₂ - z₁** be the vertical distance between the tap centres (when delivery is above suction) or vice versa.

**Step 1 — Pressures at Mercury Interfaces:**
Let the lower mercury level be on the suction side. The pressure at the suction-side mercury surface (just below it) is:

$$
p_1 + \rho g \, z_A
$$

where z_A is the height of water column in the suction limb above the mercury.

**Step 2 — Pressure at Delivery-Side Mercury Interface:**
The pressure at the same horizontal level (lower mercury) in the delivery limb is:

$$
p_2 + \rho g \, z_B - \rho_{Hg} \, g \, x
$$

**Step 3 — Hydrostatic Equilibrium:**
Since both points are at the same elevation in a connected fluid (mercury), the pressures must be equal:

$$
p_1 + \rho g \, z_A \;=\; p_2 + \rho g \, z_B - \rho_{Hg} \, g \, x
$$

**Step 4 — Rearranging for the Pressure Difference:**

$$
\frac{p_2 - p_1}{\rho g} \;=\; (z_A - z_B) + \frac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho} \, x
$$

**Step 5 — Substituting Specific Gravity of Mercury:**

Since S_Hg = **ρ_Hg / ρ** = **13.6**, and noting that h = z_A - z_B (the vertical separation between pressure-tap centres, measured as water column height):

$$
\boxed{\,H_{man} \;=\; h \;+\; 13.6 \cdot x\,} \quad \text{(metres of water)}
$$

**Step 6 — Total Head (Optional Full Form):**
If velocity heads are to be retained, the *total head* is:

$$
H \;=\; \frac{p_2 - p_1}{\rho g} \;+\; \frac{v_2^{\,2} - v_1^{\,2}}{2g} \;+\; (z_2 - z_1)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In most KTU lab setups, the suction and delivery pipe diameters are equal, so the velocity-head terms cancel, giving H ≈ Hₘₐₙ.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Overall Efficiency (η₀)

**Step 1 — Define Water Power:**
The power actually delivered to the fluid is the product of weight-flow rate and head developed:

$$
WP \;=\; \rho \, g \, Q \, H
$$

**Step 2 — Define Shaft Input Power (Brake Power):**
Using the rope-brake dynamometer, with W₁ and W₂ as the two spring-balance readings and R as the effective brake-drum radius, the net tangential force is (W₁ - W₂). The torque is:

$$
T \;=\; (W_1 - W_2) \, R
$$

The brake power is then:

$$
BP \;=\; \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} \;=\; \frac{2 \pi N (W_1 - W_2) R}{60}
$$

**Step 3 — Define Overall Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_0 \;=\; \frac{\text{Water Power Output}}{\text{Shaft Power Input}} \;=\; \frac{\rho g Q H}{BP}
$$

In percentage form:

$$
\boxed{\,\eta_0 \,(\%) \;=\; \frac{\rho \, g \, Q \, H}{BP} \times 100\,}
$$

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Sample Numerical (KTU Pattern)

> **Worked Problem [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]:**
> During a performance test on a centrifugal pump, the following observations were recorded. Calculate the manometric head, discharge, brake power, and overall efficiency at the operating point.

**Given Data:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Mercury manometer deflection, x | 0.18 m |
| Distance between pressure tap centres, h | 0.08 m |
| Diameter of delivery pipe, d_d | 75 mm |
| Diameter of suction pipe, d_s | 100 mm |
| Rise of water in measuring tank (area 0.5 m²) | 0.40 m |
| Time for collection | 50 s |
| Spring balance readings, W₁ and W₂ | 45 N and 8 N |
| Effective radius of brake drum, R | 0.30 m |
| Pump speed, N | 1440 rpm |

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**(a) Manometric Head (Hₘₐₙ):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{man} &= h + 13.6 \cdot x \\
        &= 0.08 + 13.6 \times 0.18 \\
        &= 0.08 + 2.448 \\
        &= \mathbf{2.528 \; m \; of \; water}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Discharge (Q):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{A \cdot R}{t} = \frac{0.5 \times 0.40}{50} \\
  &= \frac{0.20}{50} \\
  &= \mathbf{0.004 \; m^3/s} \quad (4 \; L/s)
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) Brake Power (BP):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T  &= (W_1 - W_2) \cdot R = (45 - 8) \times 0.30 = 11.1 \; N\text{-}m \\
BP &= \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1440 \times 11.1}{60} \\
   &= \frac{99\,593.4}{60} \\
   &= \mathbf{1659.89 \; W} \approx \mathbf{1.66 \; kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(d) Water Power (WP):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WP &= \rho \, g \, Q \, H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.004 \times 2.528 \\
   &= \mathbf{99.20 \; W} \quad (\approx 0.0992 \; kW)
\end{aligned}
$$

**(e) Overall Efficiency (η₀):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_0 &= \frac{WP}{BP} \times 100 = \frac{99.20}{1659.89} \times 100 \\
       &= \mathbf{5.97 \; \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The low efficiency (~6 %) indicates the pump is operating at a point far from its **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)**, typical of a low-flow, high-head condition. The BEP can be located only after plotting all observations on the η–Q curve.

---

### 3.4 Characteristic Curves — Behavioural Summary

| Curve | Trend with Increasing Q | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **H vs Q** | Decreases (slight, near-parabolic) | At Q = 0 (shut-off head), H is maximum. |
| **P (BP) vs Q** | Increases (slightly parabolic) | More water → more energy required. |
| **η vs Q** | Rises to a peak, then falls | Peak = **BEP**; preferred operating region. |
| **NPSHr vs Q** | Increases with Q | High Q risks cavitation at low suction head. |

> [!TIP]
> At the **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)**, the pump operates with minimum vibration, minimum noise, and maximum energy economy — hence BEP is the design point.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagrams are adapted as **Block-Level Functional Architecture** representations, as per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 fallback rule for complex physical drawings (actual pump cross-sections and graph plots cannot be rendered in Mermaid syntax).

### 4.1 Centrifugal Pump Test Rig — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[SUCTION TANK] -->|Suction Pipe with Foot Valve| B[VOLUTE CASING]
    B --> C[IMPELLER ON MOTOR SHAFT]
    C --> D[DIFFERENTIAL MERCURY MANOMETER]
    B --> E[DELIVERY PIPE WITH GATE VALVE]
    E --> F[MEASURING TANK WITH OVERFLOW]
    F --> G[STOPWATCH AND SCALE]
    C --> H[ROPE BRAKE DRUM]
    H --> I[SPRING BALANCE W1]
    H --> J[SPRING BALANCE W2]
    K[TACHOMETER] --> C
    L[STARTER PANEL] --> C
    M[MOTOR PRIME MOVER] --> C
    D --> N[MANOMETER SCALE X READING]
    G --> O[DISCHARGE Q]
    I --> P[BRAKE POWER BP]
    J --> P
    N --> Q1[MANOMETRIC HEAD Hman]
    O --> R[OVERALL EFFICIENCY ETA0]
    P --> R
    Q1 --> R
```

### 4.2 Calculation Flow — From Observations to Performance Curves

```mermaid
graph TB
    S1[Start: Raw Observations] --> S2[Compute Hman = h + 13.6x]
    S2 --> S3[Compute Q = AR over t]
    S3 --> S4[Compute BP = 2piN DeltaW R over 60]
    S4 --> S5[Compute WP = rho g Q Hman]
    S5 --> S6[Compute ETA0 = WP over BP x 100]
    S6 --> T1[Tabulate H Q BP ETA vs Time]
    T1 --> U1[Plot H vs Q Curve]
    T1 --> U2[Plot BP vs Q Curve]
    T1 --> U3[Plot ETA vs Q Curve]
    U1 --> V1[Identify Best Efficiency Point BEP]
    U2 --> V1
    U3 --> V1
    V1 --> W1[Recommended Operating Region for Field Use]
```

### 4.3 Centrifugal Pump Internal Component Map

```mermaid
graph TB
    P1[MOTOR COUPLING] --> P2[PUMP SHAFT]
    P2 --> P3[IMPELLER WITH CURVED VANES]
    P3 --> P4[VOLUTE CASING]
    P4 --> P5[DELIVERY NOZZLE]
    P2 --> P6[STUFFING BOX AND GLAND]
    P6 --> P7[SUCTION PIPE WITH STRAINER]
    P2 --> P8[BEARINGS FOR SHAFT SUPPORT]
    P4 --> P9[STAY VANES OR GUIDE RINGS]
    P3 --> P10[IMPELLER EYE OR SUC INLET]
```

### 4.4 Characteristic Curve Map (Conceptual Block Output)

```mermaid
graph LR
    XAXIS[DISCHARGE Q axis] --> H1[H vs Q DECREASING CURVE]
    XAXIS --> B1[BP vs Q INCREASING CURVE]
    XAXIS --> E1[ETA vs Q INVERTED U SHAPE]
    H1 --> ZB[BEST EFFICIENCY POINT BEP]
    B1 --> ZB
    E1 --> ZB
    ZB --> OP[RECOMMENDED OPERATING ZONE]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define *manometric head* of a centrifugal pump. State the formula used to measure it using a differential U-tube mercury manometer.

**Model Answer:**
The manometric head of a centrifugal pump is defined as the head developed by the pump corresponding to the net rise in pressure between the suction and delivery sides, neglecting kinetic-energy differences and minor elevation effects. It is measured using a differential U-tube mercury manometer by the formula:

$$
H_{man} \;=\; h \;+\; 13.6 \, x
$$

where h is the vertical distance between the centres of the suction and delivery pressure taps (in metres of water) and x is the difference of mercury levels in the manometer limbs. It is expressed in **metres of water**.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]** What is the *Best Efficiency Point (BEP)* of a centrifugal pump? Why is operation at BEP preferred?

**Model Answer:**
The **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)** is the specific discharge (Q) and corresponding head (H) at which the overall efficiency of the pump attains its maximum value on the η–Q characteristic curve. Operation at or near the BEP is preferred because it ensures:
- Minimum energy loss (best economy),
- Minimum vibration, noise, and wear,
- Maximum bearing and seal life,
- Stable hydraulic performance (no recirculation or cavitation).

---

### Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 2]*

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **manometric head** of a centrifugal pump when a differential U-tube mercury manometer is connected across the suction and delivery pressure taps. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In a performance test on a centrifugal pump running at **1450 rpm**, the following data were obtained at the operating point:
Mercury deflection, x = **0.15 m**; Centre-to-centre distance between pressure taps, h = **0.10 m**; Delivery pipe diameter = **80 mm**; Rise of water in a measuring tank of area **0.6 m²** = **0.45 m** in **60 s**; Rope-brake spring balance readings, W₁ = **48 N** and W₂ = **7 N**; Effective brake-drum radius, R = **0.25 m**.

Calculate:
(i) Manometric head, (ii) Discharge, (iii) Brake power, (iv) Overall efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:** (See Section 3.1 above — the boxed formula Hₘₐₙ = h + 13.6x with full hydrostatic-equilibrium steps.)

**Assumptions:**
1. Mercury is the manometric fluid (S_Hg = 13.6).
2. Suction and delivery pipes are of equal diameter (so velocity heads cancel).
3. Water is incompressible and flow is steady.
4. Friction losses in the connecting tubes of the manometer are negligible.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Manometric Head:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{man} &= h + 13.6 \, x \\
        &= 0.10 + 13.6 \times 0.15 \\
        &= 0.10 + 2.04 \\
        &= \mathbf{2.14 \; m \; of \; water}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Discharge:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{A \cdot R}{t} = \frac{0.6 \times 0.45}{60} \\
  &= \frac{0.27}{60} \\
  &= \mathbf{4.5 \times 10^{-3} \; m^3/s} \quad (4.5 \; L/s)
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Brake Power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T  &= (W_1 - W_2) \, R = (48 - 7) \times 0.25 = 10.25 \; N\text{-}m \\
BP &= \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1450 \times 10.25}{60} \\
   &= \frac{93\,343.5}{60} \\
   &= \mathbf{1555.73 \; W} \approx \mathbf{1.556 \; kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Overall Efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WP &= \rho \, g \, Q \, H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 4.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.14 \\
   &= 94.49 \; W \\
\eta_0 &= \frac{WP}{BP} \times 100 = \frac{94.49}{1555.73} \times 100 \\
       &= \mathbf{6.07 \; \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** With a neat labelled block diagram, explain the **construction and working of a single-stage centrifugal pump**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Sketch and explain the **performance characteristic curves** of a centrifugal pump: (i) H vs Q, (ii) BP vs Q, and (iii) η vs Q. What is the significance of the **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)**? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (Outline):**

**(a) Construction and Working:**
- **Main components:** Impeller (with curved/semi-open vanes), volute casing, suction pipe with foot valve and strainer, delivery pipe with a control valve, shaft, bearings, stuffing box with gland packing, and a prime mover (motor).
- **Working:** The motor rotates the impeller at high speed. Water enters the impeller eye axially, is flung outward by centrifugal force into the volute, where its kinetic energy is converted to pressure energy. The pressurised water leaves through the delivery nozzle.
- **Priming:** The suction pipe must be filled with water before starting (foot valve retains priming water).

**(b) Characteristic Curves:**
- **H vs Q Curve:** Head decreases slightly and almost linearly as Q increases; at Q = 0 (shut-off), H is maximum.
- **BP vs Q Curve:** Brake power increases with Q, because more water requires more energy.
- **η vs Q Curve:** Efficiency rises with Q up to a maximum (the BEP) and then declines, forming an inverted-U shape.
- **BEP Significance:** This is the point of maximum overall efficiency. Operating near the BEP ensures maximum economy, minimum wear, and quiet operation. It is the designer's recommended working point.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks in KTU 2024 ESE:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in Efficiency:** Mixing BP in watts with WP in kilowatts (or vice versa). *Fix:* Convert both to the same unit before computing η₀.
> 2. **Wrong Manometer Formula Sign:** Writing Hₘₐₙ = h − 13.6x instead of h + 13.6x. *Fix:* Remember that the delivery-side mercury is *always* depressed (lower) than the suction-side; hence the rise on the delivery side contributes positively to head.
> 3. **Forgetting to Square the Pipe Diameter in Velocity Calculations:** v = Q / A = 4Q / (πd²). Skipping the square leads to major errors in velocity-head corrections.
> 4. **Neglecting h (Centre-to-Centre Distance):** Some students only use 13.6x, omitting h, which is the *vertical distance between the gauge points* and is often small but non-zero.
> 5. **Plotting Graphs Without Identifying BEP:** The Examiner always looks for the BEP marking on the η–Q curve; failure to label it costs 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Missing the Effect of Speed Variation:** A question like *"What happens when N is doubled?"* requires the use of **Affinity Laws** (Q ∝ N, H ∝ N², P ∝ N³). Students often confuse the linear and quadratic relationships.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Manometric Head:** Hₘₐₙ = h + 13.6x (m of water) — derived via hydrostatic equilibrium of the U-tube manometer.
- ✅ **Discharge (Tank Method):** Q = (A × R) / t (m³/s).
- ✅ **Brake Power (Rope Brake):** BP = 2πN(W₁ − W₂)R / 60 (W).
- ✅ **Water Power:** WP = ρgQH (W).
- ✅ **Overall Efficiency:** η₀ = (WP / SP) × 100 (%).
- ✅ **Manometric Efficiency:** ηₘₐₙ = (Hₘₐₙ / H) × 100 (%).
- ✅ **Mechanical Efficiency:** ηₘₑcₕ = (BP / SP) × 100 (%).
- ✅ **BEP:** The discharge at which η₀ is maximum — preferred operating point.
- ✅ **Affinity Laws:** Q ∝ N, H ∝ N², P ∝ N³ (for same impeller geometry).
- ✅ **Standard Constants:** ρ_water = **1000 kg/m³**, g = **9.81 m/s²**, S_Hg = **13.6**.
- ✅ **Cavitation Prevention:** Maintain adequate NPSH margin (typically 1–2 m above NPSH₍ᵣ₎).
- ✅ **Procedure Outline:** Start with delivery valve closed → record no-flow reading → gradually open valve → take 8–10 sets of (x, h, t, R, W₁, W₂, N) readings → compute (Hₘₐₙ, Q, BP, η₀) → plot curves → identify BEP.
- ✅ **Precautions:** Ensure proper priming, check for air leaks in the suction line, maintain constant motor voltage for stable N, and read manometer to the nearest mm.
- ✅ **Engineering Use:** Pump selection, performance verification, fault diagnosis, and design-point identification in real pipeline systems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Test on Gear Pump
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL407 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB
