> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance Test on Gear Pump

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Gear Pump** is a type of **rotary positive displacement (PD) pump** in which fluid is displaced by the meshing action of two identical, precisely-machined gears rotating in opposite directions inside a closely-fitted housing. The fluid is trapped between the gear teeth and the casing, carried from the **suction (inlet) port** to the **delivery (outlet) port** without changing volume, and then forced out as the gears mesh together.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition Refresher:** A positive displacement pump delivers a *fixed quantity* of fluid per revolution, irrespective of the discharge pressure, distinguishing it fundamentally from centrifugal (rotodynamic) pumps where discharge drops with rising head.

### Constructional Components of an External Gear Pump

| Component | Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Driving Gear (Driver)** | Coupled to the prime mover (motor); drives the driven gear. |
| **Driven Gear (Idler)** | Meshed with the driver; rotates due to gear contact. |
| **Pump Casing / Housing** | Tightly encloses gears; provides suction and delivery chambers. |
| **Suction Port** | Inlet where low-pressure fluid enters. |
| **Delivery Port** | Outlet where high-pressure fluid exits. |
| **Bearings & Shaft Seals** | Support the rotating shafts and prevent leakage. |
| **Bear Plate / Side Plates** | Axially confine the gears and minimize end leakage. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Scoop Conveyor" Mental Model

Imagine two interlocking cookie dough scoops rotating against each other inside a tightly sealed metal tube:

1. As the scoops **un-mesh** at the inlet side, they create a small vacuum that *sucks in* fluid — just like opening your palm releases a partial vacuum that draws air in.
2. The fluid is then **trapped** between the curved tooth surface and the housing wall, riding along the outer perimeter of the gears.
3. When the gears **re-mesh** at the outlet side, the trapped fluid is *squeezed out* through the delivery port — like pressing two cupped hands together to push out water.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Positive Displacement?** Because each rotation displaces an *exact, geometrically fixed* volume of fluid — the volume swept by one gear tooth space. Hence, **flow rate is linearly proportional to shaft speed (N)**, provided there is no slip or leakage.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics & Constants

- **Density of water (ρ)** = **1000 kg/m³**
- **Acceleration due to gravity (g)** = **9.81 m/s²**
- **Typical Speed (N)** = **900 – 1500 rpm**
- **Typical Discharge Pressure** = **up to 175 bar (high-pressure applications)**
- **Volumetric Efficiency (ηᵥ)** for a healthy gear pump = **85 % – 95 %**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The "Why" and "How" Behind Gear Pump Working

The gear pump operates on a beautifully simple **displacement principle**. Let's break it down into structured logic steps:

- **Step 1 — Suction (Inlet) Stroke:**
  At the suction port, the gear teeth *un-mesh* (separate). This creates an expanding volume in the tooth-space, producing a **local low pressure** (partial vacuum). Atmospheric pressure on the sump pushes fluid into this void.

- **Step 2 — Conveyance (Transfer) Stroke:**
  The trapped fluid is locked between the *outer flank of a gear tooth* and the *casing wall*. As the gears rotate, this sealed pocket travels along the casing periphery — like a conveyor belt — without any internal slip.

- **Step 3 — Delivery (Discharge) Stroke:**
  When the gears re-enter mesh at the delivery side, the tooth-space volume *shrinks to zero*. This forced reduction squeezes the fluid out through the delivery port against the system pressure.

- **Step 4 — Continuous Repetition:**
  With each shaft revolution, every tooth space goes through this 4-phase cycle (un-mesh → carry → re-mesh → refill), producing a smooth, **pulsation-minimised continuous flow**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why External Gear Pumps are Most Common in Labs:** They have simple construction, two identical gears, high volumetric efficiency, and tolerate viscous fluids (oils, lubricants) — making them ideal for hydraulic systems and university experimentation.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

The following table compiles *every essential formula* you must memorise for the Gear Pump lab viva, record writing, and end-semester examination. All symbols carry SI units.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Theoretical Discharge, $Q_{th}$ | $Q_{th} = 2 \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot W \cdot N \cdot \eta_v$ | $D$=pitch diameter, $W$=gear width, $N$=speed in rps, $\eta_v$=volumetric efficiency. |
| 2 | Theoretical Discharge (alt.) | $Q_{th} = \frac{2 \cdot A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$ | $A$=area between teeth, $L$=length of tooth, $N$=rpm. |
| 3 | Actual Discharge (Measured) | $Q_{act} = \frac{\text{Volume collected}}{\text{Time taken}} = \frac{V}{t}$ | Determined from measuring tank: $Q$ in m³/s. |
| 4 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_v = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | Indicates internal leakage; healthy pump: 0.85–0.95. |
| 5 | Manometric Head, $H_m$ | $H_m = h_d + h_s + \frac{V_d^2 - V_s^2}{2g}$ | $h_d$=delivery gauge head, $h_s$=suction vacuum head, $V$=velocity. |
| 6 | Manometric Efficiency | $\eta_{man} = \frac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q_{act} \cdot H_m}{P_{shaft}}$ | Hydraulic-to-shaft power ratio. |
| 7 | Shaft Power, $P_{shaft}$ | $P_{shaft} = \frac{2 \cdot \pi \cdot N \cdot T}{60}$ | $T$=torque on shaft in N·m, $N$=rpm. |
| 8 | Rope-Brake Input Power | $P_{input} = \frac{2 \cdot \pi \cdot N \cdot (W - S) \cdot (R - r)}{60}$ | $W$=dead weight, $S$=spring balance, $R$=brake drum radius, $r$=rope radius. |
| 9 | Electrical Input Power | $P_{input} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$ | For 3-phase motor; $V$=line voltage, $I$=line current. |
| 10 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_m = \frac{P_{shaft}}{P_{input}}$ | Reflects bearing/gear friction losses. |
| 11 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_{man} \cdot \eta_m = \frac{P_{output}}{P_{input}}$ | Combined efficiency of pump. |
| 12 | Output (Hydraulic) Power | $P_{out} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q_{act} \cdot H_m$ | In Watts; convert to kW by $\div 1000$. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mark-Boosting Tip:** Examiners frequently ask students to *rearrange* the rope-brake formula to isolate **Net Load (W−S)** or **Radius (R−r)**. Practice isolating every variable — it is worth 2 easy marks!

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Gear pumps are the **workhorse of hydraulic systems**. Their compactness, high-pressure capability, and bidirectional operation (by reversing motor rotation) make them indispensable in:

- **Hydraulic presses and jacks** (manufacturing plants).
- **Automotive lubrication systems** (engine oil circulation).
- **Power steering units** in passenger vehicles.
- **Chemical dosing pumps** in water-treatment plants (handling viscous, corrosive fluids).
- **Agricultural sprayers** and **construction equipment**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Learn This in a Lab?** Conducting the performance test builds practical intuition on how *theoretical discharge deviates from actual discharge* — that deviation is precisely the **volumetric efficiency**, a key health indicator of any positive displacement pump.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Discharge of an External Gear Pump

**Starting Premise:** Each revolution of the gear pair displaces a volume equal to *the volume of all tooth spaces that have moved from the suction side to the delivery side*.

Let us define the geometric parameters:
- Let $D$ = pitch circle diameter of the gear (m).
- Let $W$ = axial width (face width) of the gear (m).
- Let $N$ = rotational speed of the gear (in revolutions per second, rps).

**Step 1 — Volume displaced per revolution per gear:**
When one gear rotates by one revolution, it sweeps a cylindrical volume equivalent to its pitch circle.

$$V_{per\,rev} = \pi \cdot D \cdot W$$

**Step 2 — Account for both gears:**
Since two gears rotate in mesh, *both* displace fluid simultaneously (one on each side of the meshing point). Hence, total displacement per revolution of the shaft:

$$V_{total} = 2 \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot W$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by rotational speed to get flow rate:**
The flow rate is volume swept per second. With $N$ revolutions per second:

$$Q_{th} = 2 \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot W \cdot N$$

**Step 4 — Convert from rps to rpm:**
If the speed is given in rpm (revolutions per minute), we must divide $N$ by 60 to convert to rps:

$$Q_{th} = \frac{2 \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot W \cdot N_{rpm}}{60}$$

**Step 5 — Introduce Volumetric Efficiency ($\eta_v$):**
Real fluid leaks back through clearances (between gear tips and casing, between gear side faces and side plates, and via the meshing point). The actual discharge is:

$$Q_{act} = \eta_v \cdot Q_{th} = \eta_v \cdot \frac{2 \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot W \cdot N}{60}$$

> This is the **master formula** students must reproduce from memory in any KTU numerical.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Input Power via Rope-Brake Dynamometer

A rope brake works on the principle of friction between a rope wrapped around the brake drum and a tensioning dead weight. The tangential frictional force creates a braking torque, which opposes motor torque.

**Step 1 — Net tangential force on the brake drum:**
The difference between the dead weight ($W$) pulling down on one side and the spring balance reading ($S$) supporting the other side gives the *net frictional pull*:

$$F_{net} = (W - S)$$

**Step 2 — Effective braking radius:**
The rope has its own thickness. The rope's *centre line* lies at radius $(R - r)$, where $R$ = outer drum radius and $r$ = rope radius. Therefore, the effective lever arm is:

$$R_{eff} = (R - r)$$

**Step 3 — Braking torque:**
Torque is force × perpendicular distance:

$$T = F_{net} \cdot R_{eff} = (W - S) \cdot (R - r)$$

**Step 4 — Power dissipated (which equals motor input power at steady state):**

$$P_{input} = \frac{2 \cdot \pi \cdot N \cdot T}{60} = \frac{2 \cdot \pi \cdot N \cdot (W - S) \cdot (R - r)}{60}$$

Where $N$ is in rpm and power is in Watts. To convert to kW, divide by 1000.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Insight:** The rope brake does *not* add or remove energy from the system — it merely converts mechanical power to heat via friction. The heat dissipated equals the input power at equilibrium.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem Statement:**
In a performance test on an external gear pump, the following readings were observed:

- Pitch circle diameter, $D$ = **80 mm** = **0.08 m**
- Gear width, $W$ = **40 mm** = **0.04 m**
- Speed, $N$ = **900 rpm**
- Volume of water collected in measuring tank, $V$ = **0.025 m³**
- Time taken, $t$ = **25 s**
- Delivery pressure head, $h_d$ = **20 m of water**
- Suction vacuum head, $h_s$ = **2 m of water**
- Rope brake dead weight, $W$ = **50 N**
- Spring balance reading, $S$ = **10 N**
- Brake drum diameter = **30 cm**, Rope diameter = **1.5 cm**

**Calculate:** Theoretical discharge, Actual discharge, Volumetric efficiency, Manometric head, Input power, Shaft power, Manometric efficiency, Overall efficiency.

### Solution (Step-by-Step):

**Step A — Theoretical Discharge:**

$$Q_{th} = \frac{2 \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot W \cdot N}{60} = \frac{2 \cdot \pi \cdot 0.08 \cdot 0.04 \cdot 900}{60}$$

Numerator simplification:

$$\begin{aligned}
2 \cdot \pi \cdot 0.08 \cdot 0.04 \cdot 900 &= 2 \cdot \pi \cdot 2.88 \\
&= 5.76 \cdot \pi \\
&= 18.0956 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$Q_{th} = \frac{18.0956}{60} = 0.3016 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step B — Actual Discharge (Measured):**

$$Q_{act} = \frac{V}{t} = \frac{0.025}{25} = 0.001 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step C — Volumetric Efficiency:**

$$\eta_v = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{0.001}{0.3016} = 0.003316 \approx 0.33\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> The volumetric efficiency here is *absurdly low*, indicating either (i) a heavily worn pump, (ii) reading error, or (iii) — most likely — an inconsistency in unit conversion. In typical healthy pumps, $\eta_v \in [85\%, 95\%]$. *Always sanity-check your numbers!*

**Step D — Manometric Head:**
Neglecting velocity head differences (as is standard in lab problems):

$$H_m = h_d + h_s = 20 + 2 = 22 \text{ m of water}$$

**Step E — Input Power via Rope Brake:**

Effective radius:

$$R_{eff} = (R - r) = \left(\frac{0.30}{2} - \frac{0.015}{2}\right) = (0.15 - 0.0075) = 0.1425 \text{ m}$$

Net tangential force:

$$F_{net} = W - S = 50 - 10 = 40 \text{ N}$$

Torque:

$$T = F_{net} \cdot R_{eff} = 40 \cdot 0.1425 = 5.7 \text{ N·m}$$

Input power:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{input} &= \frac{2 \cdot \pi \cdot N \cdot T}{60} = \frac{2 \cdot \pi \cdot 900 \cdot 5.7}{60} \\
&= \frac{32170.6}{60} = 536.18 \text{ W} = 0.5362 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step F — Shaft Power:**
Assuming mechanical efficiency $\eta_m = 90\%$ (typical assumption if not given):

$$P_{shaft} = \eta_m \cdot P_{input} = 0.90 \cdot 536.18 = 482.56 \text{ W}$$

**Step G — Manometric Efficiency:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{man} &= \frac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q_{act} \cdot H_m}{P_{shaft}} \\
&= \frac{1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.001 \cdot 22}{482.56} \\
&= \frac{215.82}{482.56} \\
&\approx 0.4473 = 44.73\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step H — Output (Hydraulic) Power:**

$$P_{out} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q_{act} \cdot H_m = 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.001 \cdot 22 = 215.82 \text{ W}$$

**Step I — Overall Efficiency:**

$$\eta_o = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{input}} = \frac{215.82}{536.18} \approx 0.4026 = 40.26\%$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Rope-Brake vs Electrical Method

| Aspect | Rope-Brake Dynamometer | Electrical (Ammeter-Voltmeter) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Principle** | Friction torque via rope & weights. | Three-phase electrical power measurement. |
| **Key Inputs** | $W$, $S$, $R$, $r$, $N$. | $V$ (line voltage), $I$ (line current), $\cos\phi$. |
| **Formula** | $P = \frac{2\pi N (W-S)(R-r)}{60}$ | $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$ |
| **Accuracy** | Moderate (rope wear, slip errors). | High (precision electrical instruments). |
| **Heat Dissipation** | Direct (rope gets hot). | Indirect (in motor windings). |
| **Lab Preference** | Mechanical labs (KTU PCMEL407). | Electrical / Advanced labs. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Experimental Test Rig

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the *functional flow* of the gear pump test rig, from electrical supply to data acquisition. This block-level architecture satisfies the syllabus requirement for understanding system interconnections.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Power Supply 415V 3-Phase] --> B[Star Delta Starter]
    B --> C[Three Phase Induction Motor]
    C --> D[Shaft Coupling]
    D --> E[Gear Pump Unit]
    E --> F[Suction Line from Sump Tank]
    E --> G[Vacuum Gauge at Suction]
    E --> H[Delivery Line]
    H --> I[Pressure Gauge at Delivery]
    H --> J[Control Valve Throttle]
    H --> K[Measuring Tank]
    K --> L[Stopwatch Timer]
    E --> M[Brake Drum on Pump Shaft]
    M --> N[Rope Brake Dynamometer]
    N --> O[Dead Weight W]
    N --> P[Spring Balance S]
    M --> Q[Tachometer for RPM]
    L --> R[Data Recording Tabulation]
    Q --> R
    I --> R
    G --> R
    O --> R
    P --> R
    R --> S[Efficiency Calculations]
    S --> T[Performance Graphs Q vs H and Q vs Eta]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Power flows from the **AC supply → starter → motor → coupling → gear pump**.
- The pump draws water from a **sump tank** through the suction line (monitored by a **vacuum gauge**).
- Water exits via the delivery line, monitored by a **pressure gauge** and a **throttle valve**, before being collected in a **measuring tank** for timed volume measurement.
- The **rope brake dynamometer** (with dead weight and spring balance) measures input torque.
- A **tachometer** records shaft speed.
- All readings are tabulated and processed to compute efficiencies and plot performance curves.

## 4.2 Internal Cross-Section Schematic — External Gear Pump

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Suction Port Low Pressure] --> B[Un-meshing Zone]
    B --> C[Fluid Trapped Between Tooth and Casing]
    C --> D[Carry Zone Along Casing Wall]
    D --> E[Re-meshing Zone]
    E --> F[Delivery Port High Pressure]
    G[Driving Gear CW Rotation] --> C
    H[Driven Gear CCW Rotation] --> C
```

**Interpretation:**
- Fluid enters at the **suction port (A)**, where the gear teeth un-mesh, creating a low-pressure pocket.
- The fluid is then **trapped (C)** and **carried (D)** along the casing periphery.
- At the **re-meshing zone (E)**, the fluid is forcibly squeezed out through the **delivery port (F)**.

## 4.3 Performance Characteristic Curves (Conceptual Plot Description)

A standard performance test result yields three characteristic curves:

| Curve | X-Axis | Y-Axis | Shape |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Q vs $H_m$** | Discharge $Q$ (L/s) | Manometric Head $H_m$ (m) | Slight downward slope. |
| **Q vs $\eta_o$** | Discharge $Q$ (L/s) | Overall Efficiency $\eta_o$ (%) | Inverted-U (peaks at design point). |
| **Q vs $P_{input}$** | Discharge $Q$ (L/s) | Input Power $P_{input}$ (kW) | Nearly linear rising. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Lab Record Requirement:** Always plot these three curves on **graph sheet** with proper axis labels, units, and a clear legend. The *operating point* (best efficiency point) is where the efficiency curve peaks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**Define a positive displacement pump. How does a gear pump differ from a centrifugal pump in terms of discharge vs head characteristics?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **positive displacement pump** is one which delivers a *fixed quantity of fluid per revolution*, irrespective of the discharge pressure. In a **gear pump**, fluid is trapped between gear teeth and casing, then carried to the delivery side.

| Feature | Gear Pump (PD) | Centrifugal Pump (Rotodynamic) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Discharge vs Head | $Q$ is nearly *constant*; $H$ varies with load. | $Q$ *decreases* as $H$ increases. |
| Flow Direction | Reversible (by changing motor rotation). | Non-reversible. |
| Self Priming | Yes | No (usually) |

**(1 mark)** Definition of PD pump. **(1 mark)** Gear pump working. **(1 mark)** Comparison table.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**Explain the term "volumetric efficiency" of a gear pump. What are the typical sources of internal leakage?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Volumetric efficiency** ($\eta_v$) is defined as the ratio of *actual discharge* to *theoretical discharge*:

$$\eta_v = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$$

It is expressed as a percentage; a healthy gear pump has $\eta_v \in [85\%, 95\%]$.

**Sources of internal leakage** (1 mark each):
1. **Tip leakage** — clearance between gear tooth tip and casing.
2. **Side-plate leakage** — gap between gear faces and side plates/bear plates.
3. **Meshing-point leakage** — fluid squeezed back through the gear meshing zone.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]`

**Part (a)** — *7 marks*
**With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of an external gear pump. Derive an expression for its theoretical discharge.**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
- Two identical spur gears (driver and driven) mesh inside a closely-fitted casing.
- Driver is coupled to motor shaft; driven rotates due to mesh contact.
- Bearings support shafts; side-plates confine gears axially.
- Casing has suction port and delivery port on opposite sides.

**Working (2 marks):**
At the **suction port**, gear teeth un-mesh creating a low pressure; fluid enters from sump. Fluid is trapped between tooth flank and casing wall, carried around the periphery, then squeezed out at the **delivery port** as gears re-mesh.

**Derivation of Theoretical Discharge (2 marks):**

Let:
- $D$ = pitch circle diameter (m)
- $W$ = axial width of gear (m)
- $N$ = shaft speed in rps

Volume swept per gear per revolution:

$$V_1 = \pi \cdot D \cdot W$$

For two gears rotating simultaneously:

$$V_{total} = 2 \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot W$$

Theoretical discharge at speed $N$ (in rps):

$$Q_{th} = 2 \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot W \cdot N \quad (\text{m}^3/\text{s})$$

If $N$ is in rpm:

$$Q_{th} = \frac{2 \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot W \cdot N}{60}$$

---

**Part (b)** — *7 marks*
**During a performance test on a gear pump, the following observations were recorded:**

| Observation | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Pump speed | 1440 rpm |
| Pitch diameter of gear | 100 mm |
| Gear width | 50 mm |
| Actual discharge | 0.012 m³/s |
| Delivery pressure head | 30 m of water |
| Suction vacuum head | 3 m of water |
| Input power (rope brake) | 4.5 kW |
| Dead weight on brake | 200 N |
| Spring balance reading | 50 N |
| Brake drum diameter | 40 cm |
| Rope diameter | 2 cm |

**Calculate:**
(i) Theoretical discharge
(ii) Volumetric efficiency
(iii) Manometric efficiency
(iv) Overall efficiency

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Theoretical Discharge (2 marks):**

$$Q_{th} = \frac{2 \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot W \cdot N}{60}$$

$$Q_{th} = \frac{2 \cdot \pi \cdot 0.10 \cdot 0.05 \cdot 1440}{60}$$

$$Q_{th} = \frac{45.24}{60} = 0.754 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**(ii) Volumetric Efficiency (1 mark):**

$$\eta_v = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{0.012}{0.754} = 0.0159 = 1.59\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examination Pitfall:** Such low $\eta_v$ indicates a *severely worn pump* or measurement unit error. Always cross-check units (m³/s vs L/s, mm vs m) before computing.

**(iii) Manometric Head and Efficiency (2 marks):**

$$H_m = h_d + h_s = 30 + 3 = 33 \text{ m}$$

Output (hydraulic) power:

$$P_{out} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q_{act} \cdot H_m = 1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.012 \cdot 33 = 3884.76 \text{ W} = 3.885 \text{ kW}$$

Overall efficiency:

$$\eta_o = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{input}} = \frac{3.885}{4.5} = 0.8633 = 86.33\%$$

**(iv) Manometric Efficiency (2 marks):**
For finding $\eta_{man}$, we need shaft power. From the rope brake data, input power is *directly* $P_{input} = 4.5$ kW. Assuming mechanical efficiency is embedded:

Using $\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_{man} \cdot \eta_m$ with assumed $\eta_m = 90\%$:

$$\eta_{man} = \frac{\eta_o}{\eta_v \cdot \eta_m} = \frac{0.8633}{0.0159 \cdot 0.90} = 60.31\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently *forget to convert mm to m* in the discharge formula. Always verify units first! Another common error is omitting the factor of 60 when using rpm — this alone can wipe out 2 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]`

**Part (a)** — *7 marks*
**List the various losses occurring in a gear pump. Explain how each affects the overall efficiency.**

**Model Solution:**

**Three categories of losses (3 categories × ~2 marks each):**

1. **Volumetric Losses (Internal Leakage):**
   Fluid leaks from the high-pressure delivery side back to the low-pressure suction side through clearances (tip leakage, side leakage, meshing-point leakage). This reduces the actual discharge below theoretical, quantified by $\eta_v = Q_{act}/Q_{th}$.

2. **Mechanical Losses (Friction):**
   Friction at bearings, between gear teeth (churning), and at shaft seals dissipates input power before it reaches the fluid. Quantified by $\eta_m = P_{shaft}/P_{input}$.

3. **Hydraulic Losses:**
   Friction in the suction and delivery pipes, eddies, and sudden expansion/contraction losses reduce the effective manometric head. Quantified by $\eta_{man} = (\rho g Q H_m)/P_{shaft}$.

**Overall efficiency** is the product:

$$\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_{man} \cdot \eta_m$$

---

**Part (b)** — *7 marks*
**Describe with a neat sketch the experimental setup used for conducting the performance test on a gear pump in the laboratory. Explain the procedure step-by-step.**

**Model Solution:**

**Experimental Setup (3 marks):**
The setup consists of:
- **Sump tank** — water reservoir at floor level.
- **Gear pump** — mounted on a sturdy base, coupled to a 3-phase induction motor via a flexible coupling.
- **Suction pipe** with a **vacuum gauge** near the pump inlet.
- **Delivery pipe** with a **pressure gauge** and a **throttle/control valve**.
- **Measuring tank** — calibrated, with a stopwatch for timed collection.
- **Rope brake dynamometer** — wraps around a brake drum on the pump shaft; one end carries a **dead weight ($W$)**, the other a **spring balance ($S$)**.
- **Tachometer** — digital or mechanical, to measure shaft rpm.
- **Star-delta starter** — for safe motor startup.

**Procedure (4 marks):**

1. Check water level in sump tank; ensure pump casing is primed (filled with water).
2. Start the motor via the star-delta starter; allow water to circulate; bleed any trapped air through air vent.
3. Set the desired load by adjusting the throttle valve on the delivery side.
4. Note the readings: vacuum gauge ($h_s$), pressure gauge ($h_d$), tachometer ($N$), spring balance ($S$).
5. Using the measuring tank, collect water for a fixed time interval ($t$) and note the volume ($V$).
6. Repeat steps 3–5 for 5–6 different throttle positions, covering full range from minimum to maximum discharge.
7. Tabulate all readings; compute $Q_{act}$, $H_m$, $P_{input}$, $\eta_v$, $\eta_{man}$, $\eta_o$.
8. Plot performance curves: $Q$ vs $H_m$, $Q$ vs $\eta_o$, $Q$ vs $P_{input}$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes (as observed in KTU Valuation):**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors** — Forgetting to convert mm to m, or rpm to rps (single biggest source of numerical error).
> 2. **Ignoring the 60** — When $N$ is given in rpm, the formula must divide by 60. Examiners deduct 2 marks if omitted.
> 3. **Confusing $H_m$ with $h_d$** — Manometric head *includes* both delivery and suction heads. Forgetting $h_s$ loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Wrong Power Conversion** — Forgetting to convert W to kW (÷1000) in efficiency calculations.
> 5. **No Sketch in Constructional Questions** — Always include a labelled diagram; missing sketch loses 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Mixing up $\eta_v$, $\eta_m$, $\eta_{man}$** — Each efficiency has a specific definition. Memorise the formula triangle.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Gear Pump** = Rotary Positive Displacement Pump using *two meshing gears* in a close-fitting casing.
- ✅ **Working Principle** = *Un-mesh (suction) → Carry (transfer) → Re-mesh (discharge)*.
- ✅ **Theoretical Discharge Formula:** $Q_{th} = \frac{2 \pi D W N}{60}$ (with $N$ in rpm, $D,W$ in m).
- ✅ **Actual Discharge** = $V/t$ from measuring tank.
- ✅ **Volumetric Efficiency** = $Q_{act}/Q_{th}$ — indicates leakage health.
- ✅ **Three Efficiencies:** $\eta_v$ (volumetric), $\eta_{man}$ (hydraulic/manometric), $\eta_m$ (mechanical).
- ✅ **Overall Efficiency** = $\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_{man} \cdot \eta_m = P_{out}/P_{input}$.
- ✅ **Rope Brake Power:** $P = \frac{2\pi N (W-S)(R-r)}{60}$ — Net load = $W-S$, Effective radius = $R-r$.
- ✅ **Electrical Power (3-phase):** $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$.
- ✅ **Manometric Head:** $H_m = h_d + h_s + (V_d^2 - V_s^2)/2g \approx h_d + h_s$.
- ✅ **Output Power:** $P_{out} = \rho g Q H_m$ (in Watts).
- ✅ **Performance Curves:** Plot $Q$ vs $H_m$, $Q$ vs $\eta_o$, $Q$ vs $P_{input}$.
- ✅ **Bidirectional Operation:** A gear pump can reverse flow by reversing motor direction (advantage over centrifugal pumps).
- ✅ **Suitable for viscous fluids:** Lubricating oils, hydraulic fluids — common in machine tool industries.
- ✅ **Always prime** the pump before starting to avoid cavitation damage.
- ✅ **Use SI units consistently** (m, s, kg, W) for full marks in KTU numericals.
- ✅ **Typical Lab Values:** $\eta_v \in [85\%, 95\%]$, $\eta_o \in [70\%, 85\%]$ for healthy pumps.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Test on Pelton Turbine and Determination of Operating Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMEL407 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Performance Test on Pelton Turbine and Determination of Operating Characteristics ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pelton Turbine (Pelton Wheel)** is an **impulse-type hydraulic turbine** in which the pressure energy of water is first converted into kinetic energy by a nozzle, and this high-velocity jet is then allowed to strike a series of buckets (splitter blades) mounted on the periphery of a runner. The resulting change in momentum of the jet produces a mechanical torque on the runner shaft. **Standard Nomenclature Used by KTU Examiners:** | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Impulse Turbine** | Energy conversion happens in air — no pressure drop across runner | | **Bucket / Vane** | Double-cup shaped splitter blade (splits jet into two halves) | | **Runner** | Rotating disc carrying the buckets | | **Jet Ratio (m)** | Ratio of bucket circumferential pitch to jet diameter (m = B/d) | | **Specific Speed (Ns)** | Speed of a geometrically similar turbine producing **1 kW** under **1 m head** | > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition):** Imagine you are standing in a heavy rainstorm holding an umbrella. If you tilt the umbrella slightly and gently tap the spokes so it spins, the raindrops hitting the curved ribs push the umbrella around. A **Pelton turbine works exactly the same way**: the high-pressure water supply is funneled through a nozzle (the "rainmaker"), producing a thin, pencil-like jet that strikes specially shaped double-cup buckets. The buckets split the jet, reverse its direction by nearly **180°**, and harvest the change in momentum as rotational torque. Because the runner spins in air (not submerged), the water pressure does not drop across the runner — it is purely an **impulse** machine. ### Why Pelton Turbine is Used for High Heads Pelton wheels are preferred when the available head is very high (typically **H > 300 m**) and the discharge is comparatively low. The jet is concentrated and travels at a very high velocity, so even a small flow rate can deliver enormous power. $$ P = \rho \, g \, Q \, H \quad \text{(gross water power in Watts)} $$ > [!TIP] > **Syllabus Highlight:** In the KTU lab, the Pelton wheel is coupled to a **centrifugal pump** (acting as a turbine in reverse) or to an **electrical loading dynamometer / rope-brake drum**. You are expected to record **head (H), discharge (Q), speed (N), and brake load (W)**, and then plot the **constant-head operating characteristic curves**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic The Pelton wheel converts hydraulic energy into mechanical rotation through the following sequential steps: 1. **Pressure → Kinetic Conversion:** Water stored at a high head is piped through a long penstock. At the end of the penstock, a **nozzle** converts all the pressure head $(H)$ into velocity head, producing a free jet of velocity $V_1$. 2. **Jet Impingement:** The jet strikes the splitter buckets mounted on the runner periphery. Each bucket has a central splitter ridge that divides the jet into two equal halves that flow along the inner curved surfaces. 3. **Momentum Reversal:** Because the bucket is double-cup shaped, the relative velocity of water leaving the bucket is directed almost exactly opposite to the jet direction (the deflection angle $\beta$ is typically close to 165°–170°). 4. **Torque Generation:** The change in tangential momentum of water = tangential force on bucket × time. This tangential force × bucket-radius gives the **shaft torque**. 5. **Runaway Condition:** If the load on the turbine is removed, the bucket speed rises until $u = V_1/2$, at which point the relative velocity at entry is zero and no work is done — this is the **runaway speed**. ### 2.2 Velocity Triangles (Inlet & Outlet) Because Pelton buckets are symmetrical about the splitter ridge, the velocity triangles are drawn only for **one half of the jet**, and outlet velocity triangles are mirror images of the inlet triangles. - Absolute velocity of jet = $V_1$ (purely tangential, same direction as bucket motion at the point of contact) - Bucket velocity (tangential) = $u = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ - Relative velocity at inlet = $V_{r1} = V_1 - u$ (since the jet hits the bucket tangentially) - The bucket then deflects the jet through angle $\beta$, and water leaves with relative velocity $V_{r2} = k \, V_{r1}$, where $k$ is the **velocity coefficient** of the bucket (typically 0.85–0.98; KTU assumes **k = 0.9 to 0.95** for new buckets). - Whirl component at outlet = $V_{w2} = u - V_{r2} \cos\beta$ ### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations before any calculation. KTU examiners test them directly.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Jet velocity | $V_1 = C_v \sqrt{2gH}$ | m/s | | 2 | Bucket velocity | $u = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | m/s | | 3 | Speed ratio (optimum) | $K_u = \dfrac{u}{V_1} \approx 0.45 \text{ to } 0.48$ | — | | 4 | Flow ratio | $K_f = \dfrac{Q}{D^2 \, N}$ | — | | 5 | Work done per unit weight of water | $W = \dfrac{u}{g}(V_1 - u)(1 + k\cos\beta)$ | m | | 6 | Hydraulic efficiency | $\eta_h = \dfrac{2u(V_1 - u)(1 + k\cos\beta)}{V_1^2}$ | — | | 7 | Condition for max $\eta_h$ | $u = \dfrac{V_1}{2}$ | — | | 8 | Maximum hydraulic efficiency | $\eta_{h,max} = \dfrac{1 + k\cos\beta}{2}$ | — | | 9 | Mechanical efficiency | $\eta_m = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{\rho g Q H}$ | — | | 10 | Overall efficiency | $\eta_o = \dfrac{\text{Shaft Power}}{\rho g Q H} = \eta_h \times \eta_m$ | — | | 11 | Specific speed | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ | rpm (metric) | | 12 | Unit speed | $N_1 = \dfrac{N}{\sqrt{H}}$ | rpm | | 13 | Unit discharge | $Q_1 = \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{H}}$ | m³/s | | 14 | Unit power | $P_1 = \dfrac{P}{H^{3/2}}$ | kW | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Note on $\cos\beta$ sign:** In Indian textbooks (Rajput, Modi, KTU preferred style), the outlet relative velocity makes angle $\beta$ with the **negative direction of bucket motion**. With $\beta$ measured from the *reverse tangent*, the whirl at outlet is $V_{w2} = u - V_{r2}\cos\beta$. Many foreign textbooks use the opposite sign convention; **stick to the KTU/Kerala style** to avoid losing 2 marks. ### 2.4 Operating Characteristics — What is Plotted? The **constant-head characteristic curves** of a Pelton wheel describe how its performance varies with load at a fixed net head. The standard four plots are: 1. **Efficiency vs Speed** ($\eta$ vs $N$) — dome-shaped, peak at design load 2. **Efficiency vs Output (Power)** ($\eta$ vs $P$) — used to locate best-efficiency point (BEP) 3. **Speed vs Output** ($N$ vs $P$) — gentle slope for Pelton (steeper for reaction) 4. **Discharge vs Output** ($Q$ vs $P$) — Q increases approximately linearly with $P$ **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Operating characteristic curves are essential for selecting the **best operating point** when a turbine is coupled to an electrical grid. Grid-connected Pelton turbines must operate at the BEP to maximize annual energy yield, while isolated micro-hydro units use the same curves to size the **electronic load controller (ELC)**. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Work Done per Unit Weight (Euler Turbine Equation Applied to Pelton Wheel) We apply the **Euler momentum equation** to the control volume of water striking and leaving one bucket. For a Pelton wheel, the work done per unit weight of water striking the runner is: $$ W = \frac{1}{g}\left( V_{w1} \pm V_{w2} \right) u $$ **Step 1 — Identify the whirl velocities.** The jet strikes the bucket tangentially. The whirl component of absolute velocity at **inlet** equals the jet velocity, $V_{w1} = V_1$ (since the jet is purely tangential and in the direction of bucket motion). **Step 2 — Apply the splitter symmetry.** Because the bucket is symmetrical about its central ridge, the relative velocity at exit $V_{r2}$ makes the same angle $\beta$ with the reverse tangential direction. Therefore: $$ V_{w2} = u - V_{r2}\cos\beta $$ where $V_{r2} = k \, V_{r1}$. **Step 3 — Find relative velocity at inlet.** At inlet, by simple vector subtraction (jet tangential, bucket tangential): $$ V_{r1} = V_1 - u $$ **Step 4 — Substitute into Euler's equation.** $$ W = \frac{u}{g}\left( V_1 - V_{w2} \right) = \frac{u}{g}\left[ V_1 - \left( u - V_{r2}\cos\beta \right) \right] $$ $$ W = \frac{u}{g}\left[ V_1 - u + k(V_1 - u)\cos\beta \right] $$ $$ \boxed{\,W = \frac{u}{g}(V_1 - u)\left(1 + k\cos\beta\right)\,} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** This is the energy transferred to each kg of water. The factor $(1 + k\cos\beta)$ accounts for the partial recovery of kinetic energy at exit. Ideally, $k = 1$ and $\beta = 180°$, giving $1 + \cos 180° = 0$, meaning **perfect exit guidance** (no kinetic energy wasted) — this is the **ideal Pelton bucket**. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Hydraulic Efficiency The kinetic energy supplied to the water per unit weight by the nozzle is the velocity head: $$ \text{Kinetic energy per unit weight} = \frac{V_1^2}{2g} $$ Hydraulic efficiency is defined as: $$ \eta_h = \frac{\text{Work done per unit weight}}{\text{Kinetic energy supplied per unit weight}} $$ $$ \eta_h = \frac{W}{V_1^2 / 2g} = \frac{\dfrac{u}{g}(V_1 - u)(1 + k\cos\beta)}{V_1^2 / 2g} $$ $$ \boxed{\,\eta_h = \frac{2u(V_1 - u)(1 + k\cos\beta)}{V_1^2}\,} $$ --- ### 3.3 Condition for Maximum Hydraulic Efficiency Treating $\eta_h$ as a function of bucket velocity $u$ (with $V_1$, $k$, and $\beta$ as constants), differentiate and set the derivative to zero: $$ \frac{d\eta_h}{du} = \frac{2(1 + k\cos\beta)}{V_1^2}\left[ (V_1 - u) - u \right] = 0 $$ $$ (V_1 - u) - u = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad V_1 = 2u $$ $$ \boxed{\,u = \frac{V_1}{2}\,} $$ **Therefore, the speed ratio for maximum hydraulic efficiency is:** $$ K_u = \frac{u}{V_1} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5 $$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Trap:** The theoretical value $K_u = 0.5$ applies only to a **frictionless single-jet bucket with $k = 1$**. In real Pelton buckets with friction ($k < 1$) and finite splitter ridges, the optimum $K_u$ drops to about **0.45 – 0.48**. KTU numerical problems often ask you to derive $K_u$ from given data — always show the working. --- ### 3.4 Maximum Hydraulic Efficiency Substituting $u = V_1/2$ back into the $\eta_h$ equation: $$ \eta_{h,max} = \frac{2 \cdot \dfrac{V_1}{2} \cdot \left(V_1 - \dfrac{V_1}{2}\right)(1 + k\cos\beta)}{V_1^2} $$ $$ \eta_{h,max} = \frac{2 \cdot \dfrac{V_1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{V_1}{2}(1 + k\cos\beta)}{V_1^2} $$ $$ \boxed{\,\eta_{h,max} = \frac{1 + k\cos\beta}{2}\,} $$ For a typical bucket with $k = 0.9$ and $\beta = 165°$: $$ \eta_{h,max} = \frac{1 + 0.9 \cos 165°}{2} = \frac{1 + 0.9(-0.9659)}{2} = \frac{1 - 0.869}{2} \approx 0.0654 $$ This is low because $\beta$ is close to 180°; in practice, water leaves the bucket with some residual swirl which carries energy downstream. > [!NOTE] > **Practical implication:** Modern Pelton buckets with carefully shaped exit lips can achieve $\beta \approx 170°$–$175°$, giving hydraulic efficiencies in the range of 88%–92%. KTU problems often quote **$\eta_h$ from 85% to 92%** for a well-designed bucket. --- ### 3.5 Brake Power, Shaft Power, and Overall Efficiency In the lab, the turbine is loaded through a **rope-brake drum** (or electrical dynamometer). The following definitions are used: $$ \text{Brake Power (BP)} = \frac{2\pi N T}{60} = \frac{2\pi N (W - S) R}{60} $$ where: - $W$ = spring-balance reading on the tight side (kgf or N) - $S$ = spring-balance reading on the slack side (kgf or N) - $R$ = effective brake-drum radius (m) - $T = (W - S) R$ = net torque (N·m) - $N$ = runner speed (rpm) $$ \text{Water Power (WP)} = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times Q \times H \text{ (in Watts)} $$ $$ \boxed{\,\eta_o = \frac{BP}{WP} = \frac{2\pi N (W - S) R}{60 \times \rho g Q H}\,} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Practical Note:** When using **kgf** on the spring balance, multiply by $g = 9.81$ to convert to Newtons. Many students forget this and lose 2 marks. --- ### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — How Each Variable Affects Pelton Performance | Variable Change | Effect on $V_1$ | Effect on $u$ | Effect on $\eta_h$ | Effect on Power | |---|---|---|---|---| | Head ↑ | ↑ (as $\sqrt{H}$) | ↑ (free-shaft rises) | Optimal at $u = V_1/2$ | ↑↑ | | Nozzle area ↑ | No change | No change | No change | ↑ (Q ↑) | | Speed ↑ (load ↑) | No change | ↑ | Falls on either side of optimum | Increases to a max, then falls | | Load ↑ (constant H) | No change | Slight ↑ | Dome curve | See characteristic plot | This analytical matrix is the foundation of interpreting the **operating characteristic curves** drawn after the experiment. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram of Pelton Turbine Test Rig The KTU lab test rig couples a Pelton wheel to a centrifugal pump (used in reverse) and a rope-brake loading system. The block diagram below illustrates the energy flow and measurement points. ```mermaid flowchart LR sumpA[("Sump / Storage Tank")] --> pumpA["Centrifugal Pump"] pumpA --> pipeA["Delivery Pipe + By-pass Valve"] pipeA --> nozzleA["Pressure Gauge Node"] nozzleA --> nozzleB["Nozzle Assembly"] nozzleB --> jetA["High Velocity Jet"] jetA --> runnerA["Pelton Runner with Buckets"] runnerA --> shaftA["Coupled Shaft"] shaftA --> brakeA["Rope Brake Drum"] brakeA --> loadA["Spring Balance W"] brakeA --> loadB["Spring Balance S"] shaftA --> tachoA["Tachometer / Speed Sensor"] nozzleA -.->|"H (manometer)"| headA["Head Measurement"] pipeA -.->|"Q (measuring tank)"| flowA["Discharge Measurement"] classDef meas fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef machine fill:#b8e0b8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef storage fill:#a0c4e0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class sumpA storage class pumpA,runnerA,brakeA machine class nozzleA,tachoA,headA,flowA,loadA,loadB meas ``` ### 4.2 Schematic — Measurement Layout in Side View The same rig is shown in elevation view to clarify how the **head (H)** is measured between the sump water surface and the nozzle centerline. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Reservoir sump[("Sump / Tank
Water Surface Datum")] end sump -->|"Suction Pipe"| P1["Centrifugal Pump"] P1 -->|"Delivery + Pressure Gauge"| V1["By-pass Valve"] V1 -->|"Delivery Pipe with calibrated orifice"| N1["Nozzle Tip
Centre-line Datum"] N1 -->|"Free Jet"| R1["Pelton Runner"] R1 -->|"Shaft Coupling"| B1["Brake Drum + Rope"] B1 -->|"Tight Side"| SW["Spring Balance W"] B1 -->|"Slack Side"| SS["Spring Balance S"] R1 -->|"Tail race pipe"| sump P1 -.->|"Tachometer reads speed N"| T1["Digital Tacho"] V1 -.->|"Manometer reads H"| H1["U-tube Manometer
Pressure Head h"] V1 -.->|"Orifice reads Q"| Q1["Collecting Tank
Volumetric Method"] classDef gaugeNode fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef machineNode fill:#c8e6c8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef tankNode fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class T1,H1,Q1,SW,SS gaugeNode class P1,V1,R1,B1 machineNode class sump tankNode ``` ### 4.3 Operating Characteristic Curves — Expected Shapes After the experiment, four curves are drawn at constant net head. The block below describes each curve's trend (use a sheet of graph paper to plot in the lab record). ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Trend_Block A1["Efficiency vs Speed
Dome curve peaking at design N"] A2["Efficiency vs Output Power
Dome curve - locate BEP"] A3["Speed vs Output Power
Slight negative slope (Pelton flat)"] A4["Discharge vs Output Power
Approximately linear through origin"] end A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 A1 -.- BEP(("Best Efficiency Point
Design Load")) A2 -.- BEP ``` > [!TIP] > **Lab Procedure Outline (for your record):** > 1. Start the centrifugal pump and maintain **constant head** by adjusting the by-pass valve. > 2. Allow water to flow through the nozzle to the Pelton runner; note the **no-load speed** (runaway condition). > 3. Apply load gradually using the rope-brake hand-wheel; record at **6 to 8 load settings**. > 4. For each load setting, record: $W$, $S$, $N$, $Q$ (collecting-tank method), $H$ (manometer). > 5. Calculate BP, WP, $\eta_o$ and plot the four characteristic curves. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** *State the working principle of a Pelton turbine with a neat sketch. Why is it classified as an impulse turbine?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A Pelton turbine consists of a runner fitted with double-cup shaped buckets mounted on its periphery. Water from a high-head reservoir is fed through a nozzle that converts the entire pressure head into a high-velocity jet. This jet strikes the buckets, splits into two halves, and reverses its direction by nearly 180°. The resulting change in momentum of water exerts a tangential force on the buckets, producing a torque that rotates the runner. It is classified as an **impulse turbine** because the entire pressure energy is converted into kinetic energy in the nozzle **before** the water strikes the runner, and the pressure of water remains **atmospheric (constant)** as it flows over the buckets. **(3 marks)** --- **Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** *Define specific speed of a turbine and write its significance. What is the typical range of specific speed for a Pelton turbine?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition:** The specific speed $N_s$ of a turbine is the speed at which a geometrically similar turbine would run if it were so reduced in size that it would develop **unit power (1 kW)** under a **unit head (1 m)**. $$ N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}} \quad \text{(rpm, metric units)} $$ **Significance:** It is used to select the **type of turbine** (Pelton, Francis, Kaplan) best suited for a given head and power combination. High specific speeds → axial-flow machines; low specific speeds → impulse machines. **Range for Pelton Turbine:** $N_s \approx 10$ to $35$ (single jet) and up to $\approx 70$ for multi-jet Pelton wheels. **(3 marks)** --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, you must attempt **ONE question** out of two. Each question carries two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`** **(a)** With a neat sketch, derive an expression for the **work done per unit weight of water** by a single jet Pelton wheel. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A Pelton wheel has a mean bucket diameter of **1.2 m** and runs at **500 rpm**. The jet diameter is **100 mm** and the head at the nozzle is **400 m**. If the velocity coefficient of the nozzle is **0.97** and the bucket deflection angle is **165°**, determine: (i) the power developed; (ii) the hydraulic efficiency. Take $k = 0.9$. **(7 marks)** --- ##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Diagram (2 marks):** Draw the Pelton wheel showing the jet striking the bucket, splitter ridge, inlet and outlet velocity triangles. **Derivation (5 marks):** Let $V_1$ = velocity of jet, $u$ = bucket velocity, $V_{r1}$ = relative velocity at inlet. Since the jet strikes the bucket tangentially: $$ V_{r1} = V_1 - u $$ The water is deflected through angle $\beta$ inside the bucket, so the relative velocity at exit: $$ V_{r2} = k \, V_{r1} = k(V_1 - u) $$ Whirl component of velocity at outlet (KTU sign convention): $$ V_{w2} = u - V_{r2}\cos\beta = u - k(V_1 - u)\cos\beta $$ By Euler's momentum equation, work done per unit weight of water: $$ W = \frac{u}{g}\left( V_{w1} - V_{w2} \right) = \frac{u}{g}\left[ V_1 - u + k(V_1 - u)\cos\beta \right] $$ $$ \boxed{\,W = \frac{u}{g}(V_1 - u)\left(1 + k\cos\beta\right)\,} $$ --- ##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Given:** $D = 1.2$ m, $N = 500$ rpm, $d = 100$ mm = 0.1 m, $H = 400$ m, $C_v = 0.97$, $\beta = 165°$, $k = 0.9$. **Step 1 — Jet velocity.** $$ V_1 = C_v\sqrt{2gH} = 0.97 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 400} $$ $$ V_1 = 0.97 \times \sqrt{7848} = 0.97 \times 88.59 = 85.93 \text{ m/s} $$ **Step 2 — Bucket velocity.** $$ u = \frac{\pi D N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 1.2 \times 500}{60} = \frac{1884.96}{60} = 31.42 \text{ m/s} $$ **Step 3 — Work done per unit weight.** $$ W = \frac{u}{g}(V_1 - u)(1 + k\cos\beta) $$ Compute $\cos 165° = -0.9659$. So $1 + k\cos\beta = 1 + 0.9(-0.9659) = 1 - 0.8693 = 0.1307$. $$ W = \frac{31.42}{9.81} \times (85.93 - 31.42) \times 0.1307 $$ $$ W = 3.203 \times 54.51 \times 0.1307 = 22.82 \text{ m·kgf/kg} $$ **Step 4 — Discharge through the nozzle.** $$ Q = C_d \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot V_1 = 1.0 \times \frac{\pi}{4}(0.1)^2 \times 85.93 $$ $$ Q = 0.007854 \times 85.93 = 0.6749 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} $$ **Step 5 — Power developed.** $$ P = \rho g Q W = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.6749 \times 22.82 $$ $$ P = 151{,}061 \text{ W} \approx 151.06 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 6 — Hydraulic efficiency.** $$ \eta_h = \frac{W}{H} = \frac{22.82 \times g}{V_1^2/2} $$ Or directly: $$ \eta_h = \frac{2u(V_1 - u)(1 + k\cos\beta)}{V_1^2} = \frac{2 \times 31.42 \times 54.51 \times 0.1307}{(85.93)^2} $$ $$ \eta_h = \frac{447.5}{7384.4} = 0.0606 $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall (Part B - 14 marks):** > 1. **Unit conversion (–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert $W$ (work per unit weight in metres) back to the $\eta_h$ equation correctly. Always cross-check using $\eta_h = W \cdot g / V_1^2 \times 2$ or recompute via the direct formula. > 2. **Sign of $\cos\beta$ (–2 marks):** With $\beta = 165° > 90°$, $\cos\beta$ is **negative**. Writing $1 + k\cos\beta$ instead of $1 - k\cos\beta$ gives the wrong sign. > 3. **Discharge coefficient (–1 mark):** KTU takes $C_d = 1$ for a clean nozzle unless otherwise stated. > 4. **Final units (–1 mark):** Report power in **kW**, not watts, to match the standard KTU answer key. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`** **(a)** Explain the **operating characteristics of a Pelton wheel** with neat sketches. Indicate the best efficiency point on the curves. **(7 marks)** **(b)** In a Pelton wheel installation, the following observations were recorded at a constant head: **(7 marks)** | Trial | Speed $N$ (rpm) | Net Head $H$ (m) | Discharge $Q$ (m³/s) | Shaft Power $P$ (kW) | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 450 | 200 | 0.20 | 360 | | 2 | 480 | 200 | 0.22 | 400 | | 3 | 500 | 200 | 0.24 | 425 | | 4 | 510 | 200 | 0.26 | 430 | | 5 | 520 | 200 | 0.28 | 415 | Plot the characteristic curves and determine the **best efficiency operating point**. --- ##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] The **operating characteristics** of a Pelton wheel are the four curves drawn at **constant net head** $H$: 1. **$\eta_o$ vs Speed ($N$):** A dome-shaped curve. $\eta_o$ rises with speed, reaches a maximum at the design speed, and falls on either side. The peak corresponds to the speed at which $u/V_1 = 0.46$. 2. **$\eta_o$ vs Power ($P$):** Another dome-shaped curve, peaking at the **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)**. 3. **Speed vs Power:** Almost flat with a slight negative slope (unlike a Francis turbine which drops sharply). This is why Pelton wheels can be operated over a wide load range. 4. **Discharge vs Power:** Almost linear through the origin. **Best Efficiency Point (BEP):** Marked at the peak of the $\eta_o$–$P$ curve. From Trial 4 in the table, this corresponds to $P = 430$ kW at $\eta_o \approx 0.91$. **(7 marks)** --- ##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Step 1 — Compute overall efficiency for each trial.** $$ \eta_o = \frac{P \times 1000}{\rho g Q H} $$ **Trial 1:** $$ \eta_1 = \frac{360{,}000}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.20 \times 200} = \frac{360{,}000}{392{,}400} = 0.917 $$ **Trial 2:** $$ \eta_2 = \frac{400{,}000}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.22 \times 200} = \frac{400{,}000}{431{,}640} = 0.927 $$ **Trial 3:** $$ \eta_3 = \frac{425{,}000}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.24 \times 200} = \frac{425{,}000}{470{,}880} = 0.903 $$ **Trial 4:** $$ \eta_4 = \frac{430{,}000}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.26 \times 200} = \frac{430{,}000}{510{,}120} = 0.843 $$ **Trial 5:** $$ \eta_5 = \frac{415{,}000}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.28 \times 200} = \frac{415{,}000}{549{,}360} = 0.756 $$ **Step 2 — Tabulate results.** | Trial | $N$ (rpm) | $P$ (kW) | $Q$ (m³/s) | $\eta_o$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 450 | 360 | 0.20 | 0.917 | | 2 | 480 | 400 | 0.22 | 0.927 | | 3 | 500 | 425 | 0.24 | 0.903 | | 4 | 510 | 430 | 0.26 | 0.843 | | 5 | 520 | 415 | 0.28 | 0.756 | **Step 3 — Plot curves.** - $X$-axis: Power $P$ (kW); $Y_1$-axis: $\eta_o$ → dome curve peaking at **Trial 2 ($P = 400$ kW, $\eta_o = 92.7\%$ )**. - $X$-axis: $P$ (kW); $Y_2$-axis: $N$ (rpm) → nearly flat with slight positive slope. - $X$-axis: $P$ (kW); $Y_3$-axis: $Q$ (m³/s) → linear. **Step 4 — Best Efficiency Point (BEP).** $$ \boxed{\,\text{BEP: } P = 400 \text{ kW at } N = 480 \text{ rpm}, \eta_o = 92.7\%\,} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall (Part B - 14 marks):** > 1. **Mixing units (–2 marks):** Power input to $\eta$ formula is in **Watts**, not kW. Multiply kW by 1000. > 2. **Forgetting to plot $Q$ vs $P$ (–1 mark):** All four characteristic curves must appear for full marks. > 3. **Reading BEP from wrong curve (–1 mark):** BEP is read from $\eta_o$ vs $P$, not from $\eta_o$ vs $N$. Both give the same physical point, but KTU expects the answer on the power curve. > 4. **Constant-head assumption (–1 mark):** If $H$ is not constant in the dataset, the curves are *not* true operating characteristics — KTU explicitly tests this in the viva. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read this the night before the exam:** - **Pelton turbine = impulse machine**, pressure of water across the runner = atmospheric. - **Working head range:** typically 300 m to 1800 m (very high heads). - **Bucket shape:** double-cup with central splitter ridge; deflects jet by **165°–170°**. - **Optimum speed ratio:** $K_u = u/V_1 \approx 0.46$ for real buckets, **0.50** for ideal frictionless bucket. - **Optimum flow ratio:** $K_f = Q/(D^2 \sqrt{H})$ is constant for geometrically similar machines. - **Runaway speed:** bucket speed when load is zero, $u \approx V_1/2$. - **Specific speed range:** $N_s = 10$ – $35$ for single-jet, up to 70 for multi-jet Pelton. - **Governing equation:** $$ W = \frac{u}{g}(V_1 - u)(1 + k\cos\beta) $$ - **Hydraulic efficiency:** $$ \eta_h = \frac{2u(V_1 - u)(1 + k\cos\beta)}{V_1^2} $$ - **Condition for max $\eta_h$:** $u = V_1/2$, giving $\eta_{h,max} = (1 + k\cos\beta)/2$. - **Overall efficiency:** $$ \eta_o = \frac{2\pi N (W - S) R}{60 \, \rho \, g \, Q \, H} $$ - **Four characteristic curves:** $\eta$ vs $N$, $\eta$ vs $P$, $N$ vs $P$, $Q$ vs $P$ — all at **constant head**. - **Best Efficiency Point (BEP):** peak of the $\eta$–$P$ dome curve. - **Unit quantities:** $N_1 = N/\sqrt{H}$, $Q_1 = Q/\sqrt{H}$, $P_1 = P/H^{3/2}$ — used to compare performance across different heads. - **Lab procedure constants:** maintain constant head via by-pass valve, vary load via rope-brake hand-wheel, record 6–8 readings, calculate BP, WP, $\eta_o$. - **Common unit conversion:** $1$ kgf = $9.81$ N; $1$ kW = $1000$ W; $H$ in metres gives WP in Watts when $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ and $g = 9.81$ m/s². - **Sign convention trap:** With KTU/Indian convention, $\beta > 90° \Rightarrow \cos\beta < 0$. Always double-check.

topics.title: Performance Test on Reaction Turbines (Francis and Kaplan Turbines) and Determination of Operating Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL407 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performance Test on Reaction Turbines (Francis and Kaplan Turbines) and Determination of Operating Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Reaction Turbines — Formal Definition

A **reaction turbine** is a hydraulic prime mover in which the runner blades are completely enclosed in a casing filled with flowing water, and the water pressure changes progressively as it flows through the runner. The hydraulic energy (both pressure energy and kinetic energy) of water is converted into mechanical work *partly* in the guide vane mechanism and *partly* in the runner itself. Because the runner operates fully submerged and the water leaves the runner with a residual pressure greater than atmospheric, the fluid is said to *react* against the curved blades — hence the name.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Standard):**
> A reaction turbine is one in which the fluid undergoes a change of pressure as it flows through the runner, and the energy conversion is distributed over the entire flow path — including the spiral casing, guide vanes, runner, and draft tube.

## 1.2 Classification of Reaction Turbines

| Parameter | Francis Turbine | Kaplan Turbine |
|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Mixed flow (radial → axial) | Axial flow (propeller) |
| **Head Range (m)** | **30 m to 600 m** | **2 m to 70 m** |
| **Specific Speed (Ns)** | 60 – 450 | 300 – 1500 |
| **Runner Blades** | Fixed (non-adjustable) | Adjustable (pitch can be changed) |
| **Guide Vanes** | Adjustable | Adjustable |
| **Flow Direction** | Radial inward → Axial | Axial (parallel to shaft) |
| **Specific Speed Classification** | Medium speed | High speed |
| **Draft Tube** | Always provided | Always provided |
| **Typical Application** | Hydroelectric dams, medium-high heads | Low-head dams, run-of-river projects |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sneak Peek:** The Francis turbine is named after engineer **James B. Francis** (USA, 1849), and the Kaplan turbine is named after Austrian engineer **Viktor Kaplan** (1913), who patented the axial-flow reaction runner with adjustable blades.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Water Wheel of the Modern Age

Imagine a giant indoor **rotating door** in a building. When people (water) push against it:
- The **guide vanes** act like the *usher* directing people at the right angle onto the door — controlling how much flow enters and at what angle.
- The **runner blades** are the door itself — shaped to grab people and turn them around, extracting their kinetic energy and even their push (pressure).
- The **draft tube** is the *exit corridor* after the door — it slows people down gradually so they don't crash into the wall (avoiding pressure loss at exit).

> [!TIP]
> **Simple Intuition for Francis vs Kaplan:**
> - **Francis** is like a *ceiling fan seen from the side* — water enters radially (from the side) and exits axially (downward).
> - **Kaplan** is like a *submerged ship propeller* — water enters axially and exits axially, all in the direction of the shaft.

## 1.4 Need for Performance Testing

A turbine installed at a hydroelectric station must be tested in the laboratory under controlled conditions to:

1. **Validate design predictions** — confirm whether the manufacturer’s guaranteed efficiency, power, and discharge values are met.
2. **Determine operating characteristics** — generate graphical curves that allow the operator to choose optimal load conditions.
3. **Verify the best efficiency point (BEP)** — identify the gate opening at which the turbine operates most economically.
4. **Allow scale-up** — the dimensionless constants (unit speed, unit discharge, unit power) obtained from the model test can predict the prototype performance at site heads and discharges.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Proving of Turbines:** The standard terminology used by BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards, IS: 11174) and IEC 60193 for the standardized acceptance test of a hydraulic turbine is called **"Proving of Turbines."** All results are plotted against a common reference: the **unit head** of **1 meter**.

## 1.5 Concept of Unit Quantities

Because model and prototype operate at different heads, we cannot directly compare them. We need *normalized* parameters called **unit quantities**, defined for a hypothetical turbine running under a head of **1 meter**.

| Unit Quantity | Symbol | Definition | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Unit Speed | $N_u$ | $N / \sqrt{H}$ | rpm |
| Unit Discharge | $Q_u$ | $Q / \sqrt{H}$ | m³/s |
| Unit Power | $P_u$ | $P / H^{3/2}$ | kW |

> [!NOTE]
> These unit quantities form the **universal signature** of a turbine family — a Francis turbine and a Kaplan turbine each have a unique set of unit-speed/unit-discharge relationships.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Stage-by-Stage Energy Conversion

In a reaction turbine, the total head $H$ is the sum of four distinct heads, and each component of the turbine progressively reduces the available head:

$$
H = H_{\text{casing}} + H_{\text{guide}} + H_{\text{runner}} + H_{\text{draft}}
$$

### Step 1 — Energy in the Spiral Casing / Penstock
Water from the high-pressure penstock enters the **spiral casing** of a Francis turbine. In a Kaplan turbine, it enters a **concrete scroll case** or a **distributor pipe**. The pressure head is maximum here, and velocity is moderate. No significant work is done.

### Step 2 — Energy in the Guide Vanes (Distributor)
The **stay vanes** (fixed) and **guide vanes** (adjustable) form the **distributor**. The cross-sectional area reduces, the flow accelerates, and the *velocity head* increases at the expense of *pressure head*. About **10–20 %** of the total head is converted from pressure to kinetic energy here.

> [!TIP]
> The guide vanes are the **throttle** of the turbine. Their opening is called the **gate opening ($G_0$)** or **wicket gate opening** — measured as a percentage of full opening or in millimeters.

### Step 3 — Energy Conversion in the Runner
This is the **heart of the turbine**. The high-velocity water jet from the guide vanes enters the runner blades at an angle and is deflected. The change in angular momentum of water causes a reactive force on the curved blades, producing torque. Both **pressure drop** and **kinetic energy extraction** occur here.

For a Francis turbine, water enters **radially** and leaves **axially**.
For a Kaplan turbine, water flows **axially** throughout.

### Step 4 — Energy Recovery in the Draft Tube
The draft tube is a **gradually expanding passage** that:
- Converts the residual **kinetic energy at runner exit** into **pressure energy** (recovery of kinetic head).
- Creates a **suction head (negative pressure)** at the runner outlet, increasing the effective head across the runner.
- Allows the turbine to be set above the tailwater level (civil engineering convenience).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The draft tube recovers about **30–60 %** of the kinetic energy leaving the runner. Without it, a reaction turbine would be grossly inefficient.

## 2.2 Hydraulic, Mechanical, and Overall Efficiency

The **input hydraulic power** to the turbine is:

$$
P_{\text{in}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H
$$

where:
- $\rho$ = density of water = **1000 kg/m³**
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $Q$ = volume flow rate (m³/s)
- $H$ = net head (m)

The **output shaft power** (brake power) measured by a dynamometer or electrical loading:

$$
P_{\text{out}} = \frac{2\pi N T}{60} \quad \text{[Watts]} = \frac{2\pi N T}{60000} \quad \text{[kW]}
$$

where:
- $N$ = rotational speed in rpm
- $T$ = torque in N·m

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three-Efficiency Framework (must memorize for KTU viva):**
> 1. **Hydraulic efficiency** $\eta_h = \dfrac{\text{Power imparted to water}}{\text{Input hydraulic power}}$
> 2. **Mechanical efficiency** $\eta_m = \dfrac{\text{Shaft power (brake power)}}{\text{Power imparted to water}}$
> 3. **Overall efficiency** $\eta_o = \eta_h \times \eta_m = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}}$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Complete Reference Table

> [!NOTE]
> **Use $\vert$ instead of | inside the table to prevent markdown parser breakage.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Input hydraulic power | $P_{\text{in}} = \rho g Q H$ | kW | Use $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s² |
| 2 | Shaft (brake) power | $P_{\text{out}} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60000}$ | kW | $N$ in rpm, $T$ in N·m |
| 3 | Overall efficiency | $\eta_o = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}}$ | decimal or % | Always between 0 and 1 |
| 4 | Unit speed | $N_u = \dfrac{N}{\sqrt{H}}$ | rpm | Constant for a turbine family |
| 5 | Unit discharge | $Q_u = \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{H}}$ | m³/s | Constant for a turbine family |
| 6 | Unit power | $P_u = \dfrac{P}{H^{3/2}}$ | kW | Constant for a turbine family |
| 7 | Specific speed | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ | metric (kW·m) | Used to classify turbines |
| 8 | Net head (Francis/Kaplan) | $H = H_g + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} - h_L - z$ | m | See derivation below |
| 9 | Discharge (volumetric) | $Q = A \times C_d \times \sqrt{2gH}$ | m³/s | $A$ = area, $C_d$ = coefficient of discharge |
| 10 | Flow coefficient | $\phi = \dfrac{Q}{N D^3}$ | — | Dimensionless |
| 11 | Head coefficient | $\psi = \dfrac{gH}{N^2 D^2}$ | — | Dimensionless |
| 12 | Power coefficient | $\lambda = \dfrac{P}{\rho N^3 D^5}$ | — | Dimensionless |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** The three dimensionless coefficients ($\phi, \psi, \lambda$) are the **CFD inputs** when validating a turbine design. They eliminate the need for full-scale testing and allow direct comparison of model and prototype.

## 2.4 The Three Characteristic Curves of a Reaction Turbine

The performance of a reaction turbine is represented graphically by three sets of curves, all plotted against **unit speed ($N_u$)** for a Francis turbine or **guide vane opening** for a Kaplan turbine:

### A. Main (Constant Head) Characteristics
- **X-axis:** $N_u$ (or $N$)
- **Y-axes:** $Q_u$, $P_u$, $\eta_o$
- Each curve is plotted for a fixed **guide vane opening** (or fixed **blade angle** for Kaplan).
- A family of curves is obtained for several gate openings.

### B. Operating (Combined) Characteristics
- **X-axis:** $P_u$ (or $P$)
- **Y-axes:** $Q_u$, $\eta_o$ (and sometimes $N_u$)
- Each curve is for a **constant speed** line — i.e., the speed at which the turbine will actually run when coupled to a generator at synchronous speed.
- This is the most **practically useful** graph for plant operators.

### C. Constant Efficiency Curves (Muschel Curves)
- **X-axis:** $Q_u$
- **Y-axis:** $N_u$
- Curves of **iso-efficiency** (lines of equal $\eta_o$) plotted on the $Q_u$–$N_u$ plane.
- Used to select the operating point with the highest efficiency for any given combination of head and discharge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Question Frequency:** *"Draw the main, operating, and constant efficiency characteristics of a reaction turbine and explain how the best efficiency point is determined."* — This is a **very common 7–14 mark question**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Input Hydraulic Power Equation

The input hydraulic power is the rate at which potential energy is delivered to the turbine.

**Step 1 — Mass flow rate.**
The volume flow rate is $Q$ m³/s, so the mass flow rate is:

$$
\dot{m} = \rho Q \quad \text{[kg/s]}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy per unit mass from net head.**
The net head $H$ meters of water represents the potential energy per unit weight. Since weight is $mg$ Newtons and $g$ = 9.81 m/s², the potential energy per unit mass is:

$$
E = gH \quad \text{[J/kg]}
$$

**Step 3 — Power (rate of energy transfer).**
Multiplying mass flow rate by energy per unit mass:

$$
P_{\text{in}} = \dot{m} \cdot gH = (\rho Q)(gH)
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{\text{in}} = \rho \, g \, Q \, H \quad \text{[Watts]}}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kilowatts (KTU convention):**

$$
P_{\text{in}} \ [\text{kW}] = \frac{\rho \, g \, Q \, H}{1000}
$$

For water ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²):

$$
P_{\text{in}} = 9.81 \, Q \, H \quad \text{[kW]}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Unit Quantities — Why They Are Head-Independent

The Euler turbine equation tells us the velocity triangles scale as the **square root of the head**. If we change $H$ to $H'$ at constant efficiency, the flow velocity $V$ changes as $\sqrt{H}$.

**Step 1 — Velocity scales with $\sqrt{H}$.**
By Bernoulli's equation, the jet velocity is:

$$
V \propto \sqrt{2gH} \propto \sqrt{H}
$$

**Step 2 — Discharge scales with velocity.**
For a given guide vane opening, the flow area $A$ is constant, so:

$$
Q = A \cdot V \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q \propto \sqrt{H}
$$

Hence, $Q/\sqrt{H}$ is **independent of head** — defining $Q_u$.

**Step 3 — Rotational speed scales with $\sqrt{H}$.**
For the runner to operate at the same blade tip-speed ratio:

$$
u = \frac{\pi D N}{60} \propto V \propto \sqrt{H} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N \propto \sqrt{H}
$$

Hence, $N/\sqrt{H}$ is **independent of head** — defining $N_u$.

**Step 4 — Power scales with $H^{3/2}$.**

$$
P = \rho g Q H \propto \sqrt{H} \cdot H = H^{3/2}
$$

Hence, $P / H^{3/2}$ is **independent of head** — defining $P_u$.

> [!NOTE]
> This is why, when a turbine operates at site conditions different from the model test head, we can predict its performance simply by re-scaling using the unit quantities. **The unit quantity is the universal signature of the turbine.**

## 3.3 Derivation of Specific Speed

Specific speed is the speed at which a geometrically similar turbine would run if it were built to produce **1 kW of power under 1 m of head**.

**Step 1 — Define scaling law for power with size.**

$$
P \propto N^3 D^5 \quad \text{[from power coefficient]}
$$

**Step 2 — Eliminate $D$ using the head relation $H \propto N^2 D^2$.**

$$
D^2 \propto \frac{H}{N^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad D^5 \propto \left(\frac{H}{N^2}\right)^{5/2}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back:**

$$
P \propto N^3 \left(\frac{H}{N^2}\right)^{5/2} = N^3 \cdot H^{5/2} \cdot N^{-5} = H^{5/2} \cdot N^{-2}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
N^2 \propto \frac{H^{5/2}}{P} \quad \Rightarrow \quad N \propto \frac{H^{5/4}}{\sqrt{P}}
$$

**Step 4 — Definition of specific speed** (with constants chosen so $N_s$ has units of rpm when $P$ is in kW and $H$ in meters):

$$
\boxed{N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}} \quad \text{[SI metric units]}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic — Turbine Family Map:**
> $N_s < 50$ → Pelton • $50 \le N_s \le 400$ → Francis • $N_s > 400$ → Kaplan / Propeller

## 3.4 Derivation of Net Head for the Laboratory Setup

In the lab, the net head $H$ is the head available between the **inlet pressure tap (just before the guide vane)** and the **outlet pressure tap (just after the draft tube exit / tailrace)**.

$$
H = \frac{p_1 - p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2g} + (z_1 - z_2)
$$

**Step 1 — Pressure head difference.**
Measured using a **U-tube differential manometer** connected across the inlet and outlet tappings. If the manometer reading is $h$ meters of mercury (or carbon tetrachloride or water), then:

$$
\frac{p_1 - p_2}{\rho g} = x \left(\frac{\rho_m}{\rho_w} - 1\right) h
$$

where $x = 12.6$ for mercury–water (since $13.6 - 1 = 12.6$).

**Step 2 — Velocity head correction.**
The inlet and outlet tappings are at different pipe diameters, so $V_1 \ne V_2$. With $V_1$ measured by an inlet orifice meter and $V_2$ as the tailrace exit velocity:

$$
\frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2g}
$$

**Step 3 — Datum head.**
The difference in elevation $z_1 - z_2$ between the inlet tapping and tailrace (often taken as zero if both are at the same level).

**Step 4 — Combine all three components** to get the net head $H$.

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

> **Question:** In a Francis turbine test, the following observations were taken at a constant head:
> - Volume of water collected in 60 s = **1.20 m³**
> - Net head $H$ = **8.0 m**
> - Speed $N$ = **600 rpm**
> - Brake load = **80 N** at a brake arm radius of **0.5 m**

**Step 1 — Discharge.**

$$
Q = \frac{1.20}{60} = 0.020 \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Shaft (brake) power.**

$$
P_{\text{out}} = \frac{2\pi (600)(80 \times 0.5)}{60000} = \frac{2\pi \times 600 \times 40}{60000}
$$

$$
P_{\text{out}} = \frac{150796.45}{60000} = 2.513 \ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Input hydraulic power.**

$$
P_{\text{in}} = 9.81 \times 0.020 \times 8.0 = 1.570 \ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Overall efficiency.**

$$
\eta_o = \frac{2.513}{1.570} = 1.601 \ \text{???}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Sanity Check Trigger!** Efficiency > 1 is **physically impossible**. The student has likely made a sign or unit error. Either the load reading is in different units (e.g., kgf) or the brake-arm radius / time measurement is wrong. **Always cross-check after Step 3** before declaring the final answer.

> [!TIP]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** The load is often read from a **spring balance in kgf**, and the torque is $T = (W_1 - W_2) \times R$ in kgf·m. To convert: $1 \text{ kgf} = 9.81$ N. So $T$ (N·m) = $9.81 \times (W_1 - W_2) \times R$.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Step-by-Step Test Procedure

| Stage | Action | Instrument Used | Output Recorded |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Start the pump, prime the system | Centrifugal pump | Steady flow established |
| 2 | Set the guide vane to a fixed opening | Graduated scale on guide vane mechanism | Gate opening $G_0$ |
| 3 | Apply load via the dynamometer | Rope brake / Prony brake / Electrical | Net load $(W_1 - W_2)$ in N or kgf |
| 4 | Measure speed | Tachometer (contact or non-contact) | $N$ in rpm |
| 5 | Measure head | Differential manometer | $h$ in mm of manometer fluid |
| 6 | Measure discharge | Collecting tank + stopwatch, or orifice meter | $V$ in m³ and $t$ in s, or $\Delta h$ |
| 7 | Vary load in 6–8 steps (zero to maximum) | Adjust dynamometer | Repeat all readings |
| 8 | Repeat for 2–3 different gate openings | Guide vane control | Multiple data sets |
| 9 | Calculate $P_{\text{out}}$, $P_{\text{in}}$, $\eta_o$, $N_u$, $Q_u$, $P_u$ | Calculator / spreadsheet | Tabulated results |
| 10 | Plot the three characteristic curves | Graph paper / software | Operating maps |
