# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Reaction Turbine Test Rig

The figure below maps the **flow path**, **energy conversions**, and **measurement points** of a typical KTU laboratory reaction turbine test rig (Francis / Kaplan).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reservoir Sump] --> B[Centrifugal Pump]
    B --> C[Delivery Pipe with Inlet Orifice Meter]
    C --> D[Inlet Pressure Tapping P1]
    D --> E[Spiral Casing / Scroll Case]
    E --> F[Guide Vane Mechanism Stay Vanes and Wicket Gates]
    F --> G[Runner Francis or Kaplan Blades]
    G --> H[Outlet Pressure Tapping P2]
    H --> I[Draft Tube]
    I --> J[Tailrace Returns to Sump]
    G --> K[Drive Shaft]
    K --> L[Prony Brake or Rope Brake Dynamometer]
    L --> M[Load Cell or Spring Balance]
    K --> N[Tachometer]
    D -. Differential Manometer .-> H
    L -. Torque Measurement .-> O[Calculation Block P_out, eta_o, Unit Quantities]
    N -. Speed Measurement .-> O
    C -. Discharge Measurement .-> O
    O --> P[Main Characteristics Plot]
    O --> Q[Operating Characteristics Plot]
    O --> R[Constant Efficiency Muschel Curves]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the diagram, the solid arrows represent **water flow paths**, and the dotted arrows represent **signal/data flow** to the calculation block. The calculation block is the conceptual location where all the equations from Section 3 are evaluated.

## 4.2 Schematic Cross-Section of a Francis Turbine

The following flowchart mimics the **vertical cross-section** view of a Francis turbine, labeling the key components encountered by water as it moves from inlet to outlet.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Inlet Penstock High Pressure Water Enters] --> nodeB[Spiral Casing Distributes Water Symmetrically Around Runner]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Stay Vanes Fixed Guide Blades Direct Flow]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Guide Vanes Wicket Gates Adjustable Throttle Flow]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Runner Inlet Radial Entry Whorl Chamber]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Runner Blades Curved Mixed Flow Path]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Runner Exit Axial Flow Leaves Runner]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Draft Tube Gradually Expanding Passage Recovers Pressure]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Tailrace Exit to River or Lower Reservoir]
    nodeJ[Main Shaft Carries Torque Out of Runner] --- nodeF
    nodeK[Tachometer Pulses on Shaft End] --- nodeJ
    nodeL[Prony Brake on Shaft Output Absorbs Mechanical Power] --- nodeJ
```

## 4.3 Schematic Cross-Section of a Kaplan Turbine

A Kaplan turbine is similar to a Francis turbine, except the flow is purely **axial** and the **runner blades are adjustable**.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    kapA[Concrete Scroll Case or Distributor Pipe] --> kapB[Guide Vane Mechanism Adjustable Wicket Gates]
    kapB --> kapC[Runner Hub with Hydraulic Servo Mechanism]
    kapC --> kapD[Adjustable Runner Blades Pitch Changed by Oil Pressure Servomotor]
    kapD --> kapE[Runner Exit Axial Downward Flow]
    kapE --> kapF[Elbow Type or Straight Draft Tube]
    kapF --> kapG[Tailrace]
    kapH[Regulating Shaft Connects Servomotor to Runner Blade Trunnions] --- kapC
    kapI[Governor Controls Guide Vanes and Blade Pitch Simultaneously] --- kapB
    kapI --- kapH
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction Visible in the Diagram:** The Kaplan turbine has an **extra control loop** (oil servomotor + regulating shaft) that allows the **runner blade pitch to be changed on the fly**. This is what gives the Kaplan turbine its flat efficiency curve over a wide range of discharges — its biggest advantage over the Francis turbine.

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Which Turbine for Which Application?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Hydropower Project Planning Stage] --> headQ{Available Head H in Meters}
    headQ --> highHead{H greater than 300 m}
    highHead --> yesHigh[Use Impulse Turbine Pelton]
    headQ --> medHead{30 m to 300 m}
    medHead --> useFrancis[Use Francis Turbine Medium Specific Speed]
    headQ --> lowHead{H less than 30 m}
    lowHead --> largeQ{Is Discharge Very Large}
    largeQ --> useKaplan[Use Kaplan Turbine High Specific Power]
    largeQ --> medQ[Use Propeller or Kaplan with Fixed Blades]
    useFrancis --> checkNs[Calculate Specific Speed Ns]
    useKaplan --> checkNs
    checkNs --> ok[Compare Against IS 11174 Charts and IEC 60193 Standards]
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram of the Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    meas1[Manometer Reading h mm] --> calcHead[Compute Net Head H in m]
    meas2[Orifice Differential h in m] --> calcQ[Compute Discharge Q in m³ per s]
    meas3[Brake Load W in N] --> calcT[Compute Torque T in Nm]
    meas4[Speed N in rpm] --> calcT
    calcT --> calcPout[Compute Shaft Power P_out in kW]
    calcHead --> calcPin[Compute Hydraulic Power P_in in kW]
    calcQ --> calcPin
    calcPout --> calcEta[Compute Overall Efficiency eta_o percent]
    calcPin --> calcEta
    calcEta --> calcUnit[Compute Unit Quantities N_u Q_u P_u]
    calcUnit --> plot[Plot Main Operating and Constant Efficiency Curves]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
*Define the term "specific speed" of a turbine and explain its significance in the classification of hydraulic turbines.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Specific speed ($N_s$) is defined as the speed at which a geometrically similar turbine would rotate if it were scaled to produce **1 kW of power under 1 m head**. Mathematically,

$$
N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}
$$

- **Significance (1.5 marks):** It is a **dimensionless classification index** for turbine families. Low $N_s$ implies an impulse-type high-head turbine (Pelton, $N_s < 50$), medium $N_s$ indicates Francis (50–400), and high $N_s$ indicates axial flow (Kaplan, $N_s > 400$). It also determines the **shape and speed** of the runner.

---

### **Question A2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
*With a neat sketch, explain the function of a draft tube in a reaction turbine.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Function 1 (1 mark):** Converts the **kinetic energy at the runner exit into pressure energy** by gradually expanding cross-section (Bernoulli's principle).
- **Function 2 (1 mark):** Creates a **suction (negative) head** at the runner outlet, thereby increasing the effective head across the runner.
- **Function 3 (1 mark):** Allows the turbine to be installed **above the tailwater level** (civil engineering convenience for inspection and maintenance).

A simple conical or elbow-shaped draft tube suffices for small installations; for high specific speed turbines, a **hydraulic efficiency of 80–90 %** can be obtained in the draft tube.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Format)

### **Question B1 (A) — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

> During a test on a Francis turbine, the following observations were recorded at a particular gate opening:
>
> - Net head, $H = 7.5$ m
> - Speed, $N = 550$ rpm
> - Discharge, $Q = 0.025$ m³/s
> - Shaft power (brake power), $P_{\text{out}} = 1.55$ kW
> - Diameter of runner, $D = 0.30$ m
>
> **Calculate:**
> **(a)** The overall efficiency of the turbine.
> **(b)** The specific speed of the turbine, and hence identify the type of turbine family.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Overall Efficiency (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Input hydraulic power:

$$
P_{\text{in}} = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.025 \times 7.5
$$

$$
P_{\text{in}} = 1839.375 \ \text{W} = 1.839 \ \text{kW}
$$

Step 2 — Output shaft power (given): $P_{\text{out}} = 1.55$ kW.

Step 3 — Overall efficiency:

$$
\eta_o = \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} = \frac{1.55}{1.839} = 0.8430
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_o = 84.3 \%}
$$

**Part (b) — Specific Speed (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Apply the specific speed formula:

$$
N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{P_{\text{out}}}}{H^{5/4}}
$$

Step 2 — Compute $\sqrt{P_{\text{out}}}$:

$$
\sqrt{P_{\text{out}}} = \sqrt{1.55} = 1.245 \ \sqrt{\text{kW}}
$$

Step 3 — Compute $H^{5/4}$:

$$
H^{5/4} = (7.5)^{1.25}
$$

Using logarithms: $\log(7.5) = 0.8751$, so $\log(7.5^{1.25}) = 1.25 \times 0.8751 = 1.0939$, giving $7.5^{1.25} \approx 12.41$.

Step 4 — Final calculation:

$$
N_s = \frac{550 \times 1.245}{12.41} = \frac{684.75}{12.41} \approx 55.2
$$

Step 5 — Identification: Since $50 \le N_s \le 400$, the turbine is a **Francis turbine** (medium specific speed, mixed flow).

> [!TIP]
> **Why $P_{\text{out}}$ and not $P_{\text{in}}$ in the specific speed formula?** The convention (IS 11174 / IEC 60193) is to use the **shaft (brake) power output** when computing $N_s$ for classification purposes. Some older textbooks use $P_{\text{in}}$ — the KTU board uses the **output power convention**.

---

### **Question B1 (B) — 14 Marks** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **Kaplan turbine**. Why are the runner blades of a Kaplan turbine made adjustable?
>
> **(b)** The following data are obtained from a model test of a Kaplan turbine at a head of **4 m**:
>
> - Speed at best efficiency point, $N = 480$ rpm
> - Discharge, $Q = 0.85$ m³/s
> - Output power, $P = 28.5$ kW
>
> **Estimate the unit speed, unit discharge, and unit power of the model. If a geometrically similar turbine of twice the linear dimensions is to be built for a site head of 16 m, calculate the speed, discharge, and power of the prototype.**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Kaplan Turbine Construction and Working (7 marks):**

A Kaplan turbine is an **axial-flow reaction turbine** with adjustable runner blades. The main components are:

1. **Scroll case / concrete spiral casing (1 mark):** Distributes water symmetrically around the runner. For low-head installations, a concrete spiral is preferred over a metal one (cost saving).

2. **Guide vane mechanism (wicket gates) (1 mark):** Adjustable stay ring with 12–24 vanes that control the flow rate and the direction of water entering the runner. Driven by a regulating ring.

3. **Runner (1 mark):** Typically 3–8 blades mounted on a hub. The hub contains a **servo mechanism** (oil-pressure operated) that rotates each blade about its trunnion to change the pitch.

4. **Draft tube (1 mark):** Elbow or straight conical type; recovers exit kinetic energy and creates suction head.

5. **Working principle (2 marks):** Water enters the guide vanes axially, gets a swirl component, enters the runner axially, and leaves axially. The reactive force on the runner blades produces torque. Adjusting the blade pitch simultaneously with the guide vane opening allows the velocity triangles to remain optimal over a wide range of discharges, giving a **flat efficiency curve**.

**Why adjustable blades?** (1 mark) To maintain **high efficiency over a wide range of load** — Kaplan turbines can operate at 80–90 % efficiency from 30 % to 100 % of rated load.

**Part (b) — Scale-Up Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Unit quantities of the model (head $H_m = 4$ m):**

$$
N_u = \frac{N}{\sqrt{H}} = \frac{480}{\sqrt{4}} = \frac{480}{2} = 240 \ \text{rpm}
$$

$$
Q_u = \frac{Q}{\sqrt{H}} = \frac{0.85}{\sqrt{4}} = \frac{0.85}{2} = 0.425 \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
P_u = \frac{P}{H^{3/2}} = \frac{28.5}{(4)^{1.5}} = \frac{28.5}{8} = 3.5625 \ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Prototype head $H_p = 16$ m, scale factor $D_p/D_m = 2$.**

**Step 3 — Prototype speed:**
For geometrically similar turbines, $N_p \cdot D_p / \sqrt{H_p} = N_m \cdot D_m / \sqrt{H_m}$ (homologous relationship).

$$
N_p = N_m \times \frac{D_m}{D_p} \times \sqrt{\frac{H_p}{H_m}} = 480 \times \frac{1}{2} \times \sqrt{\frac{16}{4}}
$$

$$
N_p = 480 \times 0.5 \times 2 = 480 \ \text{rpm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The prototype runs at the same speed as the model!** This is the **homologous (similarity) speed** — a remarkable result of the dimensionless scaling laws. It is also why turbine manufacturers can build model turbines at convenient speeds and scale up to prototype sizes.

**Step 4 — Prototype discharge:**

$$
Q_p = Q_m \times \left(\frac{D_p}{D_m}\right)^2 \times \sqrt{\frac{H_p}{H_m}} = 0.85 \times 4 \times 2 = 6.80 \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 5 — Prototype power (assuming same efficiency):**

$$
P_p = P_m \times \left(\frac{D_p}{D_m}\right)^2 \times \left(\frac{H_p}{H_m}\right)^{3/2} = 28.5 \times 4 \times 8 = 912 \ \text{kW}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to use $P_{\text{out}}$, not $P_{\text{in}}$** in the specific speed formula — KTU board deducts 1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing up unit quantities with specific speed** — $N_u$ and $Q_u$ are for a single turbine at different heads; $N_s$ is for a *family* of geometrically similar turbines.
> 3. **Using $H^{1/2}$ instead of $H^{3/2}$ in the power scaling law** — students frequently confuse these. Power scales as $H^{3/2}$, not $H^{1/2}$ and not $H^{5/4}$.
> 4. **Skipping unit conversions** — $P$ in kW, $H$ in meters, $N$ in rpm. If you use $P$ in W or $H$ in cm, you lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Not stating the assumption of constant efficiency** during scale-up — always mention "assuming the prototype operates at the same overall efficiency as the model."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam Day:**

- [ ] **Reaction turbines** convert pressure + kinetic energy in the runner itself; impulse turbines convert only kinetic energy in the runner.
- [ ] **Francis** = mixed flow (radial → axial), $N_s$ = 60–450, $H$ = 30–600 m. **Kaplan** = axial flow throughout, $N_s$ = 300–1500, $H$ = 2–70 m.
- [ ] **Three efficiencies to remember:** $\eta_h$ (hydraulic), $\eta_m$ (mechanical), $\eta_o = \eta_h \times \eta_m$ (overall).
- [ ] **Input power formula:** $P_{\text{in}} = \rho g Q H$ = $9.81 \cdot Q \cdot H$ kW for water.
- [ ] **Shaft power formula:** $P_{\text{out}} = 2\pi N T / 60{,}000$ kW (with $N$ in rpm, $T$ in N·m).
- [ ] **Overall efficiency:** $\eta_o = P_{\text{out}} / P_{\text{in}}$ — must be between 0 and 1.
- [ ] **Unit quantities** eliminate head dependence: $N_u = N/\sqrt{H}$, $Q_u = Q/\sqrt{H}$, $P_u = P/H^{3/2}$.
- [ ] **Specific speed formula:** $N_s = N \sqrt{P} / H^{5/4}$ — use $P$ in **kW** (shaft power) and $H$ in **meters**.
- [ ] **Turbine family from $N_s$:** Pelton < 50 ≤ Francis < 400 ≤ Kaplan.
- [ ] **Draft tube** does three jobs: (1) recovers kinetic energy, (2) creates suction head, (3) allows turbine to be above tailrace.
- [ ] **Three characteristic curves:** Main (constant gate opening), Operating (constant speed), Constant Efficiency (Muschel curves).
- [ ] **Scale-up laws** (homologous turbines): $Q \propto D^2 \sqrt{H}$, $P \propto D^2 H^{3/2}$ (constant efficiency), $N \propto \sqrt{H}/D$.
- [ ] **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)** is the gate opening and speed combination where $\eta_o$ peaks — this is the **design point** of the turbine.
- [ ] **Proving of turbines** is done per IS 11174 / IEC 60193 standards at a reference head of 1 m (unit head).
- [ ] **Viva Favourite:** *"Why is a draft tube always provided in a reaction turbine but never in an impulse turbine?"* — Because the runner in an impulse turbine discharges to atmosphere, there is no residual pressure to recover; in a reaction turbine, the water leaves the runner with significant pressure and kinetic energy that can be usefully recovered.

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line Summary for the Last-Minute Glance:**
> *In a reaction turbine, water is under pressure throughout the runner, energy conversion is distributed, and the performance test measures $Q$, $H$, $N$, and $T$ to compute $\eta_o$ and the unit-quantity signature — which is then used to predict prototype performance and draw the operating characteristic curves.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Speed Variation Test on Pelton Turbine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMEL407 - FLUID MECHANICS AND HYDRAULIC MACHINES LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Speed Variation Test on Pelton Turbine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Speed Variation Test** (also called the **Constant Head Test** or **Iso-Head Test**) on a **Pelton Turbine** is a standard laboratory experiment conducted to obtain the **Main Characteristic Curves** of the turbine. In this test, the **net head (H)** and the **spear valve opening (discharge control)** are held strictly constant, while the **speed (N)** is varied over a wide operational range by progressively changing the **brake load** applied on the **rope brake dynamometer**.

The test fundamentally establishes the **homologous (dimensionless) performance** of the Pelton wheel, which can subsequently be used to **predict the behaviour of any geometrically similar Pelton turbine** running under **any head condition** — without actually building and testing that machine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme – Course Outcome Mapping**
> This experiment directly addresses:
> - **CO2:** Conduct experiments on hydraulic turbines and determine characteristic curves.
> - **CO3:** Analyse the performance of impulse and reaction turbines using dimensional / unit-quantity analysis.
> - **Bloom's Level:** Apply (L3) → Analyse (L4) → Evaluate (L5)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of a Pelton turbine like a **bicycle on a treadmill**:

- The **treadmill speed** = Net head *H* (energy of water jets). It must be constant.
- The **roller resistance strap (brake)** = The rope brake load. You tighten or loosen it.
- The **pedalling rate** = Rotor speed *N*. When you brake harder → speed drops; when you release → speed rises.
- The **flow of water through the nozzle** = The fuel. The spear valve (nozzle needle) is set at a fixed position, so the discharge *Q* stays nearly constant — just like keeping a fuel injector at a fixed opening in an engine dyno test.

By plotting the **fuel efficiency (η)** of the bicycle at different pedalling rates *while keeping the treadmill constant*, you get a universal efficiency curve. **Any other cyclist (similar geometry, different treadmill) can use your curve to predict their own performance** — that's the entire purpose of the unit-quantity (homologous) method.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Big Picture (Why this test matters)**
> Building a full-scale 500 MW Pelton turbine and testing it in a lab is impossible. But a small 5 kW model can be tested. By converting its measurements into **dimensionless unit quantities**, we obtain a "**blueprint**" of performance valid for the entire family of geometrically similar turbines — from 5 kW lab models to 500 MW real machines. This is the **practical engineering utility** of the Speed Variation Test.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Density of water | $\rho$ | **1000 kg/m³** |
| Specific weight of water | $\rho g$ | **9810 N/m³** |
| Net head range (lab) | $H$ | typically **20–50 m** |
| Rotational speed range | $N$ | typically **200–600 rpm** |
| Specific weight of water | $\gamma$ | **9.81 kN/m³** |
| Effective arm radius (rope brake) | $L$ | **0.30–0.50 m** (must be in **metres** for SI) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: The Need for Unit Quantities

Two geometrically similar Pelton turbines operating at **different heads** are said to be in a state of **kinematic and dynamic similarity** when their **unit (homologous) quantities are equal**. This is the essence of the **Buckingham π theorem** applied to turbomachinery.

For the Pelton turbine, the power output *P* is a function of:
- $P = f(\rho, Q, N, D, g, H)$ — a set of 6 variables and 3 fundamental dimensions (M, L, T).

Dimensional reduction yields **three independent dimensionless groups** that completely describe performance:

$$N_u, \quad Q_u, \quad P_u \quad \text{(unit speed, unit discharge, unit power)}$$

If two turbines are **geometrically similar** and run at the **same unit speed**, they will have the **same unit discharge, unit power, and efficiency** — regardless of their actual size or head.

## 2.2 Logical Step-by-Step Operational Analysis

**Step 1 — Fix the operating environment:**
- Open the supply valve to establish a **steady constant head** *H* in the upper tank.
- Verify with the **piezometer / pressure gauge** at the nozzle inlet. *H* should remain within ±2% throughout the test.

**Step 2 — Fix the discharge:**
- Set the **spear valve opening** to a predetermined graduation. For a Pelton wheel, $Q$ is theoretically governed only by the spear needle position and net head (it is essentially **independent of speed** — unlike a reaction turbine). Hence at constant *H* and fixed spear, *Q* is practically constant.

**Step 3 — Vary the speed via the brake load:**
- Apply load using the **rope brake dynamometer** by tightening the tightening handle.
- Each loading step causes the rotor to **decelerate** to a new equilibrium speed.
- Record the corresponding **spring balance readings** *W* and *S*, the **effective arm length** *L*, and the **speed** *N* on the **tachometer / stroboscope**.

**Step 4 — Tabulate the raw observations:**

| Trial | Speed *N* (rpm) | Spring Balance *W* (N) | Spring Balance *S* (N) | Net Head *H* (m) | Discharge *Q* (m³/s) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | max | high | low | const | const |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| n | min (runaway) | low | ~0 | const | const |

**Step 5 — Compute the derived performance parameters** (refer to Section 3 for derivation):
- Brake Power $BP = (W - S) \cdot L \cdot 2\pi N / 60$
- Water Power $WP = \rho g Q H$
- Overall Efficiency $\eta_o = BP / WP$
- Unit Speed $N_u = N / \sqrt{H}$
- Unit Discharge $Q_u = Q / \sqrt{H}$
- Unit Power $P_u = P / H^{3/2}$

**Step 6 — Plot the Main Characteristic Curves:**
1. $N_u$ vs $Q_u$  → **Constant Head / Iso-Head Curve**
2. $N_u$ vs $P_u$  → **Iso-Head Power Curve**
3. $N_u$ vs $\eta_o$ → **Constant Head Efficiency Curve**

**Step 7 — Draw Constant Efficiency (Muster) Curves:**
From the main characteristic curves, draw horizontal lines of constant $\eta$ and pick the corresponding $(N_u, Q_u)$ and $(N_u, P_u)$ points. Re-plotting these across multiple assumed heads gives a **universal set of curves** valid for *all* heads.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Board Exam Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains EVERY formula you need for a 3-mark, 7-mark, or 14-mark question on this experiment. Memorise this.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) | Typical Range / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Brake Power | $BP = (W - S) \cdot L \cdot \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | Watt (W) | $W$, $S$ in **N**; $L$ in **m**; $N$ in **rpm** |
| 2 | Water Power | $WP = \rho \, g \, Q \, H$ | Watt (W) | $\rho g = 9810$ N/m³ |
| 3 | Specific Speed | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ | (metric units) | For **turbines**; **dimensionless** when using metric |
| 4 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_o = \dfrac{BP}{WP} = \dfrac{BP}{\rho g Q H}$ | (decimal / %) | $0 < \eta_o < 1$ |
| 5 | Unit Speed | $N_u = \dfrac{N}{\sqrt{H}}$ | $\dfrac{\text{rpm}}{\sqrt{\text{m}}}$ | Holds for **impulse** turbines |
| 6 | Unit Discharge | $Q_u = \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{H}}$ | $\dfrac{\text{m}^3/\text{s}}{\sqrt{\text{m}}}$ | For **impulse** turbines |
| 7 | Unit Power | $P_u = \dfrac{P}{H^{3/2}}$ | $\dfrac{\text{W}}{\text{m}^{3/2}}$ | For **impulse** turbines |
| 8 | Net Head (for Pelton) | $H_{\text{net}} = H_{\text{gross}} - h_f$ | m | Subtract pipe-friction loss |
| 9 | Runaway Speed | $N_{\text{runaway}} \approx 1.8 \, N_{\text{rated}}$ | rpm | Load $\rightarrow 0$ |
| 10 | Bucket velocity ratio | $K_u = \dfrac{u_1}{\sqrt{2 g H}}$ | – | Optimum $\approx 0.45$–$0.48$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reaction vs Impulse — which exponents to use?**
> The exponents $\sqrt{H}$ and $H^{3/2}$ are for **impulse turbines (Pelton)**. For **reaction turbines (Francis, Kaplan)**, discharge varies with $H^{1/2}$ but **power varies with $H^{3/2}$** only when the unit speed is held constant. KTU generally uses the impulse form for Pelton questions.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydroelectric Power Plant Design:** Before a 250 MW Pelton is commissioned at Idukki or Sharavathi, scale models are run on this exact test rig. The unit quantities tell the designer **at which head and speed the prototype will achieve peak efficiency**.
- **Turbine Selection:** Characteristic curves allow selection of an "operating point" balancing cost, efficiency, and machine life.
- **Predicting Off-Design Behaviour:** If the river head drops from 250 m to 200 m, the engineer uses unit quantities to forecast the **new speed, power, and efficiency** without re-testing.
- **Condition Monitoring:** Periodic repetition of this test detects **cavitation damage, nozzle wear, or bucket pitting** (visible as a sudden drop in $\eta$ at certain $N_u$ values).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Unit Quantities (Dimensional Homology)

**Step 1 — Identify the dependent variable and independent parameters.**
For a geometrically similar family of Pelton turbines, the power output $P$ depends on the following independent parameters:

$$P = f(\rho, Q, N, D, g, H)$$

where $\rho$ = density, $Q$ = discharge, $N$ = speed, $D$ = jet diameter (or bucket width), $g$ = gravity, $H$ = net head.

**Step 2 — Count the variables and fundamental dimensions.**
- Total variables: $n = 6$ ($P, \rho, Q, N, D, g, H$ → **7 including $P$** itself; **6 independent**).
- Fundamental dimensions: $k = 3$ (M, L, T).
- Number of dimensionless $\pi$ groups: $n - k = 6 - 3 = 3$.

**Step 3 — Select repeating variables** $\rho$, $N$, $D$ (these contain all three dimensions and are not dependent on the others).

**Step 4 — Form the $\pi$ groups:**

For $\pi_1$ (containing $P$):

$$\pi_1 = \dfrac{P}{\rho^{a} \, N^{b} \, D^{c}}$$

Equating dimensions of $[M L^2 T^{-3}] = [M L^{-3}]^a \, [T^{-1}]^b \, [L]^c$:

- M: $1 = a \Rightarrow a = 1$
- L: $2 = -3a + c = -3 + c \Rightarrow c = 5$
- T: $-3 = -b \Rightarrow b = 3$

$$\pi_1 = \dfrac{P}{\rho \, N^{3} \, D^{5}}$$

For $\pi_2$ (containing $Q$):

$$\pi_2 = \dfrac{Q}{\rho^{a} N^{b} D^{c}} \Rightarrow [L^3 T^{-1}] = [M L^{-3}]^a [T^{-1}]^b [L]^c$$

- M: $0 = a \Rightarrow a = 0$
- T: $-1 = -b \Rightarrow b = 1$
- L: $3 = c \Rightarrow c = 3$

$$\pi_2 = \dfrac{Q}{N \, D^{3}}$$

For $\pi_3$ (containing $gH$):

$$\pi_3 = \dfrac{gH}{N^{2} D^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Express the dependent group as a function:**

$$\dfrac{P}{\rho N^{3} D^{5}} = \phi\!\left(\dfrac{Q}{N D^{3}}, \dfrac{gH}{N^{2} D^{2}}\right)$$

**Step 6 — For a Pelton turbine, the jet diameter $D$ relates to discharge as:**

$$Q = C_d \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^{2} \cdot \sqrt{2gH} \quad \Rightarrow \quad D^{2} \propto \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{H}}$$

Substituting and rearranging in terms of unit speed $N_u = N/\sqrt{H}$:

$$\boxed{N_u = \dfrac{N}{\sqrt{H}}, \quad Q_u = \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{H}}, \quad P_u = \dfrac{P}{H^{3/2}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification by substitution:** If two Pelton turbines have the same $N_u$, $Q_u$, $P_u$, then the relationship $\pi_1 = \phi(\pi_2, \pi_3)$ is satisfied, guaranteeing **dynamic similarity**. This is the formal proof used by KTU board examiners.

## 3.2 Derivation of Brake Power from Rope-Brake Dynamometer

A rope wound around the **brake drum** of radius $R$ (effective arm length $L$) experiences tension $T_1$ on the **tight side** and $T_2$ on the **slack side**. Two spring balances measure these as $W$ and $S$ respectively.

**Step 1 — Net tangential force on the drum:**

$$F_{\text{net}} = T_1 - T_2 = W - S \quad [\text{N}]$$

**Step 2 — Torque transmitted:**

$$T = F_{\text{net}} \times L = (W - S) \cdot L \quad [\text{N·m}]$$

**Step 3 — Angular velocity in rad/s:**

$$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} \quad [\text{rad/s}]$$

**Step 4 — Brake Power (mechanical output):**

$$\boxed{BP = T \cdot \omega = (W - S) \cdot L \cdot \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} \quad [\text{Watt}]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit conversion trap (Kerala KTU board) — DO NOT LOSE MARKS HERE.**
> If $L$ is given in **cm**, you **must** divide by 100 before plugging in. If $W, S$ are in **kgf**, multiply by **9.81** to convert to **N** first. If $N$ is in **rps**, remove the 60 from the denominator. The 14-mark valuation explicitly checks these conversions.

## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Example (Full 14-Mark Solution Style)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Pattern]**
> A Pelton turbine is tested under constant head. The following observations are recorded. Calculate the **overall efficiency** at each trial and plot the **main characteristic curves**.

**Given Data:**

| Trial | Speed *N* (rpm) | *W* (N) | *S* (N) | Effective Arm *L* (m) | Net Head *H* (m) | Discharge *Q* (m³/s) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 360 | 60 | 5 | 0.50 | 30 | 0.020 |
| 2 | 320 | 52 | 8 | 0.50 | 30 | 0.020 |
| 3 | 280 | 44 | 11 | 0.50 | 30 | 0.020 |
| 4 | 240 | 36 | 14 | 0.50 | 30 | 0.020 |
| 5 | 200 | 28 | 17 | 0.50 | 30 | 0.020 |
| 6 | 160 | 20 | 20 | 0.50 | 30 | 0.020 |

**Solution — Trial 1 (representative):**

Step 1 — Net force:
$$\Delta T = W - S = 60 - 5 = 55 \; \text{N}$$

Step 2 — Torque:
$$T = 55 \times 0.50 = 27.5 \; \text{N·m}$$

Step 3 — Angular velocity:
$$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi \times 360}{60} = 37.70 \; \text{rad/s}$$

Step 4 — Brake power:
$$BP = 27.5 \times 37.70 = 1036.7 \; \text{W} \approx 1.037 \; \text{kW}$$

Step 5 — Water power (constant for all trials):
$$WP = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.020 \times 30 = 5886 \; \text{W} \approx 5.886 \; \text{kW}$$

Step 6 — Overall efficiency:
$$\eta_o = \dfrac{1036.7}{5886} = 0.1761 = 17.61\%$$

Step 7 — Unit quantities:
$$N_u = \dfrac{360}{\sqrt{30}} = 65.73 \; \text{rpm}/\sqrt{\text{m}}$$

$$Q_u = \dfrac{0.020}{\sqrt{30}} = 0.00365 \; \text{m}^{5/2}/\text{s}$$

$$P_u = \dfrac{1036.7}{30^{3/2}} = \dfrac{1036.7}{164.32} = 6.31 \; \text{W}/\text{m}^{3/2}$$

**Complete tabulated result:**

| Trial | $N$ (rpm) | $BP$ (W) | $\eta_o$ (%) | $N_u$ (rpm/√m) | $Q_u$ | $P_u$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 360 | 1036.7 | 17.61 | 65.73 | 0.00365 | 6.31 |
| 2 | 320 | 921.1 | 15.65 | 58.42 | 0.00365 | 5.61 |
| 3 | 280 | 807.3 | 13.71 | 51.11 | 0.00365 | 4.91 |
| 4 | 240 | 691.2 | 11.74 | 43.81 | 0.00365 | 4.21 |
| 5 | 200 | 577.0 | 9.80 | 36.51 | 0.00365 | 3.51 |
| 6 | 160 | 0.0 | 0.00 | 29.21 | 0.00365 | 0.00 |

> *(Note: Trial 6 corresponds to runaway/no-load; ideally S approaches W so BP → 0. Values here are illustrative.)*

**Step 8 — Plotting:** Draw three curves on the same x-axis ($N_u$):
- Curve A: $N_u$ vs $Q_u$ → essentially a **vertical line** (since $Q$ is constant for Pelton at fixed spear and head).
- Curve B: $N_u$ vs $P_u$ → a **rising curve**, maximum at best-efficiency point.
- Curve C: $N_u$ vs $\eta_o$ → a **parabolic shape** with peak at "best efficiency point".

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Summary of Trends (What to Write in the Viva)

| Operating Regime | $N$ | $W-S$ | $BP$ | $\eta_o$ | Physical Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| No Load (Runaway) | Max | $\approx 0$ | 0 | 0 | All energy goes to friction/kinetic |
| Light Load | High | Small | Small | Low | Jet slips past buckets |
| **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)** | **Rated** | **Optimum** | **Max η** | **Peak** | **Bucket velocity ≈ jet velocity component** |
| Full Load | Low | Large | Large | Moderate | Heavy braking, low speed |
| Overloaded | Very low | Max | Max mechanical | Very low | Risk of stalling, water spill-back |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of the Pelton Turbine Test Rig

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Centrifugal Pump] --> B[Upper Supply Tank]
    B --> C[Pressure Gauge / Piezometer]
    C --> D[Delivery Pipe with Control Valve]
    D --> E[Spear Valve / Nozzle Assembly]
    E --> F[High-Velocity Water Jet]
    F --> G[Pelton Runner with Buckets]
    G --> H[Brake Drum with Rope]
    H --> I[Spring Balance W - Tight Side]
    H --> J[Spring Balance S - Slack Side]
    G --> K[Tachometer / Stroboscope]
    B --> L[Measuring Tank with V-Notch]
    L --> M[Stop Watch for Q]
    M --> N[Volumetric Discharge Q]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style E fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style G fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style H fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
    style L fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92
```

## 4.2 Experimental Procedure Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Check water supply and instruments] --> B[Start centrifugal pump]
    B --> C[Open delivery valve to establish constant net head H]
    C --> D[Set spear valve opening to fixed graduation]
    D --> E[Allow turbine to reach steady runaway speed]
    E --> F[Apply initial brake load using rope brake handle]
    F --> G[Wait for steady state speed]
    G --> H[Record N, W, S, time for Q collection]
    H --> I[Increase brake load progressively]
    I --> J{More trials to record?}
    J -->|Yes| G
    J -->|No| K[Stop pump, drain measuring tank]
    K --> L[Calculate BP, WP, eta for each trial]
    L --> M[Calculate unit quantities N_u, Q_u, P_u]
    M --> N[Tabulate and plot main characteristic curves]
    N --> O[Draw constant efficiency muster curves]
    O --> P[End: Report Best Efficiency Point]

    style A fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b
    style P fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20
    style L fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17
    style N fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
```

## 4.3 Main Characteristic Curves (Graphical Schematic)

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Main Characteristic Curves of Pelton Turbine"
    x-axis "Unit Speed N_u (rpm / sqrt m)" [20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70]
    y-axis "Efficiency eta and Unit Power P_u" 0 --> 100
    line [0, 40, 75, 90, 85, 60]
    line [0, 15, 35, 60, 80, 95]
```

> **How to read this chart:**
> - The **bell-shaped curve** peaking near $N_u \approx 50$ represents **Efficiency $\eta_o$** (%) — it rises to a maximum (Best Efficiency Point) and then falls.
> - The **monotonically rising curve** represents **Unit Power $P_u$** (× some scale factor) — it keeps increasing as $N_u$ rises.
> - The **Unit Discharge $Q_u$** curve is a **vertical straight line** for Pelton (because $Q$ is independent of $N$ at fixed spear opening and head).

## 4.4 Construction of Constant Efficiency (Muster) Curves

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Main Characteristic Curves at H1] -->|Plot on transparent paper| B[Mark iso-efficiency points]
    C[Main Characteristic Curves at H2] -->|Plot on same paper| B
    D[Main Characteristic Curves at H3] -->|Plot on same paper| B
    B --> E[Join same eta points across heads]
    E --> F[Universal Muster / Constant Efficiency Curves]
    F --> G[Valid for ALL similar Pelton turbines]

    style A fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style C fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style D fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style F fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style G fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Muster Curves vs Main Characteristic Curves — When to use which?**
> - **Main Characteristic Curves** are valid for a **specific head** at which the test was done.
> - **Constant Efficiency (Muster) Curves** are valid for **any head** and are obtained by combining main characteristic curves from multiple test runs. They are the **final deliverable** of a complete turbine testing programme.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**"Define unit speed, unit discharge, and unit power for a Pelton turbine. Why are these quantities important?"**

**Model Answer:**

Unit speed, unit discharge, and unit power are the **dimensionless homologous parameters** that define the performance of a geometrically similar family of impulse (Pelton) turbines. They are defined as:

$$N_u = \dfrac{N}{\sqrt{H}} \quad ; \quad Q_u = \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{H}} \quad ; \quad P_u = \dfrac{P}{H^{3/2}}$$

These quantities are important because they allow the **performance of a model turbine** (tested in the laboratory at a low head) to be **transferred to a full-scale prototype** operating at a much higher head. **Two geometrically similar Pelton turbines running at the same unit speed will have the same unit discharge, unit power, and overall efficiency** — irrespective of their physical size. This is the foundation of **model-to-prototype scaling** in hydraulic machine design. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**"What is meant by the 'Best Efficiency Point' (BEP) of a Pelton turbine? How is it identified from the characteristic curves?"**

**Model Answer:**

The **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)** is the unique operating condition at which the **overall efficiency of the Pelton turbine attains its maximum value** for a given head and spear-valve opening. At this point, the **kinetic energy of the water jet is most efficiently converted into mechanical shaft work** because the bucket velocity $u$ is optimally matched with the jet velocity $V_1$ — typically $u / V_1 \approx 0.45$–$0.48$.

It is identified from the **$N_u$ vs $\eta_o$** characteristic curve as the **peak (apex) of the parabolic curve**. Dropping a vertical line from this peak onto the $N_u$ axis gives the **unit speed at BEP**, and the corresponding values of $Q_u$ and $P_u$ can be read off the other two characteristic curves. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **7 + 7**. Two sub-parts (a) and (b). **You must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B (internal choice is between whole questions, not sub-parts).**

---

### **🅰️ Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]`

**"A Pelton wheel is tested under constant head of 40 m. The discharge measured is 0.025 m³/s. The effective arm of the rope brake is 0.50 m. The following readings are observed:"**

| Trial | $N$ (rpm) | $W$ (N) | $S$ (N) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 400 | 65 | 4 |
| 2 | 360 | 58 | 7 |
| 3 | 320 | 50 | 10 |
| 4 | 280 | 42 | 13 |
| 5 | 240 | 33 | 16 |
| 6 | 200 | 25 | 19 |

**(a)** Calculate the **overall efficiency** and **unit speed, unit discharge, unit power** for each trial. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Plot the **three main characteristic curves** of the Pelton turbine and identify the **Best Efficiency Point**. What inferences do you draw from the shape of the curves? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Common calculations (constant for all trials):**

Angular velocity at $N = 400$ rpm:
$$\omega_1 = \dfrac{2\pi \times 400}{60} = 41.89 \; \text{rad/s}$$

Net force for Trial 1:
$$\Delta T_1 = 65 - 4 = 61 \; \text{N}$$

Torque for Trial 1:
$$T_1 = 61 \times 0.50 = 30.5 \; \text{N·m}$$

Brake power for Trial 1:
$$BP_1 = 30.5 \times 41.89 = 1277.6 \; \text{W}$$

Water power (same for all trials):
$$WP = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.025 \times 40 = 9810 \; \text{W}$$

Overall efficiency for Trial 1:
$$\eta_{o,1} = \dfrac{1277.6}{9810} = 0.1302 = 13.02\%$$

Unit speed for Trial 1:
$$N_{u,1} = \dfrac{400}{\sqrt{40}} = 63.25 \; \text{rpm}/\sqrt{\text{m}}$$

Unit discharge (constant):
$$Q_u = \dfrac{0.025}{\sqrt{40}} = 3.953 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^{5/2}/\text{s}$$

Unit power for Trial 1:
$$P_{u,1} = \dfrac{1277.6}{40^{3/2}} = \dfrac{1277.6}{252.98} = 5.05 \; \text{W}/\text{m}^{3/2}$$

**Full Tabulation:**

| Trial | $N$ (rpm) | $BP$ (W) | $\eta_o$ (%) | $N_u$ | $P_u$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 400 | 1277.6 | 13.02 | 63.25 | 5.05 |
| 2 | 360 | 1195.6 | 12.19 | 56.92 | 4.73 |
| 3 | 320 | 1051.5 | 10.72 | 50.60 | 4.16 |
| 4 | 280 | 850.1 | 8.67 | 44.27 | 3.36 |
| 5 | 240 | 639.0 | 6.51 | 37.95 | 2.53 |
| 6 | 200 | 376.4 | 3.84 | 31.62 | 1.49 |

---

#### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Plot the three curves** with $N_u$ on the x-axis:

- **$N_u$ vs $Q_u$** → A **vertical straight line** at $Q_u = 3.953 \times 10^{-3}$ (because $Q$ is constant for Pelton at fixed spear and head).
- **$N_u$ vs $P_u$** → A **monotonically rising curve** from origin to peak (here at $N_u = 63.25$, $P_u = 5.05$).
- **$N_u$ vs $\eta_o$** → A **bell-shaped (parabolic) curve** rising to a peak and then falling.

**Step 2 — Best Efficiency Point:**

The peak of the $\eta_o$ curve in this data lies at the **highest loaded trial** ($N_u = 63.25$, $\eta_o = 13.02\%$). In a real test, the curve continues beyond this point and reaches a maximum, then falls as $N_u$ further increases. Hence **BEP corresponds to the apex of the $N_u$–$\eta_o$ curve**.

**Step 3 — Inferences (these are KTU-board standard answers — memorise these):**

1. **At low unit speed (over-loaded condition)**, the bucket moves too slowly to fully absorb the jet momentum; the water **slips past the buckets** without transferring energy → low efficiency.

2. **At very high unit speed (near runaway / no-load)**, the buckets move almost as fast as the jet, the relative velocity of impact becomes very small, and the water **does not impinge effectively** on the bucket splitter → low efficiency.

3. **At the Best Efficiency Point**, the bucket velocity ratio $K_u = u / \sqrt{2gH} \approx 0.46$, ensuring that the jet is deflected through approximately $165°$–$170°$ with maximum momentum transfer.

4. **The $N_u$ vs $Q_u$ curve is a vertical line** — this confirms that for an impulse turbine, **discharge is independent of speed** at a given head and spear opening. This is a unique signature of impulse machines.

5. **The $N_u$ vs $P_u$ curve rises linearly up to BEP and then flattens** — beyond BEP, the rising friction and windage losses counter the increase in mechanical output.

---

### **🅱️ Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE** — `[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]`

**"With the help of a neat sketch, describe the experimental procedure to conduct the speed variation test on a Pelton turbine in the laboratory."**

**(a)** Draw a labelled **schematic of the test rig** and list the **instruments** used. List at least **six observations** that must be recorded during the test. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **expressions for unit speed, unit discharge, and unit power** for a Pelton turbine using **dimensional analysis**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Schematic (reproduce the block diagram from Section 4.1 of this document):**

A labelled diagram of the test rig must include:
- **Centrifugal pump** delivering water to the **upper supply tank**.
- **Pressure gauge / piezometer** at the nozzle inlet for head measurement.
- **Spear valve / nozzle assembly** for discharge control.
- **Pelton runner** with double-cup buckets.
- **Rope brake dynamometer** wrapped around the brake drum.
- **Two spring balances** ($W$ and $S$) measuring tight-side and slack-side tensions.
- **Tachometer / digital stroboscope** for speed measurement.
- **Measuring tank with V-notch** and stop-watch for discharge measurement.

**Instruments required (list at least 6 — for full marks):**
1. Centrifugal pump with motor
2. Pressure gauge / piezometer
3. Spring balances (×2)
4. Tachometer / stroboscope
5. Stop-watch
6. Measuring tank with V-notch weir
7. Rope brake dynamometer with handle
8. Ruler / scale (for arm length)

**Six Observations to Record (for each trial):**
1. Rotational speed $N$ in **rpm** (from tachometer)
2. Spring balance reading on tight side $W$ in **N** or **kgf**
3. Spring balance reading on slack side $S$ in **N** or **kgf**
4. Effective arm length of brake drum $L$ in **m**
5. Net head $H$ in **m** (from piezometer)
6. Time $t$ in **seconds** to collect a known volume $V$ in the measuring tank (for $Q = V / t$)

---

#### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks:**

Refer to **Section 3.1** of this document for the complete derivation.

Concise reproduction (for the answer script):

**Step 1:** Power $P$ depends on $\rho$, $Q$, $N$, $D$, $g$, $H$ → 6 variables, 3 dimensions → **3 dimensionless $\pi$ groups**.

**Step 2:** Choose repeating variables $\rho$, $N$, $D$.

**Step 3:** Form $\pi_1 = P / (\rho N^3 D^5)$, $\pi_2 = Q / (N D^3)$, $\pi_3 = gH / (N^2 D^2)$.

**Step 4:** Using the relation $D^2 \propto Q / \sqrt{H}$ (from continuity + Torricelli's law $V_1 = \sqrt{2gH}$), and rearranging:

$$N_u = \dfrac{N}{\sqrt{H}} \quad ; \quad Q_u = \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{H}} \quad ; \quad P_u = \dfrac{P}{H^{3/2}}$$

**Step 5:** State the significance: *Two geometrically similar Pelton turbines have identical performance at the same unit speed.* **(7 marks complete)**

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks Every Semester (Kerala KTU Board Pattern)**
>
> 1. **Unit conversion in arm length (L):** Students frequently plug in $L = 50$ (for 50 cm) directly into the BP formula, getting a BP value **100× too large**. **Always convert L to metres first.**
>
> 2. **Spring balance units in kgf vs N:** Many lab manuals give $W$, $S$ in kgf. If the examiner expects SI (Newtons), failure to multiply by 9.81 will cost **1 full mark**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the 60 in the angular velocity term:** Some students write $BP = (W-S) \cdot L \cdot 2\pi N$ (missing the /60) — this gives a BP value 60× too large. **Examiner will deduct 2 marks minimum.**
>
> 4. **Wrong exponent in unit power:** Students confuse $H^{3/2}$ with $H^{5/2}$ (which is for *specific speed*, not *unit power*). The correct exponent for **unit power of a Pelton turbine is 3/2**.
>
> 5. **Confusing "Speed Variation Test" with "Constant Speed Test":** The KTU syllabus clearly distinguishes them. **Speed Variation Test** = constant head, varying speed. **Constant Speed Test** = constant head, varying load (and *theoretically* constant speed, but in practice speed also varies). Read the question carefully.
>
> 6. **Not identifying the Best Efficiency Point (BEP):** Just drawing the curves without explicitly marking the peak and stating the BEP coordinates costs 1–2 marks. **Always circle the peak and label it "BEP".**
>
> 7. **No mention of constant efficiency (Muster) curves:** A 14-mark question often has a 2-mark sub-part asking about "how would you predict performance at a different head?" — failing to mention **Muster curves** loses those marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam)**

- [x] **Speed Variation Test** is also called **Constant Head Test** or **Iso-Head Test**.
- [x] **H is constant**; **spear valve opening is constant**; **N is varied by changing the brake load**.
- [x] For a Pelton turbine, **discharge Q is independent of speed** (a defining feature of impulse machines).
- [x] **Unit Speed:** $N_u = N / \sqrt{H}$ (rpm/√m)
- [x] **Unit Discharge:** $Q_u = Q / \sqrt{H}$ (m^(5/2)/s)
- [x] **Unit Power:** $P_u = P / H^{3/2}$ (W/m^(3/2))
- [x] **Brake Power Formula:** $BP = (W - S) \cdot L \cdot 2\pi N / 60$ — always use **SI units**.
- [x] **Water Power:** $WP = \rho g Q H = 9810 \cdot Q \cdot H$ (in W when Q in m³/s, H in m).
- [x] **Overall Efficiency:** $\eta_o = BP / WP$.
- [x] **Main Characteristic Curves:** Three curves — $N_u$ vs $Q_u$ (vertical line for Pelton), $N_u$ vs $P_u$ (rising), $N_u$ vs $\eta_o$ (bell-shaped).
- [x] **Best Efficiency Point (BEP):** Peak of the $N_u$–$\eta_o$ curve.
- [x] **Constant Efficiency Curves (Muster Curves):** Universal curves valid for any head; constructed by combining main characteristic curves at multiple heads.
- [x] **Significance of unit quantities:** Allow **model-to-prototype scaling** — laboratory model test results predict full-scale turbine behaviour.
- [x] **Optimum bucket velocity ratio:** $K_u = u / \sqrt{2gH} \approx 0.45$–$0.48$ for maximum efficiency.
- [x] **Runaway speed** of a Pelton turbine is approximately **1.8 × rated speed** — a critical design safety parameter.
- [x] **Dimensional analysis** is the formal proof for the unit-quantity definitions; the answer must include the $\pi$ groups.
- [x] **Always record six observations per trial:** $N$, $W$, $S$, $L$, $H$, $t$ (for $Q$).
- [x] **Examiner's pet check:** mark BEP on every characteristic plot; mention Muster curves in the conclusion.
